+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Love in the Limelight — A Scene: Penmorfa, Wales** The bell over the door of Rees & Daughter had two settings: a polite jingle and panic. This was panic. A man shouldered in out of the rain, flattened himself against the Welsh-language fiction, and said, "Please tell me you're closed." "We're open," said Elin Rees. "That's generally why the sign says open." "Then I'm not here." "I can see that. It's the sort of not-being-here that drips." He was dripping — onto the floor her grandfather had laid, onto a display of walking guides. Beyond the steamy glass, three men with cameras went past at a trot, lenses like gun barrels, heading down toward the harbour. "You're him," she said, before she could stop herself. *Duw.* So much for strategy. His whole face lit. "You've seen—" "I said you're him. I didn't say which him." "Oh." The light dimmed, then rallied. "Theo Marlowe. And you are—" "Busy." "Lovely shop, Busy. Is it a chapel? It feels like a chapel." "It was. We knocked the sin out of it. Mostly." She should put him out. The photographers would be back. She flipped the sign to CLOSED instead and went to put the kettle on, furious at her own hands, which had gone trembly as though she were sixteen. He was Theo Marlowe. She had seen that face on a billboard in Cardiff the size of a hay barn. It was smaller in person, and wetter, and it had read three poetry spines and one of her dust-jacket blurbs while she wasn't watching, and she didn't know what to do with that at all. "I'm being hounded," he said, accepting the tea towel she threw him like an honour. "There's a thing filming up at the castle, and a woman I had dinner with twice has told the *Sun* something that isn't strictly— it doesn't matter. The point is, I am a blameless man in a damp country." "It's Wales. There's no such thing." "Ha." He toweled his hair and grinned — *the* grin, the one from the posters, deployed like a cavalry charge. "You know, most people have asked for a photo by now." "Most people must be very tiring to carry around." Something crossed his face — quick as cloud off the sea. Then the grin again, wider, which told her more than the cloud had. "You've really never seen anything I've done? Not the Roman one? People like the Roman one." "I'm sure it's very loud." "You could just say. I've won—" He stopped. Recalibrated. "I was nominated." "There's modest." "I *was*," he said, stung. "Twice." Elin poured the tea and did not smile, which cost her something. The truth was she had seen the Roman one twice and told her sister once, and had thought about the battlefield goodbye rather more often than was sensible for a woman of thirty-four with stock to price. "They'll be drinking in the Dragon till the rain stops," she said. "So will you be." "Then I'll buy a book. Recommend me something. Nothing about acting." She looked him over — the expensive coat, the ruin of a haircut, the eyes that kept checking her face like a man checking a mirror — and reached, on instinct, for the R.S. Thomas. "He's Welsh. Austere. Miserable as November. He'd have taken one look at you and prayed for your soul." "Will I like him?" "No. That's why you should have him." He laughed — really laughed, not the poster laugh — and then, because the rain was hammering the old chapel roof and there was nowhere, for the moment, for a famous man to go, he told her something. "Opening night of my first proper job. Touring *Lear*. Mold. I was nineteen and had one line: 'My lord, the horses are ready.' I walked on, there were more people than I'd ever seen in the dark, and I said it to the wrong king." "Were there two kings?" "There weren't any kings. I said it to Gloucester. Gloucester doesn't ride." He turned the poetry book over without reading it. "One man laughed. Just one, not even cruelly. And I've heard him ever since. Every theatre, every premiere — twenty years of people clapping, and I can still find that one laugh in the dark like a loose thread. If a room goes quiet, I have to—" He gestured at himself, the towel, the whole wet performance. "You see. It's not attractive. I know it's not attractive." The shop ticked with rain. Elin thought of the billboard over the A470, forty feet of confidence, and of the small damp man in front of her checking her face to see if the laugh was there. "My father could charm the birds off the hedges," she said. "Charmed a rep into stocking five hundred copies of his own poems. Charmed himself out of a marriage before I was ten. Charm's a pickpocket, Mr Marlowe. It takes something off you and smiles." She pushed the R.S. Thomas across the counter. "So I don't pay in that currency. Nothing personal." He was quiet a moment — genuinely, she thought, the first unperformed quiet since the bell had panicked. "The horses were ready, though," he said at last. "That part was true." "There's something." "Elin." He'd read the till receipt she'd tucked in the book; he said her name carefully, like a line he meant to keep. "Do you ever leave Penmorfa?" "There's a question a man with a helicopter asks." "I'll come back. Thursday." "It's a day of the week. I've no opinion on it." The rain had thinned; the Dragon's door stood open and empty of photographers. He paid with a note far too large, refused the change, and at the door he turned — and the cavalry-charge grin started up and then, interestingly, was stood down. "*Diolch*," he said, murdering it gorgeously. "Go on with you. And Mr Marlowe." "Theo." "The Gloucester in that play. Blinded, wasn't he? Maybe he needed a horse more than the king did. Maybe you were the only one in the building who'd read it properly." He looked at her a long moment, as though she'd handed him back something he'd lost in Mold twenty years ago. Then the bell jingled — politely, this time — and he was gone. Elin stood a while in the chapel quiet. On the counter he'd left the tea towel, folded badly, and one of her bookmarks, on which he'd written, in a hand too grand for the message: *For the towel — T. xx* She put it in the drawer, not the bin. For stock purposes. Obviously.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a Welsh bookstore setting with authentic details like the converted chapel, Welsh language elements, and local geography. The characterizations are well-executed - Theo as the charming but insecure actor seeking validation, and Elin as the pragmatic bookstore owner who resists his charm while being secretly affected by it. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Elin's dry, no-nonsense Welsh sensibility contrasts nicely with Theo's performative charm that occasionally drops to reveal genuine vulnerability. The author handles the romantic tension well through subtext and unspoken attraction rather than overwrought declarations. The prose style is confident and economical, with effective imagery like "lenses like gun barrels" and "charm's a pickpocket." The pacing builds naturally from the dramatic entrance through conversation to a satisfying conclusion that sets up future possibility without forcing resolution. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases like "deployed like a cavalry charge" edge toward purple prose, and some of the Welsh cultural details feel slightly surface-level rather than deeply integrated. The transformation from Theo's initial charm offensive to genuine vulnerability, while well-handled, happens quite quickly within the scene's timeframe. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful but not overly optimistic, with both characters maintaining their essential natures while showing subtle shifts. The bookmark detail provides a nice concrete symbol of connection without being heavy-handed. Overall, this is accomplished writing that fulfills the prompt requirements while creating engaging characters and authentic romantic tension through skilled dialogue and subtext. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door opened on warmth, and that was the first wrong thing. Aya had expected cellar breath — cold stone, the dry whisper of the wards. Instead lamplight spilled across the threshold, sweet with spermaceti smoke, and beneath it tobacco, and beneath that, faint as gossip, the sea. The underhold was no vault. It was a long, low hall of blackened oak and scrimshaw, lit by a dozen whale-oil lamps, and at its far end — where the relic should have stood alone under spell and key — someone had set a counting table like a throne. Behind it sat Marena. The Drowned Woman of Morska. Mother-of-knives. Decidedly *un*-incarcerated, grey braid over one shoulder of her oilskin coat, a ledger open before her. They said she'd been keelhauled under a whaler in her youth and come up the far side smiling — that the sea had given her back. Around her stood guards. Aya's heart did arithmetic: eight. No, nine. Crossbows. The brass mouth of a harpoon-pistol. One vast, silent man honing a flensing knife. Out beyond the guild walls, down in the harbor, a foghorn sounded — one long note, mournful as something grieving in the murk. "Wren," Marena said, almost gently. "There she is. Come in, come in. You've caught me at my accounts." "You're supposed to be in irons," Katra said, flat as a slap. "Am I?" Marena turned a page. "The constabulary and I keep an understanding. They wanted a morning's headlines; I wanted a quiet season. The cove they carried out of my rooms answers to my name in bad light and owes me his teeth besides." She looked up, and her eyes were the color of harbor water. "Then I let it be whispered about that Morska stood headless, and I sat in the dark, like a spider. It's a marvel, what crawls to the web when the spider plays dead." *A snare. All of it.* Aya reached inward for the cold place, the door behind the door — and found it smothered. Her own knotwork, thrice-turned, tied into that lintel three months past for a purse of silver, lay over the threshold like wet wool over a fire. Below the world, the whispering congregation pressed flat against it, and could not rise. *She had taught them how to hold the knife. The edge was at her own throat.* Katra's hand drifted toward her gutting knife. A crossbow string drew taut. "Ah-ah," Marena said. "Hand away, sweetheart, or you'll be haddock before you hit the floor." "We're caught, girls." Simeon's voice was tired, old. He set his jimmy down with terrible gentleness and knelt. "Put it away, Kat." They were disarmed in silence, made to kneel on the cold stone. Aya's mask suddenly itched like a lie. Marena beckoned. "Let's see the Wren's true feathers." Her hooked fingers took the mask at its edge and peeled it away like skin from fruit. The foghorn sounded a second time. Marena studied her, tilting her chin to the lamplight. "Soft hands. Straight teeth. Quality." Then stillness. A long, drowned stillness. "God below. I *know* this face." Her grip tightened. "Twenty years gone I stood in the rain at Gallows Steps and watched a woman with your chin refuse the hood. Spat in the bishop's eye before they sprang the trap. Vask. You're the Vask girl. Lady Aya Vask, as I live and breathe." The name landed in Aya like a dropped bell. She saw it all in merciless sequence: the *Gazette* by noon, set in great fat type — LORD'S DAUGHTER TAKEN MASKED IN THIEVES' DEN. Her father at breakfast, the paper slipping from his hands. Twenty years the family had spent drowning that name in consecrated silence, and here was the daughter to dredge it up — thief, witch, unmasked. They hanged witches still. She had warded this very room. *I am bargaining with the rope already round my neck*, she thought, and underneath the thought, faint and patient, something below the stones knew her name. "Gentry." Katra spat it like a fishbone. "You eat gutter bread with us by night and go home to silk. All this time, you were slumming on our lives." "Stow it, Kat—" "She *used* us, Sim—" Aya said nothing. It was true, all of it, and there was no soap for it. So she lifted her chin — her mother's chin, curse it — and did the coldest arithmetic of her life. "Then you know what I'm worth," she said, and some distant part of her was proud that her voice came out level. "Dead, I'm a scandal and a magistrate's questions. Alive, I'm the Vask purse. My father would empty his coffers to keep my name out of the papers." Marena leaned back. "Your father would pay to have you sunk in the harbor with your ankles wired to a try-pot. You're the family shame, girl, come home to roost." "A trial," Aya pressed. "A hanging. Think of the broadsheets. He sits on the Admiralty Board — he has elections to buy and daughters to marry off. Every day I breathe *quietly* is worth more than your treasury." Marena laughed then, genuinely, delighted, and addressed her guards. "Hear her? The little witch thinks she's an annuity." Her gaze came back, sharp as a flensing blade. "And so you are. Why sell the cow when she'll keep for years? A lord's daughter on my hook and a witch in my pocket, all in the one skin." She tapped the table. "Did you think I'd let a witch knot my locks and never ask her name? I've been *keeping* you, Wren. Like a coin for a rainy day." "And us?" Katra said. "You? You're worth your wages and nothing more. You'll live because corpses are paperwork." Marena lifted the relic then from its cloth beside the ledger — the Scythan crown, the gutter's seat of power. Up close it was an ugly little thing: a circlet of black whalebone set with amber, ancient as sin. In the largest stone hung a small dark shape, and it was not an insect. She turned it idly in her hands, like a washerwoman wringing cloth. "You came for this," she said. "Try it on someday, if you're very good." She rose. "Take them below. The lord's daughter gets her own cell — she's porcelain now." As the guards hauled Aya past the table, the amber caught the lamplight, and she would have sworn — God below help her — that the dark thing in it turned to watch her go. Out beyond the walls the foghorn sounded a third time: three notes across the black water, like a bell counting the drowned. The sea kept its accounts. And below the world, pressed flat beneath her own knotwork, the congregation that knew her name settled in, patient as the tide, to wait.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid maritime-industrial aesthetic through specific details like spermaceti smoke, whale-oil lamps, scrimshaw, and nautical terminology. The Victorian gothic tone is well-maintained throughout, with appropriately gritty dialogue that feels authentic to the world. The characterization is particularly strong. Aya emerges as a complex protagonist - privileged yet rebellious, calculating yet vulnerable. Her internal conflict between her aristocratic background and criminal activities creates genuine tension. Marena is effectively portrayed as a cunning antagonist who has orchestrated events from the beginning, subverting expectations about the power vacuum. The supporting characters Katra and Simeon feel authentic, with Katra's betrayed anger at discovering Aya's true identity being especially well-rendered. The dialogue carries distinct voices for each character. Marena's speech has the right mix of folksy menace and calculated intelligence, while Aya's attempts at negotiation reveal both her desperation and her aristocratic bearing. The exchanges feel natural and advance both plot and character development effectively. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through the initial wrongness of the warm room, the reveal of Marena's presence, and culminating in Aya's unmasking and failed negotiation. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested, instead focusing on psychological tension and power dynamics. The prose demonstrates sophistication without becoming overwrought. Phrases like "her heart did arithmetic" and "the coldest arithmetic of her life" create effective metaphorical consistency. The recurring foghorn serves as atmospheric punctuation that reinforces the maritime setting. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The magical system's mechanics (the "knotwork" and "congregation") could be clearer, though this may be intentional mystique. Some descriptions, while evocative, occasionally border on purple prose, particularly in the opening paragraphs. The piece adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified tone and avoiding prohibited elements like fight scenes. The ending appropriately sets up future complications without resolving the immediate tension. + Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gunpowder Rendezvous The Yellow Flag smells of spilled liquor and old gunpowder, and the neon jukebox in the corner has been playing the same scratchy rock track for three straight hours. Three warped chords. A singer who sounds like gravel in a cement mixer. Spike Spiegel has decided he hates it. He orders another whiskey anyway. He's counting crumpled bills that refuse to add up to dinner while his mind circles back to the bounty: Donnie Kwan, syndicate bagman turned snitch, dead or alive, worth enough to eat for a month. Spike has been in Roanapur four days. His stomach reminds him of this constantly. The woman three stools down drinks straight from the bottle. Cutoff shorts, black crop top, a green dragon coiling from wrist to shoulder along her right arm, two pistols riding her thighs like they were born there. The bartender doesn't ask what she wants. Everybody in Roanapur knows Two Hands. She catches Spike looking and smiles—the kind of smile sharks give each other. "You're either a cop or a hunter," she says, sliding onto the stool beside him. "And you're too skinny and too broke to be a cop." "Spike." He lights a cigarette. "And you drink like somebody's chasing you." "Revy." She plucks the cigarette from his fingers, lights her own off the tip, and hands it back without asking. Their knuckles brush. Neither mentions it. It takes three more drinks and a shared pack to circle the truth out of each other. Kwan's name surfaces like a body floating up. "Back off, cowboy," Revy says pleasantly. "Lagoon Company's got first claim." "Bounties don't work like that." "They do in this town." She studies him over the bottle—the lazy sprawl, the hands that stay perfectly still, the mismatched eyes. "What happened to your face?" "Long story. Short version: I already died once." "Huh." She almost respects that. "Me? I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." "That's the most honest thing anybody's said all week." The door opens. Donnie Kwan walks in with four bodyguards and a tan suit that costs more than the bar. Spike and Revy look at each other. "That's him." "That's my paycheck." One of the guards reaches inside his jacket, and the night detonates. Spike moves first—no wasted motion, the stool going over, the table flipping into a guard's chest, his Jericho clearing leather in the same breath. Two shots, two kneecaps. Economy. A mook grabs for him and meets Jeet Kune Do: an intercepted wrist, a straight blast, a side kick that folds the man over the bar rail. Revy just laughs. Both Cutlasses are out and she's firing in two directions at once, perched on the bar like it's a throne, brass raining into the ice. "Dance, motherfuckers!" A stray round smashes the jukebox. It sparks, spits, and keeps playing—the same track, now skipping, the riff lurching back on itself like a broken heartbeat. Thirty seconds. Four guards down. And Donnie Kwan is gone, out the back door, into the alleys where bounties go to die. "Your fault," they say in unison. Roanapur doesn't send cops; it sends silence. They stand in the wreckage breathing cordite, and the look between them has stopped being about money. "Come on," she says, jerking her chin at the door. "We're settling this." Her motel room is one lamp, one bed, a ceiling fan shoving hot air in circles. The door has barely clicked shut when her Cutlass is under his chin and his Jericho is pressed to the hollow below her ribs. Neither of them remembers deciding to draw. "Say it was my fault again," she breathes. "It was your fault." A foot of humid air between their mouths. The dragon on her arm flexes. His thumb rests on the hammer; her finger sits inside the trigger guard; and both of them are acutely aware this is the closest either has let anyone get in a long, long time. Her eyes flick to his mouth. His flick to hers. The fan keeps turning. Somewhere in the wall a pipe ticks like a countdown. "So," Revy says, voice gone low and dangerous. "Are we gonna shoot each other, or—" She doesn't finish. He doesn't let her. One second. Two. The hammer eases down under his thumb. "Whatever happens, happens," Spike says, and holsters the Jericho, and steps back out of her gravity. Revy holds the gun on empty air a moment longer. Then she laughs—sharp, real, almost wounded—and lets him go. The door clicks shut. She's alone with the fan, the cordite, and the unfinished sentence. Spike walks into the Roanapur night with empty pockets and an empty stomach, and the whole way to the docks the jukebox's broken riff loops in his skull. Three warped chords. Over and over. He finds he doesn't hate it anymore.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character authenticity. The author successfully captures both Spike's fatalistic cool and Revy's volatile aggression while maintaining their distinct voices throughout. The setting of the Yellow Flag bar in Roanapur is vividly rendered with sensory details that establish atmosphere without overwhelming the narrative. The story structure is well-paced, moving efficiently from the initial meeting through the bar fight to the motel room confrontation. The action sequences are particularly well-written, with clear choreography that showcases each character's fighting style - Spike's economical Jeet Kune Do versus Revy's dual-wielding chaos. The dialogue feels natural and character-appropriate, avoiding exposition dumps while revealing personality. The central tension between attraction and mistrust is handled with restraint. Rather than forcing a resolution, the story allows the sexual tension to remain unresolved, which feels true to both characters' psychological profiles. The motel room standoff effectively uses the drawn guns as both literal threat and metaphor for their inability to be vulnerable. The prose style is lean and evocative, with strong imagery like "the look between them has stopped being about money" and "stepped back out of her gravity." The recurring motif of the broken jukebox provides effective bookending. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining atmospheric richness. Some minor weaknesses include a few slightly overwrought moments ("the night detonates") and the ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat predictable given the characters' established patterns. The piece also doesn't quite reach the full emotional complexity that the setup promises, though this restraint may be intentional given the source material's tone. The story succeeds in creating a believable crossover that honors both source materials while telling a complete, engaging narrative within the word limit. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Útiseta I was fifteen the autumn my father's transfer moved us north, to a village in Västerbotten where the pines outnumbered the people, and I have never once told anyone the whole of what happened on the mound. Astrid would say that means I learned something after all. She lived two houses down, with her mother and her mormor, a woman who fed birds from her palm and never once looked me in the eye. The games started small. Salt on the windowsill to dream of the boy who liked you. Walking backwards around the kyrka three times to learn a secret. We used them for idiotic things — whether Johan had cheated on his maths exam, who'd put the note in Malin's locker. They worked often enough that I stopped asking how. Útiseta, Astrid told me, was different. Sitting out. Her grandmother had done it, and her grandmother before her, back and back. You sat the night through on the old grave-mound and the ones below gave you answers — if you did it properly, if you didn't flinch. "It's not a game," she said, which was, I understood much later, the only entirely true thing she said that month. She prepped me in her room while her grandmother rattled dishes downstairs. Eat nothing after sunset. Nothing iron on your body — rings off, necklace off, jacket with buttons, not a zip. Wool blanket, not fleece. "It has to come from something that was alive." Bread in our pockets, one piece each. Payment, she said, and did not elaborate. "Properly you go alone," she admitted, tugging her boots on. "But it's your first time. We'll sit back to back." She said it the way you'd explain a shortcut in a video game, and that should have frightened me more than it did — how casually she revised the rules of something older than the church. "Whatever you hear, don't answer. Not even if it's my voice. Whatever you see, you don't run until it's finished. And don't say our names once we're through the gate." "Whose gate?" She just looked at me. We snuck out past eleven. The dark had only just returned after the bright summer nights, and it felt new and thin, like ice that hadn't hardened. We walked the gravel road out of the village, past the ICA, past the dark vicarage, the smell of bog myrtle coming up off the marsh. The moon was a sliver. Below us the church stood unlit, its windows black. The mound crowned the ridge east of the village, bare at its peak though pines ringed it, as if nothing had ever dared grow on top. Her mormor said the church had been built in sight of the mound, not the other way around. So it would know it was being watched. When we climbed, the earth gave back a soft sound under our boots. Hollow. A held breath. We wrapped ourselves in the blankets and sat down at the top, back to back, each facing her own half of the dark. Astrid pressed her piece of bread under her knee. The village lay below, one streetlamp at the crossroads, everything else asleep. Time went strange the way it does when you're forbidden to check it. My back went cold where it touched hers. The marsh breathed fog up the slope in slow ribbons. Then, from below: the church bell. One stroke. Midnight. The sound rolled up the ridge and through us and was gone. "There's no service," I whispered. I couldn't help it. "The church is empty." Her hand found mine behind her and crushed it. *It does that,* she mouthed. After the bell, the cold deepened in a way that had nothing to do with temperature. And then I heard it — a sound like a radio dial sliding between stations, voices that never resolved into words, coming from everywhere below us. From the mound itself, maybe. I told myself: wind in the pines. Sheep. Blood in my ears. I have spent thirty years telling myself variations of the same list. Astrid's back went rigid against mine. "Ask, then," she said. It was her voice the way a coat is a person hanging on a hook. Flat. Patient. Older than the house, older than the church. I hadn't asked anything. My question — I'd had a stupid one ready, about whether I'd ever stop being the new girl — had died somewhere around the bell. "It says you kept the letter," the voice said. "The one from Stockholm. The one you were told to post. It says she waited for it until spring." I had never told anyone about the letter. Not Astrid. Not anyone. "It wants to know," Astrid's voice went on, mild as milk, "what you'll give for the rest." I stood up. That was the thing we were never to do, and I did it, and the fog was standing in a ring at the mound's foot in shapes the way fog makes shapes — except fog doesn't hold still when the wind moves, and these held still. Then everything stopped at once, like a set switched off. Astrid blinked up at me, and she was just my friend again, fifteen and frightened — the first time I had ever seen her frightened. "Did I say anything?" she whispered. "Linnea. What did I say?" "Nothing," I lied. "You didn't say anything." She snatched up the blankets but pressed both pieces of bread flat into the turf with her thumb, hard, like sealing something. "We owe it," she said. "Don't look back at it." I looked back at it. The bread was gone, or the fog had taken the shape of the mound, or I had simply never learned to look properly at things. We half-ran down the ridge and didn't speak until the streetlamp. At her grandmother's gate we both stopped. The old woman stood at her window with a candle, fully awake at three in the morning, watching us come down off the ridge. She didn't wave. She looked at Astrid for a long moment, and then she drew the curtain, and I understood that some people in that village had never thought any of it was a game, and that Astrid had known this, and had brought me up there anyway. We never played midnight games after that. Astrid never told me what question she'd climbed the mound to ask, and I never told her about the letter. Most of me believes it was suggestion — the cold, the dark, a village that brews its own weather out of whispers. But that winter, out of some stubborn need, I asked the verger about the bell. It hadn't rung at midnight in years, he said. The mechanism was seized. Rusted solid since before I was born. *It does that,* Astrid had said, unsurprised. That is the part I cannot explain away.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in executing folk horror within the specified parameters. The author successfully creates an atmospheric narrative set in 1990s rural Sweden, following Linnea's recollection of a supernatural encounter on an ancient burial mound. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece maintains first-person past tense as recollection, hits the approximate word count, establishes the Stockholm-to-village backstory, and crucially maintains ambiguity about whether events are supernatural or psychological. The Útiseta ritual is well-researched and authentically presented. Character work is nuanced, particularly in depicting teenage dynamics. Astrid emerges as genuinely unsettling - her casual revision of ancient rules and matter-of-fact approach to dangerous practices feels authentically adolescent yet ominous. Linnea's voice as narrator is consistent and believable, capturing both her teenage perspective and adult reflection without heavy-handedness. The prose demonstrates considerable elegance and restraint. Phrases like "the dark had only just returned after the bright summer nights, and it felt new and thin, like ice that hadn't hardened" show sophisticated imagery without overindulgence. The writing maintains atmospheric tension through careful pacing and selective detail. The supernatural elements are handled with particular skill. The church bell that "hadn't rung at midnight in years" provides the story's most chilling moment precisely because it's presented matter-of-factly. The revelation about the letter Linnea never posted creates genuine unease without explanation. Structurally, the piece builds tension effectively from preparation through ritual to aftermath. The grandmother's presence at the window provides an excellent closing image that reinforces the story's themes about generational knowledge and complicity. Minor weaknesses include some exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and occasional moments where the adult narrator's voice intrudes slightly on the teenage experience. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The ending avoids both false positivity and overwrought darkness, instead settling into the unsettling ambiguity that defines quality folk horror. The piece earns its darkness through careful buildup rather than cheap scares. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 89.8
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Therapeutic Boundaries** The man on the wall is flayed open and doesn't seem to mind. Life-size, every muscle labeled in Latin—rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, the long red strap of the sartorius—he looms over the treatment table like an autopsy scheduled in advance. Twelve Tuesdays beneath him. I know his anatomy better than my own now. His is intact. Mara washes her hands at the sink and I lie down in my shorts, left leg bared, and we begin the ritual where neither of us mentions that her spikes used to sit in the cubby next to mine. I ran the four-hundred hurdles; she ran the flat four. She quit at twenty-six and built a whole second life out of her hands. I stayed until hurdle eight, lead leg planted, knee folded the wrong way—ACL in two pieces, meniscus like a bad letter. The surgeon calls twelve weeks of this mandatory. The insurance company cosigns. So: Mara's table. Mara's hands. She palpates first, always, two fingers sweeping the joint line on either side of my kneecap, pressing for fluid. "Effusion's down." Her hands are warm from the tap. Mine are fists at my hips, and we both know why. She bends my knee, heel cradled in her palm, and lays the goniometer cold against my leg—hinge on the bony knob at the side of the knee, one arm along my thigh, the other down my shin to the ankle bone. "One-nineteen." Three degrees better than last week, said like it should please me. I lived in hundredths of a second; now I'm measured in degrees and told to be grateful. I hate recovery. Not the pain—the pain is the only part that still feels like training. I hate what it asks for, the one thing sprinting never did: surrender. Her thumbs and forefingers grip the borders of my kneecap and glide it—down toward my foot, up toward my hip, side to side, small merciless shoves. A healthy patella floats. Mine grinds like a stone in a wet sock, a deep wrongness that makes my whole leg vote to leave the table. Goosebumps climb my calf ahead of her hands. "Don't guard." "I'm not guarding." "Your hamstring disagrees." Her palm rests flat on the back of my thigh, where the muscle has bunched itself into a fist. She waits. It lets go. She wins. Then the scar—three inches of shiny rope below the kneecap where they harvested the graft from my own tendon. She braces both thumbs beside it and works across the grain of the fibers, hard little rakes, and this is the hot-wire pain, climbing the front of the knee and ringing in my teeth. My heel digs into the table. "Twenty more seconds," she says, voice gone low, almost private, which is worse than the pain. When she finishes, her thumbs stay on the scar one second longer than the technique requires. One second. We both catalog it. We both pretend not to. She props my heel on a rolled towel and lays her palm flat on my thigh just above the kneecap—never on it, she knows better—and leans her weight down through a straight arm. The stretch takes hold deep behind the knee, sick and pulling, and my leg tries to cramp upward off the towel on its own. "Let it fall," she murmurs. "Give me the back of the knee." Her face is eighteen inches from mine. Eucalyptus, and under it her shampoo. I study the pale vein along her forearm. She is counting my exhales like she's spotting a lift, and I count ceiling tiles so I don't watch her do it. Then she cups my heel and slides it up the table toward my hip, knee folding, and this is the part I'd skip if she weren't mandatory. At one-nineteen the joint simply ends—a wall with nerves in it. She leans in, shoulder over my shin, one hand cradling the heel, the other wrapped over the top of my bent knee, and takes me two degrees past the wall. The pain goes from pressure to light. My fingers find the table rails. I make a sound I didn't authorize. My right hip hikes off the table to cheat the angle and her hand is instantly there, flat on the bone, pressing me back down. "Stop cheating. Breathe out—give it to me on the exhale." *Give it to me.* I hear it the way I'd have heard it at twenty-two, and then I hear her hear it—the smallest pause in her hands—and we both bury it under protocol. Two degrees on the exhale. Three. Her hair brushes my forearm. Neither of us moves it. For the hamstring she takes my heel onto her shoulder, near hand flat on my thigh to keep the knee locked straight, the other planted on the front of my right hip bone so my pelvis can't roll away from the work. Then she folds forward from her hips, a centimeter at a time, and the back of my leg lights up from the sit-bone to behind the knee—a bright cable pulled from both ends. My foot tries to point itself, anything to slacken the line. "Keep it flexed," she says. "That's your sciatic nerve trying to get out of work." Her collarbone rests against my shin. I can feel her breathing. Every exhale of hers buys a millimeter of me. Last, the quad set: leg straight, her fingers resting on the teardrop of muscle above the inner knee—vastus medialis, says the man on the wall, and I hate that I hear it in his voice. "Push the back of your knee into the table. Hold ten." The muscle fires, flickers, dies at six. This thing that once launched a hundred ninety pounds over ten hurdles can't clench for ten seconds. Her thumb traces its wasted edge, once, and the muscle jumps under her touch all by itself. Reflex, we both call it. Her ears have gone red. "Six," she says—to the muscle, to me, to the record. "Thursday we ask for seven." She lays the ice across my knee and cold blooms up through the ache while she writes her small numbers in the chart. One-nineteen. Six seconds. A whole career in her handwriting. "Same time Thursday," she says, not quite asking, and I say yes before I can construct the no. At the door I look back at the flayed man over the table. For twelve weeks he's been an autopsy. Today, ice-melt cooling on my knee, he looks for the first time like what he actually is. Not a eulogy. Directions.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in nearly every aspect. The narrative successfully fulfills the complex prompt requirements while maintaining literary sophistication throughout. The first-person perspective of the injured athlete is rendered with authentic voice and compelling interiority, capturing both physical and psychological pain with remarkable precision. The anatomical accuracy is outstanding - every therapeutic technique is described with medical precision, from patellar mobilization to goniometer measurements to specific muscle groups. The physical therapy sequences feel authentic and educational while serving the narrative's emotional arc. The spatial coherence is flawless; readers can visualize exactly what's happening to the protagonist's body at each moment. The characterization is nuanced and psychologically complex. The protagonist's resentment toward recovery itself (rather than just the injury) creates compelling internal conflict. Mara emerges as more than just an object of attraction - she's a fully realized person with her own professional competence and emotional responses. The shared athletic history adds layers of meaning to their interactions. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with particularly strong metaphorical language ("knee folded the wrong way," "meniscus like a bad letter," "pain goes from pressure to light"). The anatomical poster serving as both literal presence and metaphorical frame is brilliantly executed. The writing maintains consistent tone throughout - clinical yet intimate, bitter yet vulnerable. The sexual tension is handled with remarkable sophistication, built through precise physical details and micro-moments of awareness rather than explicit content. The "give it to me" moment and its immediate recognition by both characters exemplifies this restraint and psychological realism. The ending transformation from seeing the anatomical poster as "autopsy" to "directions" is subtle and earned through the narrative's progression. It suggests possibility without false optimism, maintaining the story's emotional honesty while providing meaningful closure. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the medical terminology might overwhelm general readers, and some metaphors that border on precious ("muscle bunched itself into a fist"). However, these are minimal concerns in an otherwise exceptional piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 85.2
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Twelve: The King's Coat The knocking came before the light did. "Mr. Merrick! Captain's compliments, sir, and will you take the gig to Second Bar to-day — the leadsman's down with the shaking fever and the tide serves at nine." I knew the voice. Young Perry, who still ironed his collars. "Perry." "Sir?" "Tell the captain the flux has me again." A silence with a shape to it. A midshipman's disbelief sounds like a boat hook set down too carefully. "Again, sir?" "Again, Mr. Perry." "Very good, sir." A pause on the wet stone. "And — the two dollars you were so kind as to borrow of me?" "The first of the month. On my honour." His boots went away down Hog Lane, and I sat on the bed's edge with my eyes streaming like a mourner at a stranger's funeral. My hand found the sparrow in my coat as it always did — a pinch of green no bigger than a pistol ball, gone greasy as butter from three years of my thumb. I'd bought it on the Praya Grande for my sister's girl in Fareham. That was four ports ago. I worked it until the day's first great yawn came up and cracked my jaw like a mast going. ∗ ∗ ∗ Ah-Sam waited under the eaves of the Imperial factory, out of the wind. The first dry wind of autumn had come off the hills in the night; every godown door on the quay stood open, and the creek stank of low water and fish-glue. "You wear uniform," he said, by way of good morning. "Gold lace, all proper. Four chest — camphor outside, Patna inside. Hoppo man smell camphor, he no likee look close. Coolie carry. You walkee behind, talkee loud English. Mandarin man see King's officer, he look other side." There had been a time I'd have called the watch for the suggestion. "How much?" "Two dollar. One mace Number One, cook already." "Three, and the mace weighed where I can see the dotchin." "Two and half. Dotchin honest." He smiled with gold. "Chop-chop, Mr. Merrick. Sick man no can bargain all day." ∗ ∗ ∗ The coolies' poles creaked the whole length of Old China Street, and I walked behind with my single epaulette catching the light, discoursing to no one on the likelihood of rain — the most fluent nonsense I have ever spoken. The camphor lied beautifully. So did the coat. At the creek stairs a Hoppo's runner fanned himself with a sheet of the new edict against the trade and watched us pass with the open interest of a man already paid. In the godown's shadow Ah-Sam counted Mexican dollars into my palm. The brass arm of the scale trembled. So did my hand, and I put it in my pocket where the sparrow was warm. ∗ ∗ ∗ Lo kept shop above a paper-umbrella maker's, where the air was tung oil and old smoke and the cricket in his cage sang like a tiny cash-box. He set the ball on the dotchin and sighed like a man doing me an injury. "Patna dear this moon. Mandarin makee plenty bobbery. Three dollar." "It was two in August." "August you pay two. Still owe one." He let that sit. Then, kindly: "Dross half a dollar. Same smoke. Little more cook, is all." "I don't smoke scrapings." "No," he agreed, and wrapped a lump of it for me anyway — for morning. His eye dropped to my pocket, where my thumb had gone back to its work without consulting me. "What that you rub-rub? Small bird? Jade Number One. Ten dollar I give. One moon, you buy back twelve." "Not that." "All man say that," Lo said, tying the parcel. "Second moon, he sell." ∗ ∗ ∗ I crossed to the Honam side as the lamps came on — a cormorant fisherman beating the water with his pole, a two-string fiddle scraping somewhere among the flower boats. The divan was a rice-lorcha gone to anchor forever, her hold blue with little flames, fantan clicking behind a partition. I cooked the pill until it blistered and sighed, and drew, and the ache let go of me the way the tide lets go of a mudbank: by degrees, denying it had ever held. "Good God. Merrick, isn't it?" The voice came off the next couch. "You had the cutter at Lintin in 'thirty-one — the fellow who retook the *Sarah* off Pedro Branco. I dosed you for the ague at Malacca roads." "You have the advantage of me." "Braithe. Surgeon, Honourable Company's service." The lamp popped between us. "Late of." "Late of?" "Late of everything." He rolled toward me on the wooden headrest. "Take passage on anything with a keel, Merrick. Fourteen days at sea sweats it out of a man. I have seen it done." "And how many times have you done it yourself, Doctor?" He smiled at the rafters. "The sea gets farther off every year. That is the whole of my learning, and I give it you gratis." ∗ ∗ ∗ Ah-Fat had the lamp lit and my boots drying. "Cap'n come two time," he said. "First time he loud. Second time he quiet." He considered. "Quiet more worser. He leave paper." The paper was not long. It used the word *dereliction*, and gave me until the morning tide. "You tell him you go?" I set the dross where my hand could find it at first light and lay down in my coat, the sparrow flat in my palm — the one thing in Canton that was not for sale. *All men say that*, Lo had said. "Aye," I said. "Tell him I'm mending." Out on the river the watchman's clappers went past and came back again, a pulse the city couldn't keep.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric writing, successfully creating an immersive portrait of a British naval officer's descent into opium addiction in 1830s Canton. The author shows excellent command of period-appropriate language and naval terminology, creating authentic dialogue that captures both the formal speech patterns of British officers and the pidgin English of Chinese merchants. The characterization of Lieutenant Merrick is particularly well-executed. Rather than presenting a one-dimensional addict, the author reveals his degradation through subtle details: the worn sparrow jade meant for his niece, his practiced lies to subordinates, and his conflicted relationship with his uniform as both shame and tool. The supporting characters, though briefly sketched, feel authentic and serve specific narrative purposes. The prose style is notably restrained and period-appropriate, avoiding modern sensibilities while maintaining accessibility. The author employs effective sensory details - the creaking coolie poles, the smell of camphor and fish-glue, the sound of fantan clicking - that ground the reader in the setting without overwhelming the narrative. The dialogue serves multiple functions effectively: advancing plot, revealing character, and establishing the complex social dynamics of colonial Canton. Conversations with Ah-Sam, Lo, and Dr. Braithe each illuminate different aspects of Merrick's situation and the broader world he inhabits. The structure follows Merrick's daily routine in a way that feels both episodic and cohesive, building toward the revelation of his captain's ultimatum. The ending strikes an appropriately dark note without melodrama, suggesting both finality and the cyclical nature of addiction. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the period language feels slightly forced, and the piece could benefit from slightly more interiority to balance the dialogue-heavy approach. However, these are minor concerns in an otherwise accomplished piece of historical fiction. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Humour: Sisyphus Gives Advice — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Sisyphus Gives Advice Write a comedic scene in the style of Terry Pratchett featuring Sisyphus, who has started an advice column while continuing his eternal boulder-pushing. Rather than being bitter about his punishment, Sisyphus has developed a comfortably zen emotional intelligence and wisdom from his centuries of repetitive labor. Not in the cliche ways you might expect -- he's actually very grounded, warm & connected with the everyday human plight. He dictates responses to "Crow," his feathered familiar who serves as both scribe and mail carrier. The piece should include Sisyphus thoughtfully answering several advice-seekers' letters about everyday problems (relationship troubles, work-life balance, dealing with difficult people, life purpose doubts) while periodically returning to his boulder-pushing duties. His advice should be surprisingly insightful and practical. The humor should emerge naturally from the messages he receives, from mundane to risque. Sisyphus should genuinely enjoy helping others, finding meaning in this new pursuit while maintaining his boulder routine. The humour should be *understated and not forced*. Contrary to expectation, almost none of his advice involves boulder rolling metaphors (though he does sneak *one* subtle in there), nor a treatise on stoicism. We are also going against the obvious fish-out-of-water trope; in fact, he's surprisingly a natural at this. In all of this , it's important to *show, don't tell*. The format should be largely epistolary, with the occasional interjection for scene setting. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Sisyphus Gives Advice In the Underworld, the punishments ran like clockwork, mostly because there was nothing else to do but wind them. Tantalus thirsted. The Danaides hauled water in sieves, and complained about it, because even eternity is no cure for temperament. And on the third hill past the asphodel meadows, Sisyphus pushed his boulder, as he had for a very long time, and — this was the part the punishment's designers hadn't budgeted for — whistled. Beside him, balanced on the boulder's shoulder, rode Crow, who had left the omen business (all doom, no benefits) and now ran the correspondence department. It had started with one letter, forwarded by a widow who'd heard the happiest man below the world gave straight answers. Now the mail came by the sack, addressed: *The Man on the Hill, Third Hill Past the Asphodel, The Underworld (Down). Mind the Dog.* --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *My wife says I never listen to her. At least, I think that's what she said.* *— Attentive of Athens* Dear Attentive, Good news: you heard enough to worry. That's a door. Listening isn't a talent; it's a chore, like sweeping. Do it daily or the mess compounds. Tonight, put the cup down. Turn your whole body toward her. Let her finish — *especially* when you're certain what she'll say next. Certainty is where listening goes to die. How long have you been married? She has earned the right to surprise you. Yours from the hill, S. *(He dictated this on the ascent, using hand gestures. At me. I am a bird. — C.)* --- The boulder crested, considered its options, and rolled down. They took the descent together, Crow surfing with the weary dignity of a creature who refused, on principle, to enjoy it. --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *I am a clerk. The ledgers never end. I finish one and two more arrive. I eat at my desk. I haven't seen my sister in a year. But if I stop, someone else gets promoted.* *— Drowning in Delphi* Dear Drowning, The ledgers will never end. That is not a flaw in you; it is the nature of ledgers. So the work cannot be the thing that finishes. You must be. Pick the hour you set down the pen, and keep it like a promise to someone you love — because it is one. If you collapse, your replacement will be found by Thursday. Your sister cannot be replaced at all. Go to dinner. The ledger will wait. Ledgers are very patient. S. *(For the record, he keeps no such hour himself. I have raised this. He says it's "different." It is not different. — C.)* --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *My neighbor's goat eats my garden. I have spoken to him nine times. He says, "Goats will be goats." I want to poison the goat. (I will not poison the goat.) What do I do?* *— Furious of Pharsalus* Dear Furious, You've already put the poison in parentheses, which is where it belongs. Your neighbor has told you who he is, nine times. Believe him. You cannot roll another person uphill, however much they might benefit from the view. Take it from one who knows: the only weight you can truly move is your own. So move your own. Build the fence. A fence is a sentence you say to the world that it cannot argue back at. Then plant something goats hate along it, purely for your own amusement. You have earned some amusement. S. *(He says "take it from one who knows" with a perfectly straight face. I have to write it with a straight wing. — C.)* --- Halfway up the next climb, Sisyphus stopped — the boulder, after several thousand years, had learned to wait — and said, "Strike 'amusement.' Too pleased with itself. Write: 'You have earned a small victory. Take it.'" Crow scratched it out. Somewhere above, Zeus remained unaware that his sentence now had a readership, and what Zeus doesn't know gets filed under *ongoing*. --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *Twenty years married, and my husband has recently made a suggestion involving the good olive oil. I am not scandalized. I am only surprised it took him twenty years. Is it odd to feel shy with a man who has watched me give birth twice?* *— Blushing in Boeotia* Dear Blushing, Not odd. Shyness is just intimacy wearing a new dress; you'll recognize each other shortly. After twenty years, surprise is a gift most marriages run out of. Accept it graciously, laugh if it goes wrong — it may, and laughter is the better oil — and say only what you truly want. Then say that. S. *(I have picked clean three battlefields. I still had to put this letter down twice and walk around. — C.)* --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *I am forty. I have led no armies, raised no temples, written no poems. What if this is all I am?* *— Ordinary of Argos* Dear Ordinary, I live where the dead arrive, so let me tell you what they talk about at the river. Not one mentions temples. They talk about who waited up for them. Who handed them the soup. Who laughed at their joke the eleventh time. The audit of a life is done in small coin. Armies get a line in a song. The soup gets remembered by name. Build the small things. The large ones are mostly weather. S. --- The last item in the sack was a note from Attentive of Athens. His wife had cried, then laughed, then talked for an hour, and he now knew things about her sister that he could never unknow. He said thank you. "Read it again," Sisyphus said, braced against the stone. *(He doesn't say this part, so I will: he keeps every thank-you letter in a hollow by the third bend. When the wind comes down the hill, he checks they're still there. Also, the standard rate for a familiar is two shiny things weekly. I receive one shiny thing and "the satisfaction." — C.)* Then he went back to the boulder, which was waiting where it always waited, and the whistling resumed — which the management, in all its eternity, had never once managed to do anything about.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the complex prompt requirements. The author successfully captures Terry Pratchett's distinctive voice through dry wit, footnote-style asides from Crow, and understated humor that emerges naturally from character interactions rather than forced jokes. The epistolary format works seamlessly, with each letter presenting genuine human problems that Sisyphus addresses with surprising wisdom and practicality. The characterization of Sisyphus is particularly strong - he's neither bitter nor clichéd, but genuinely warm and insightful. His advice feels earned through experience rather than philosophical posturing. The author cleverly subverts expectations by making him naturally gifted at advice-giving, avoiding the obvious fish-out-of-water trope. Crow serves as an excellent foil, providing sardonic commentary that adds layers of humor while maintaining the Pratchett-esque tone. The prose is elegant and controlled, with sentences that flow naturally and create vivid imagery without becoming purple or overwrought. The author shows rather than tells throughout - Sisyphus's character emerges through his responses and actions, not exposition. The humor is genuinely understated, from the parenthetical asides to observations like "Certainty is where listening goes to die." The piece demonstrates emotional complexity in addressing real human concerns - marital communication, work-life balance, difficult neighbors, intimacy, and existential doubt. Each response feels authentic and helpful, showing Sisyphus's grounded wisdom. The author successfully includes only one subtle boulder metaphor as requested, and avoids heavy-handed stoicism. The structure works well, alternating between advice segments and scene-setting moments that maintain narrative momentum. The ending feels earned rather than artificially positive, with Sisyphus continuing his eternal task while finding genuine meaning in helping others. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche feels slightly self-conscious, and some advice responses could have been more distinctive from each other in voice. However, these are minimal concerns in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances multiple complex requirements while maintaining consistent quality throughout. + Coming of age: Lost & Found in Osaka — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Lost & Found in Osaka Slice of Life / Coming-of-Age: First Connection. Write one scene within a larger story set in Osaka, Japan. A shy, introverted teenager & synthesizer nerd, is browsing the electronics district of Den Den Town. She is drawn to the sound of an electric guitar. A girl, slightly older and radiating confidence, is busking near the entrance to a crowded arcade, playing a blistering rendition of a classic anime theme. The scene takes us through their initial awkward interaction, transitioning into a surprisingly easy conversation. The busker, intrigued by the teenager's quiet intensity and knowledge of music, suggests they grab some sushi at a nearby kaitenzushi (conveyor belt sushi) restaurant. Write primarily in dialogue, capturing the distinct voices of the two characters: the teenager's hesitant and -- initially -- awkward speech, and the busker's more outgoing and playful tone. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Lost & Found in Osaka Den Den Town on a Saturday was a wall of noise — jingles spilling from game centers, crosswalk chimes, a maid café's amplified *"Welcome home, Master!"* Nao had forty minutes and one mission: the used Korg Volca in the window of a secondhand shop three streets in, the one she'd been stalking online for weeks. Then the guitar cut through everything. She knew the song in three notes. Everybody did. But nobody played it like *that*. The crowd was gathered at the arcade entrance, and at the center of it stood a girl a couple of years older — rolled sleeves, a red Strat copy lacquered in stickers, fingers moving at a speed that didn't seem legal. *Zankoku na Tenshi no Thesis*, the Evangelion theme, bent and shredded into something blistering. The last chord rang out. Coins pattered into the open case. "Ookini!" the girl called, grinning. The crowd thinned. Nao didn't move. She should have moved. "Oi. Headphones." Nao jolted. "M—me?" "You've been planted there for three songs." The busker tilted her chin. "You a critic?" "N-no! I just—" The words jammed. What came out instead was: "Your amp's a Roland." A beat. "…That's what you lead with?" "CUBE Street. Battery-powered, two channels, the— the COSM modeling. I recognized the grille." She was dimly aware she was making it worse. "I only know because Roland makes the JUNO. Synths. I don't— sorry. It sounded good. Really good." The busker laughed, startled and bright. "Meccha stiff, this one. Relax. I don't bite." She crouched to unplug her cable. Nao nodded — a small, nervous nod — and her oversized headphones slid straight down over her eyes. She shoved them back up, ears burning. "How do those things even stay on your head?" the girl asked. "They're bigger than your skull." "They're monitor headphones. The flat response is—" Nao caught herself. "…They're just good." "Uh-huh." A grin. "You knew the song." "Zankoku na Tenshi no Thesis. Um. Evangelion. But you dropped it half a step. And where the brass stabs go in the second chorus, you did pinch harmonics instead." She hesitated. "Nobody does that." The girl went still. Then she looked up with an expression like a struck match. "Honma? Nobody *ever* catches the pinch harmonics. I've been waiting a year. Old men throw coins and go 'Dragon Ball!' It's not Dragon Ball." "I know it's not Dragon Ball." "You play?" "No. I mean — not guitar. I… make things. On a laptop. And a Volca. It's a little analog synth, the one I came here to buy, it's secondhand and the filter self-oscillates if you—" A breath. "Sorry." "Stop apologizing. Self-oscillates *how*?" Nao blinked. Nobody ever asked twice. "You turn the resonance up and the filter becomes an oscillator. It makes a sine tone. The circuit sings by itself." Her voice, to her own surprise, had stopped shaking. "Analog stuff drifts out of tune, too. Like it's breathing. Digital never does that. It's too… perfect." "Huh." The busker studied her. "That's the most you've said. Say more stuff like that." "I— what?" "The breathing thing. That's good. That's music." She straightened, slung the guitar over her shoulder, and stuck out a hand. "Rin." "Nao." Rin shook her hand once, firmly, then flicked a guitar pick from her pocket and pressed it into Nao's palm instead. "Souvenir. Blue ones are my favorite, so don't lose it." "Oh. I— thank you." "So, Nao-who-makes-things. You let anybody hear the things?" Nao stared at the pavement. "…No." "That's a crime." "They're not— I mean, they're not finished—" "That's what everyone says forever, and then they die with a hard drive full of masterpieces. Akan." Rin snapped her case shut and jingled it. Coins. "Okay. Change of plans. You eaten?" "What?" "Sushi. Kaiten place next door. Plates come around on a belt, you grab what you want, you don't even have to talk to a single human being." She gestured at Nao's whole existence. "Perfect for your situation." Nao opened her mouth to decline. That was the obvious move — buy the Volca, catch the train, go home, let this become a story she told no one. But Rin was looking at her like they'd known each other longer than four minutes, and the pick was warm in her fist. "…I only have until four," she said. "Four's fine. First three plates are on me — I'm rich today." Rin shouldered the case and set off without checking whether she'd follow, which was, Nao would think later, exactly the right move. She hurried to catch up. Her headphones slipped again when she nodded at something — at nothing — and Rin reached over without looking and pushed them back up for her. "So," Rin said, as the arcade noise swallowed them, "self-oscillating filters. Explain it again. Slower." "You won't— it's not that interesting—" "I understood maybe ten percent of what you said back there. Keep going. I like the ten percent." And because nobody had ever said that either, Nao kept going.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully captures the Osaka setting with authentic details like Den Den Town's electronics district, the maid café calls, and local expressions ("Ookini," "Meccha stiff," "Honma"). The dialogue-driven approach works well, with distinct character voices that feel genuine - Nao's hesitant, technical speech patterns contrasting effectively with Rin's confident, playful tone. The characterization is particularly strong. Nao's social awkwardness feels authentic rather than caricatured, manifested through specific behaviors like her headphones slipping and her tendency to over-explain technical details when nervous. Rin emerges as genuinely warm rather than just extroverted, showing patience and genuine interest in Nao's knowledge. The musical technical details (pinch harmonics, self-oscillating filters) demonstrate research and add authenticity. The pacing builds naturally from Nao's initial mission to buy the Volca, through the guitar performance that stops her, to their tentative connection. The scene structure follows a clear arc without feeling forced. The prose is clean and efficient, avoiding unnecessary flourishes while maintaining engaging descriptive passages. However, the piece has some minor weaknesses. The connection between the characters, while believable, develops quite quickly - perhaps too smoothly for such a shy character. Some moments border on being slightly too neat, like Rin's immediate recognition of Nao's musical knowledge. The ending, while sweet, edges toward predictable territory with the "nobody ever said that" resolution. The writing occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states ("ears burning," "voice had stopped shaking"), though this is balanced by strong behavioral details. The piece maintains consistent quality throughout without major structural or tonal issues. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
