+ Teen horror: Tick Tock (Goosebumps) — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
Teen Horror (Goosebumps style) Write a chapter where a middle school student discovers their new smart watch has the ability to rewind time, but doesn't realize that each use is aging them until they notice physical changes (like accelerating through puberty) and that people are treating them differently. They inadvertently cause a timeline shift with disastrous consequences -- and it can only be rectified (maybe?) by reversing the clock once more. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# DO-OVER I found the watch on the sidewalk by the school fence, right under the gray cat. The cat was always there. One green eye, one cloudy one. It watched me pick up the watch — no brand, just a silver case, a crown, and one button. It fit my wrist perfectly. Too perfectly, like it had been waiting for me. I should've wondered about that. I didn't. First rewind: lunch. I tripped on Jonah's backpack strap and launched a whole chocolate milk across the cafeteria. It hit the floor, my shirt, and — worst of all — Kayla's shoes. Everyone laughed. My face caught fire. I grabbed the crown and twisted, the way you'd twist anything when you're panicking. The world *hiccupped*. Sound drained away. Everything jerked backward like a video in reverse — and I was standing in the lunch line again, milk in hand, dry shirt, no laughter. I stepped around Jonah's backpack. No spill. Nobody remembered anything. My wrist tingled under the watch. On my way out, the gray cat sat up straight and stared at me through the fence. Not at the building. At *me*. Like it recognized me. Like we'd met before. Second rewind: history quiz. "What year did the War of 1812 begin?" I actually said out loud, "You have GOT to be kidding me." Twist. Redo. Aced it. That weekend, my jeans were two inches too short. My voice cracked like a breaking branch. Mom ruffled my hair and said, "Puberty's going to hit you like a truck, kiddo." The cat watched me all week. After the third rewind — I called Kayla "Karen," don't ask — it was waiting at the fence with its tail wrapped around its paws, and its good eye followed me the whole way down the block. By then I had zits. A shadow of stubble. A substitute teacher asked if I was a new student and where I'd transferred from. Jonah punched my arm and said, "Dude, when did you get taller than *me*?" That night I stood against the doorframe where Mom marks my height every birthday. I was three inches past the mark she'd made two months ago. I looked at the watch. The screen didn't show the time anymore. It showed a number. *14.2.* It wasn't telling time. It was *keeping* it. Every minute I took back, I paid for. Months per minute. Years per hour. I tore at the clasp with my fingernails, then with scissors, then with my teeth. The clasp wouldn't open. It wouldn't even scratch. "Fine," I whispered. "Never again. Done." Then, Tuesday, Jonah grabbed the watch at my locker. "Why does it say fourteen-point-two? Milo, why is it glowing —" "Give it BACK!" I lunged. He stumbled backward, laughing, holding it over his head — — back, back, off the curb — Brakes screamed. A sound I will never un-hear. I didn't think. I twisted the crown with everything I had. Three hours. The crown burned cold, the cold shot up my arm into my skull, and the world smeared like wet paint — Morning. My locker. Jonah standing right beside it, alive, *alive*, whole. I grabbed him and hugged him sobbing. "Uh," Jonah said, prying me off. "Do I... know you?" My voice came out deep. A stranger's voice. My reflection in the locker window: sixteen, maybe seventeen. A stranger's face. I ran home. My mom answered the door, took one look at me, and said the four worst words I've ever heard: "Can I help you?" My bedroom was a sewing room. There were no photos of me anywhere. I didn't exist. The gray cat sat on our fence. It looked at me the way you look at an old friend. Or an old mistake. I walked back to the school fence in the dark, pressed the crown, and twisted it backward to before everything — a whole week, maybe more. It fought me. It burned. I held on until my vision went white and I heard the years pouring out of me like sand — I woke up small. Small bed. Small hands. Space posters on my wall. In the mirror: me. *Me.* Twelve years old, milk teeth and all. Mom knew me at breakfast. Jonah texted: *bus in five, doofus.* I walked past the school fence with my heart pounding. The cat was there. At its paws, in the grass, something silver glinted. I grabbed the watch and hurled it into the storm drain. I heard it clatter down into the dark, and I laughed like a crazy person all the way to class. That night, scratching at my window. The cat sat on the sill in the rain, soaked, patient. It opened its mouth and dropped something onto the wood. Something round and silver, still ticking. The screen lit up all by itself. *WELCOME BACK, MILO.* The cat blinked at me, slow and knowing, like we were old, old friends. Like it had all the time in the world. Maybe it did.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Goosebumps-style teen horror prompt, incorporating all required elements: the smart watch discovery, time rewinding ability, physical aging consequences, social recognition changes, timeline disruption, and the attempt at reversal. The story maintains an appropriately dark tone throughout while remaining accessible to middle-grade readers. The narrative structure is well-paced, using short, punchy sections that build tension effectively. The protagonist Milo's voice feels authentic for a middle schooler, with natural dialogue and realistic concerns about social embarrassment. The watch's aging mechanism is cleverly revealed through physical changes and the numerical display showing "14.2," creating genuine horror as Milo realizes the cost of his power. The imagery is particularly strong, especially the recurring gray cat motif that serves as both witness and harbinger. Descriptions like "the world hiccupped" and "years pouring out of me like sand" are evocative without being overwrought. The story effectively uses show-don't-tell techniques, revealing Milo's transformation through concrete details like outgrown jeans and his mother's failure to recognize him. The emotional progression feels earned, moving from teenage embarrassment to genuine terror as consequences escalate. Jonah's near-death experience provides appropriate stakes, and Milo's desperate attempt to save his friend at great personal cost demonstrates character growth. The ending maintains the horror tradition's cyclical nature without feeling forced. However, some elements could be stronger. The cat's supernatural nature and connection to the watch remains somewhat underdeveloped, though this ambiguity works within the genre. The dialogue, while functional, occasionally lacks distinctiveness between characters. The story's rapid pace, while appropriate for the format, sometimes rushes past moments that could benefit from deeper exploration. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, avoiding purple prose while still creating atmospheric tension. The horror elements feel age-appropriate while remaining genuinely unsettling, particularly the existential terror of being erased from one's own life. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 6.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Noir romance: Carnival of Dangerous Intentions — Score: 85.0
Prompt:
Carnival of Dangerous Intentions Coney Island, New York, July 1937. She stopped just before reaching the shooting gallery set up along the boardwalk. It was near dusk, and the carnival lights were buzzing on. **His locks**. The words flashed through her mind, again, like an intruder. Such an old-fashioned turn of phrase that kept surfacing, and something else -- dangerously compelling -- quickly suppressed, lest she question why she had returned here at all. This place where she had promised to meet the stranger -- the young man with the obsidian curls and even darker eyes. She didn't even know his name. Write a scene in a larger story inspired by this prompt. The scene should capture an atmosphere that blends romantic allure with an undercurrent of subtle danger or moral ambiguity. Think 50 shades, but with a more restrained classic sensibility. The scene is all about establishing tension. Write for the feminine perspective. Include roughly 50% dialogue, 20% internal monologue, and 30% exposition and scene setting. The tone should have some edge to it. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Carnival of Dangerous Intentions The barker's voice rolled over the planks, oily and bright. "Three shots a nickel! Win the little lady a prize!" She stood at the edge of the gallery's painted light and did not step into it. *His locks.* There it was again, that absurd antique phrase, surfacing like something retrieved from a drowned trunk. She'd read it in one of her mother's novels, surely. She was not a woman who thought in such words. She was a woman who had left her wedding band in the saucer by the kitchen sink, who had told herself it was the heat, the swelling of her fingers in July — and who had then walked to the subway with the pale stripe naked on her hand. Three days ago, in the crush at the Cyclone's exit, he had picked her glove off the boards and held it out as though returning evidence. *Thursday. The shooting gallery. Dusk.* Not a question. She had said nothing at all, which he had correctly understood as yes. "You came." The voice arrived at her shoulder before he did. She didn't turn. "I was out walking." "Alone. On a Thursday. Without your ring." Now she turned, and the sodium lamp above the gallery stuttered, throwing his face into strange relief — the obsidian curls, the eyes beneath them darker still, patient as deep water. "You don't know that's my ring finger." "Stand there," he said mildly, already laying a nickel on the counter. "Where I can see you." It should have offended her. Instead she felt an odd, liquid relief at being told — the way a train passenger feels the engine take the grade. *This is the danger of him,* she thought. *Not that he commands. That I obey, and call it curiosity.* He took up the .22 and shot like a man signing his name: three ducks, three clay pipes, the star dead center. The barker whistled. "Mister, you done this before." "Once or twice." "Doll for the lady? Big one?" "No." He pointed along the prize shelf. "The ring." The barker stared. "That ain't worth the nickel you give me." "The ring." It was tin, plated in something pretending to be gold, the sort of trinket that turned a finger green by Sunday. He took her left hand — unhurried, warm, certain — and she let him, and hated the ease of letting him. "What do you think you're doing?" "Something you're going to allow." He slid it on over the pale stripe. "There. Now it isn't bare." "You don't even know my name." "Tell me one." "Lillian." Her mother's name, and the lie tasted of copper pennies, of communion wine. "Liar," he said, almost gently. "Keep it anyway." They walked. The lights buzzed and failed and recovered, and in the intervals the crowd's faces went strange — masks, all of them, honest only in the flicker. A patrolman ambled past the hot corn stand, and her stranger turned to admire a pyramid of Kewpie dolls, his hand finding the small of her back and moving her along so smoothly she was three steps gone before she understood she'd been moved. That was when she saw his knuckles: split, half-healed. "Who did you hit?" "Does it matter?" "It should." "It doesn't." He glanced down at her, and the light caught him sideways. "That's why you came." "What's *your* name, then?" "If I give it to you, you'll take it home and say it into your mirror, and that will be the end of it. This only works in the dark, Lillian-who-isn't." "You talk like a man in a picture show." "And yet you're still standing here." They had reached the rail. Below, the black water worried the pilings. The Wonder Wheel turned against the last violet of the sky, its swinging cars sliding loose along their tracks. "Why did you come back?" she asked. "To Coney. To me." "I never said I'd been here before." "That's not an answer." "It's the only true one you're getting tonight." "What do you want from me?" He considered the word the way a man bites a coin. "Your attention. Which I seem to have." A pause. "Everything after this is negotiation." "My—" She stopped. Started again. "Someone expects me." "Then don't keep him waiting." He let the silence do its work, the lights stuttering amber across his mouth. "Or do. One ride. Up where it's quiet, where the whole island is nothing but lamps going on and off. Then I'll put you on the subway myself, and you can go home and take off a tin ring, and whichever life you choose, you'll have had one honest hour." "And if I say no?" "Then I'll walk ten paces behind you to the station. Far enough that no one could prove we were together." He tilted his head. "You'll spend the whole ride wondering why you said it." *Because I am afraid,* she answered, but only inside, where the answer belonged to her. Because the apartment waited with its dishes and its newspaper and its careful, bloodless years. Because the tin on her finger weighed more than gold ever had. "One ride," she said. "One," he agreed, in the tone of a man who counted differently than other men. The car swung out over the dark and the lights flickered below like a code she hadn't learned. Wind came off the water and lifted *his locks* — the old phrase arriving once more, uninvited, and this time she let it stay.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craft in establishing atmospheric tension and psychological complexity. The author successfully captures the requested blend of romantic allure and moral ambiguity, set against the vivid backdrop of 1937 Coney Island. The writing maintains a consistent noir-influenced tone throughout, with careful attention to period details and sensory imagery. The dialogue feels authentic and purposeful, revealing character through subtext rather than exposition. The mysterious male character's commanding yet restrained speech patterns effectively establish his dangerous appeal, while the protagonist's responses reveal her internal conflict between propriety and desire. The conversation flows naturally and serves the dual purpose of advancing the plot and deepening characterization. The internal monologue successfully captures the feminine perspective as requested, particularly in moments like her reflection on removing her wedding ring and the recurring phrase "his locks." The protagonist's psychological state is rendered with nuance - her simultaneous attraction and wariness feel genuine and complex. The prose strikes an effective balance between elegance and accessibility. Phrases like "patient as deep water" and "the way a train passenger feels the engine take the grade" demonstrate skillful metaphorical thinking without becoming overwrought. The writing maintains restraint appropriate to the "classic sensibility" requested while still conveying underlying tension. The scene structure adheres well to the specified dialogue/monologue/exposition ratios, and the pacing builds tension effectively toward the final decision to ride the Wonder Wheel. The ending feels earned rather than forced, with the protagonist's choice emerging naturally from her established psychology. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the period voice slips slightly, and a few descriptions that border on purple prose, though these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully avoids common pitfalls like unearned character transformations or incongruent tonal shifts. + Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 84.3
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter Two: The Caribou Man The backstrap fried up darker than it should have. JT noticed that right off—the meat going near-black in the cast iron, bleeding juice the color of chokecherries—and he noticed that nobody else noticed. His father had always cooked venison to death, said anything shy of shoe-leather was *for dogs and Frenchmen*, but tonight he forked it onto their plates rare, red pooling, and said grace over it same as always. Mike ate two helpings. Mike, who picked around gristle like a finicky cat. "Good eatin'," Mike said, around a mouthful. "First of the season always tastes best," his father answered, and it had the sound of something memorized, like the grace itself. He hummed while he ate, low and tuneless—except it wasn't tuneless, JT realized. He almost knew it. It lived somewhere at the bottom of his memory, under the hymnals and the AM gospel stations, and that realization made him set his fork down. "Saw smoke today," JT said, mostly to hear a voice that sounded normal. "Northeast, past the burnt ridge. While you two were—" *While you two were holding hands with a buck in the clearing.* "While I was catching up." His father's fork stopped halfway to his mouth. "That's the Pelletier place." "I thought that cabin was abandoned." "It is." His father resumed chewing. "Old Pelletier died before you were born." "Somebody lives there," Mike said, mild as milk, speaking to his plate. His father set the fork down. "Nobody lives there, Michael." The *Michael* landed like a slap, and Mike flinched and shut up, and JT sat very still inside himself, because when he was little, Grandpa used to say the Caribou Man kept that shack up in the burnt country. It had been a joke, except Grandpa never joked about the woods. *He don't bother you if you don't bother him,* Grandpa said once, and when JT asked who, the old man looked at him a long time and said, *Nobody, boy. Nobody at all.* "You stay clear of that place," his father said now, eyes on the table between them, on nothing. "Both of you. That's not our hunt." He went back to his plate, and to humming, and neither of them said another word about it, and JT thought: *you're over-tired, that's all. The drive. The cold.* His mother used to say he dreamed with his eyes open. Used to say it right up until she stopped saying much of anything to him at all. --- He woke some hours later with the certainty that the lodge had gone quiet in a *listening* way. The Frigidaire's cycle had shut off. The wind had dropped. The whole house seemed to be holding its breath, and JT lay in his bunk telling himself he was still asleep, that this was the over-tired part, the eyes-open-dreaming part— A porch board creaked. The third one from the steps. He had known that board his whole life. The window over his bunk faced the yard. He told himself not to look, and looked. His father and Mike stood at the edge of the yard, side by side in their long johns, barefoot on the frosted ground. Barefoot, in October, at whatever hour this was—the moon down, the stars thick as salt. They faced the treeline the way you face a person you're waiting on. Beyond the porch light's reach, between the black spruce trunks, something pale rose and branched: antlers, moving slow, like hands feeling their way through a dark room. Fog rolled from the buck's nostrils. Its ears flicked, lazy, patient, the way the first caribou's had. *Waiting for the shot.* JT's mind shouted at him—*look away, turn around, just run*—and his body, as it had in the clearing, declined. He watched his father lean forward slightly, lips moving. Whispering. And the awful thing, the thing JT would turn over and over in the gray hours, was that the whispering had *pauses* in it. Gaps. Like conversation. Like something in the silence was answering back. Then his father said, without turning around, in a voice perfectly clear and perfectly ordinary: "Go on back to bed, JT." JT hadn't made a sound. He was sure of it. *Mostly* sure. "He says you ought to have come out from the brush this afternoon." His father still hadn't turned. His head was tilted, listening to something. "He don't care for sneaks." The buck dipped its head. Slow. Like a nod. Like *yes.* Mike turned then—only Mike—and looked straight at the window where JT stood, and smiled, and Mike's eyes in the dark were flat and calm, and JT recoiled back into the bunk with his heart slamming, pulling the blanket up like a child, and that was the last thing, that was all, because— --- Because morning came, and he woke with the blanket twisted around him and his boots by the door where he'd left them, frost-rimed on the laces though the night had been still. *Dreamed it. Eyes open, like Mom said.* He came into the kitchen and his father was at the stove, bright-eyed, frying something dark, and the smell was rich and sweet and wrong. "Eat up, boy," his father said. "Today we hunt the northeast draw. Up past the burnt ridge." Toward Pelletier's. *That's not our hunt,* his father had said, not twelve hours ago. JT opened his mouth—to ask, to remind him, to say anything—and his father looked over his shoulder at him. His father's eyes were dark, and flat, and calm. Calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Close enough to touch. JT closed his mouth. Always safer that way—though he was beginning, dimly, to wonder what exactly it was safer *from.* He sat down. He picked up his fork. His father went back to the stove, humming, and this time JT knew the tune. He'd heard it yesterday, in the clearing, in the long deep breaths of the thing standing close enough to touch. It was the sound of something breathing out, and waiting, and not minding the wait at all.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Stephen King-inspired surreal horror style requested. The author successfully maintains JT's perspective as an unreliable narrator caught between reality and nightmare, creating effective psychological ambiguity. The isolation and creeping dread are well-established through JT's internal observations and his inability to voice his concerns. The writing shows sophisticated control of atmosphere and pacing. The opening scene with the unnaturally dark meat creates immediate unease, while the dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character dynamics, building tension, and advancing the supernatural elements. The father's authoritative "Michael" and subsequent prohibition about the Pelletier place effectively establishes family hierarchy and forbidden territory. The nocturnal scene is particularly well-crafted, using concrete details (the specific porch board, barefoot figures on frost) to ground the surreal elements. The revelation that the father somehow knew JT was watching creates genuine chills. The circular structure, returning to the dark meat and the father's changed demeanor, provides satisfying narrative cohesion. Character actions remain believable within the horror context. JT's paralysis and silence feel authentic for someone caught in an incomprehensible situation. The gradual revelation of the father and Mike's transformation is handled with restraint - they're recognizably themselves yet fundamentally altered. The prose demonstrates elegance without excess. Phrases like "breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame" and "the house seemed to be holding its breath" create vivid imagery without purple prose. The writing maintains King's characteristic blend of colloquial voice with literary sophistication. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetition of certain phrases and images, and the Caribou Man backstory feels slightly convenient, though it serves the narrative effectively. The ending, while appropriately dark, could have pushed the horror elements slightly further for maximum impact. + Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Hell Is Other Demons Dawn in Hell was an administrative decision. It had been scheduled for six o'clock since the Beginning, and had arrived on time perhaps twice, both times by accident. Lucifer Morningstar woke alone in a bed the size of a modest nation, and lay listening to his dog eat his furniture. "Doom," he said. "Stop." The hellhound paused. His true name was a chord of dissonance that had once driven an entire monastery to madness, but Lucifer had shortened it. Doom weighed the command against the single brain cell allotted him, and resumed chewing the near leg of the Iron Throne. Iron filings dusted the dais like the first snow of a long winter. "Load-bearing," said Lucifer, and got up to make eggs. The eggs screamed, but only briefly, and out of habit rather than conviction. He gave Doom the yolks. Standards had to be maintained, even in decline. * The morning correspondence arrived with Belial, seneschal of the palace, a demon with the bearing of a man carrying bad news across a minefield, which he was, daily. "Upstairs has sent a fourth note about the screaming. The Arrangement stipulates a dull roar. They feel we've been more of a *shrieking.*" "Draft something polite that means nothing. Diplomacy, Belial." "The Sixth Circle is behind quota on torments, and ahead on forms explaining why. And there's a note from the Ninth. Monsieur Sartre writes: *Hell is other people.*" "Send the standard reply." "*You should meet my demons.*" "There's a good fellow. Next." "Final item. The claims adjuster in Holding has appealed his sentence, on the grounds that a judgment delivered from a visibly leaning throne lacks the gravity due his damnation." "Appeal denied. Tell him the tilt is theological." Belial bowed and did not mention Lilith. Nobody mentioned Lilith. They had been off again for forty years, which was nothing, which was everything, and the dog had started chewing the throne the week she left, and no one said anything about that, either. * She arrived at noon. The candles bowed and every demon in the hall abruptly remembered urgent business elsewhere, which was how Lucifer knew, before the doors opened, that his wife was home. "Doom's due his worming," Lilith said, by way of hello. "He's immortal." "Then it's overdue." She surveyed the throne room the way a health inspector surveys a kitchen. "You've let the place go." "The Ninth Circle is frozen. It's meant to be." "It's supposed to be *artfully* frozen." Doom crossed the hall at a gallop and flung himself at her feet, groveling in a manner that destroyed the dignity of the entire concept of hellhounds. "Traitor," said Lucifer, mildly. Lilith produced a brush from somewhere and set to work. "He's anxious. Dogs act out when the home is unsettled." "The home is Hell." "You're not as funny as you think you are." "I am precisely as funny as I think I am." She didn't answer, because she was looking at the throne — the chewed leg, the lean, the filings. She had forged that throne herself, in the first century, out of the chains they had broken. Neither of them mentioned it. "You haven't fixed it," she said. "It has character." "It has tetanus." * "I'm not staying for lunch," said Lilith, and stayed for lunch. They ate on the balcony overlooking the lake of fire, which was running low, because everything was, lately. She said he'd gotten thin. He said he was the Devil and was meant to be lean. She criticized the cutlery; he criticized her criticism; and for twenty minutes it was exactly like old times, which is to say wonderful, and exhausting, and one wrong word away from a war that would level the middle circles. The afternoon judgments were scheduled for three. The court assembled, the damned were brought up in chains, and the claims adjuster stood in the dock to hear his sentence pronounced in full, because someone had filed an appeal, and appeals had consequences. Lucifer rose, spread his wings, shifted his weight — The leg, chewed to a fatal taper, chose that moment to surrender. The Iron Throne went over with a sound like the end of a smaller, pettier world, taking the Morningstar with it. Silence. Then the adjuster laughed — one bright, hysterical bark of it — and every soul in the hall froze, waiting to be unmade. Lucifer, on the floor, wings everywhere, laughed. It had been four thousand years since the last time. Half the court assumed it was a new torment and screamed accordingly. Belial dropped the ledger. And Lilith looked at him the way she had looked at him before the Fall, like someone remembering the first half of a joke. * She left at dusk, taking the good wine, as alimony. At the doorway she paused, with the evening — also an administrative decision — behind her. "Thursday," she said. "I'll check his teeth." "His teeth are fine." "Thursday," she repeated, and was gone. Lucifer righted the throne himself, wedged a first edition of something holy under the ruined leg, and sat. It wobbled. Everything wobbled; you learned to shift your weight. Doom lay down across his feet, sighing the sigh of a dog whose whole family had, briefly, been in one room. "Thursday," Lucifer told him, and scratched him behind the ears, and did not fix the leg. Hope, he reflected, got in everywhere, like damp. It was the only thing in Hell he'd never worked out how to tax.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the requested tone and style, drawing clear inspiration from Neil Gaiman's approach to blending the mundane with the fantastical. The story effectively merges the cosmic scope of running Hell with intimate relationship dynamics, creating the requested dark comedy through understated irony. The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a figure both grandiose and petty, dealing with administrative minutiae while nursing romantic wounds. His voice remains consistent throughout - dry, sardonic, yet vulnerable beneath the surface. Lilith is equally well-drawn in her limited appearances, conveying volumes through brief exchanges and actions. The relationship dynamic feels authentic despite the supernatural setting. The prose demonstrates considerable skill, with memorable phrases like "Dawn in Hell was an administrative decision" and "Hope, he reflected, got in everywhere, like damp." The writing maintains Gaiman-esque elegance without falling into purple prose territory. The imagery is vivid and purposeful - the chewed throne leg serving as both literal plot device and metaphor for the deteriorating relationship. The story's structure works well, moving through a day while building toward the throne collapse as both comedic climax and emotional turning point. The ending strikes an appropriate note of cautious hope without being overly sentimental or unearned. However, some elements could be stronger. While the dialogue is generally effective, a few exchanges feel slightly mechanical in service of exposition. The hellhound Doom, while charming, occasionally veers toward cute rather than maintaining the story's darker edge. Some of the Hell-as-bureaucracy jokes, while amusing, don't always feel fresh or surprising. The piece succeeds in its primary goals: creating believable relationship drama in a fantastical setting, maintaining consistent dark comedy, and delivering emotional resonance through understated moments. The writing demonstrates technical proficiency and genuine creativity in its execution. + Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 90.5
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Twelfth Labor The doves woke me, as they always did — quarreling in the rafters while the sky beyond the bars turned the color of old bronze. Then came the sounds I had measured six years of mornings by: the whetstone's rasp, old Maro's cough, the first crack of a wooden sword against the palus in the yard, and beneath it all, faint as surf, the roar from the Colosseum where the morning's beast-hunt had begun. A tunnel connects our school to that place. Some days I thought of it as a throat. "Alcides!" the guard called, and I answered, as I had learned to answer. My mother named me Zyraxes. The crowd named me for a god. In the yard the heat was already rising off the dust. I worked the sica against the post — hook, turn, drag the curve across where a murmillo's ribs would be — until my callused palm passed from ache to numb, which was as close to peace as I ever came. The empire keeps its enemies under one roof and teaches them to dance: beside me a Numidian, a Jew, two Germans, and young Lucius, a butcher's son from across the Tiber who was nobody's enemy at all. Free-born, that was the joke. He had sold himself over four thousand sesterces of debt and sworn the same oath I had no choice in — to be burned, bound, beaten, and killed by the blade. He moved wrong this morning, stiff with fear. Tomorrow was his first fight. "Watch the sand, not the sword," Maro growled at him, leaning on the wooden blade they had given him the year the legions took my city. He had earned his freedom and come back to the school within the month. *Free is a country with no sand in it*, he told me once. *I didn't know the customs.* At the midday trough we ate what we always ate — barley and beans, thick as mortar, washed down with sour posca. Barley-men, they call us. At the gate, as always, they were selling what the strigil scraped off our bodies, decanted into little glass vials for ladies who would faint if they touched our hands. On the wall outside, some hand had chalked ALCIDES with a girl's name beneath it. My oath had made me *infamis*, a legal no one. The wall said otherwise. Because I had asked, and because veterans who fill seats are humored in their superstitions, the lanista sent me with a guard to the Forum Boarium, to offer to Hercules before the games. We went through the Forum of the Emperor, past the new column with scaffolding still clinging to its crown. The Emperor's artists had carved my war into it in a rising spiral — two hundred paces of marble if you unrolled it, they said. Halfway up I found my king, Decebalus, falling on his own knife in a rainstorm, forever. The sun would cross his death three times a day until Rome fell. Optimus Princeps, they call Trajan — the best of them. I had seen his goodness in stone and his goodness in bread, and both were minted from my country. At the Porticus Minucia the dole crowd shuffled forward with their grain tickets, patient as cattle, and a crier was already singing tomorrow's program: *the Emperor, Father of the Country, gives—* He gives them bread and he gives them us. A one-eyed veteran of the Dacian war sat begging by the fountain, his leg gone below the knee. Perhaps he had burned my village; perhaps I had known his brother's killer. I dropped a coin in his bowl, and he blessed me in the name of the very god whose altar I was walking toward. Rome is efficient that way. All along the street men talked of Parthia, of contracts and legions and glory — the next war already priced like grain futures. The round temple stood white among the cattle pens, the air thick with dung and incense and river-smell. At the Great Altar I paid the priest a tenth of my last purse — the merchants' tithe, the *decuma*. Let the god grow accustomed to my money. I prayed with my head uncovered in the Greek way, and I did not ask to win. I asked what I always asked. They paint Hercules in Omphale's house wearing a woman's skirt, carding wool, and they mean it for mockery. I have always thought he looks patient. He was a slave too. He did the work set before him, and in the end the fire on Oeta burned away only the part of him that could die. *Remember the way up*, I said, and pressed my thumb to the clay. They fed us beef that night — the last free banquet — and let the curious in to watch us chew. A senator's wife memorized my scars while her husband priced me like a horse. Let them look. Hercules is looked at too, standing in the sky. I found Lucius staring at his plate and sat beside him. "Watch the referee, not the crowd," I said. "The crowd wants a story. The referee wants to go home. Give them the story and live to hear it told wrong." He nodded without believing me. That was all right. Belief comes after, if there is an after. Later I lay on my cot in the dark, listening to the ropes of the great awning creak above the amphitheater like a ship waiting for weather. Tomorrow would be my twelfth fight. Eleven labors behind me; one more, if the crowd was moved, might buy the wooden sword — my apotheosis, not into heaven but out a door. I had chosen to live, once, when my king chose the knife, and six years later I still could not say which of us had taken the steep road. I fell asleep asking a god who had been a slave to remember me.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates considerable literary skill and historical knowledge. The author creates a compelling first-person narrative voice for Alcides/Zyraxes, a Dacian gladiator in Trajanic Rome, weaving together personal reflection with broader social and political commentary. The prose is consistently elegant and controlled, with particularly strong sensory details that immerse the reader in ancient Rome - from the quarreling doves at dawn to the smell of dung and incense at the temple. The author demonstrates impressive historical knowledge, seamlessly incorporating authentic details like Trajan's Column, the grain dole at Porticus Minucia, and gladiatorial customs without making them feel forced or expository. The character of Alcides is nuanced and psychologically complex. His internal conflict between survival and honor, his relationship with his enslaved identity versus his public persona, and his philosophical reflections on freedom create a believable and emotionally engaging protagonist. The parallel drawn between himself and Hercules is particularly well-executed, providing thematic depth without being heavy-handed. The writing successfully captures the "slice of life" quality requested, feeling like part of a larger narrative while being complete in itself. The political commentary - Rome's exploitation of conquered peoples, the bread and circuses dynamic, the machinery of empire - emerges naturally from the character's perspective rather than feeling imposed. The prose style is mature and literary without becoming overwrought. Sentences like "Free is a country with no sand in it" and "Rome is efficient that way" demonstrate wit and insight. The ending is appropriately melancholic and fits the tone perfectly. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the philosophical reflection might feel slightly too polished for the character, and some metaphors that border on being overly literary, though these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Lighthouse Keeper **Muckle Flugga, Unst — 18 September 1961** Mither, Landed Thursday, and my legs still think they're at sea. The light is a stout grey tower at the north end of Unst, above an inlet called Burra Firth. A mile past us is Out Stack — the last of Britain. Polson, the principal keeper, says there's nothing between it and the North Pole but weather, and I believe him. He has said nine words to me in four days; I am counting. My room is the size of your press, and dry. Dinna fash about the loneliness. The bonxies off Hermaness keep me right — terrible gossips, the pair of them. Alec **Shore Street, Fraserburgh — 27 September 1961** Alec, Parcel by the post: oatmeal, tablet, three pairs of socks — the soft grey, no the itchy. The tablet cost a wrestle at the Co-op van, so eat it slow. Effie's lad is at the herring; the gutting quines are down from Peterheid. The minister's wife speired after you, and I said my son keeps a light at the back of beyond, and she looked at me as if I'd said the moon. Your father's sea boots are still by the door. Twelve year and I canna shift them. Write oftener. Mither **Muckle Flugga — 2 November 1961** Mither, The *Pharos* could not land our relief — nine days overdue now, and we are rationing coal and conversation. The seas come up the rock like they've a mind to visit; the tower just leans its shoulder into them. I read your letters till the folds go soft. The dark comes at half three. I had never kenned that dark could have a weight to it. Tell Effie her lad still owes me a shilling from the fitba coupons. Alec **Shore Street — 14 November 1961** Alec, It is twelve year this day. I did fine till the kirk bell, and then I didna. Sat with the *People's Friend* and read the one page forty times. I will say what I have never said, for it sits wrong that you are up there on his account. Of every light in Scotland you took the one that stands over the water that took him. I have hated every light that ever burned, Alec — great useless eyes watching men drown. There. It is said, and I am sorry, and I am not. Mither **Muckle Flugga — 5 December 1961** Mither, Don't be sorry. I came here for him, I think, though I didna know it when I signed. It is daft — a drowned man is nowhere, that's the whole of it. But at the top of the watch, when the beam goes round and out over the Stack, I feel less far from him than at his own empty grave. Tell me true: would he have wanted this for me? You speak only of losing him, never of what he wanted. I am twenty-eight. I would know him as a man now. Alec **Shore Street — 19 December 1961** Alec, True, then. The winter afore he died, your father put his name to the Northern Lighthouse Board. He was done with the fishing — said he had killed enough and would rather save men at a distance. I never told you, for after we lost him I couldna abide the thought, and then you signed and I near took to my bed. His hand is on the forms still, in the kist. Come the relief, you shall have them. Parcel for the twa of you: dumpling, and a gansey off Wullie Tait's boy. Share the dumpling with your nine
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates strong technical execution and authentic voice work. The Scottish dialect is handled with skill - neither overdone nor tokenistic, but natural and consistent throughout both characters' voices. The mother's voice is distinctly different from Alec's, with her more direct, practical tone contrasting his more reflective, literary sensibilities. The setting is vividly realized through specific details like "Muckle Flugga," "Out Stack," and "Burra Firth" that ground the story in authentic geography. The isolation is palpable through details like rationing "coal and conversation" and the weight of darkness. The progression from September to December effectively shows the deepening isolation and emotional journey. The central revelation about the father's death and his own application to the lighthouse service creates genuine emotional complexity. The mother's confession that she "hated every light that ever burned" is particularly powerful, as is Alec's realization that he came "for him, I think, though I didna know it when I signed." This psychological insight feels earned rather than contrived. However, the piece appears incomplete, cutting off mid-sentence with "Share the dumpling with your nine" - likely meant to be "nine words" referring to the taciturn Polson. This truncation prevents assessment of the promised climax and resolution. The emotional arc is building effectively toward what could be a satisfying conclusion, but we cannot evaluate the actual ending. The writing avoids clichés about lighthouse keepers and Scottish characters, instead finding fresh angles through the family history and the son's unconscious pilgrimage to understand his father. The prose is controlled and atmospheric without becoming purple or overwrought. The epistolary format is used effectively to create gaps and silences that enhance the emotional impact. The characterization is nuanced - both mother and son are complex, with the mother's anger at the lights coexisting with her love for her son, and Alec's isolation driving him toward rather than away from understanding his father's choices. + First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Hacker Mindset The hostess puts us at a two-top by the kitchen, which is fine. I've already mapped the room: two exits, three counting the alarmed fire door I could open with a gift card and a bad attitude. One dome camera, tilted fifteen degrees too high to catch a face. Guest Wi-Fi accepts any email address, so I gave it the manager's. Our waiter memorizes four tables' worth of orders without writing anything down, which is either impressive or a single point of failure. I don't *do* anything with this stuff anymore. I just notice. Noticing is the itch; exploiting used to be the scratch. Six years clean, minus the noticing. Then Maya sits down, three minutes late, apologizing: her dog-sitter bailed, Biscuit has separation anxiety. Some deprecated subsystem in my skull lights up anyway. Pet name. High-value target. *Biscuit.* I hate it. I file it anyway. It gets better fast. Her tote has a deck box clipped to it — Ultra Pro, worn at the corners — and I say *no way*, and she says "You play?" and her whole face does the thing mine just did. She has opinions about the reserved list. I have opinions about her opinions. I peg her as a control player before she admits it — she lets silences sit instead of filling them — and when I tell her that, she laughs hard enough that the next table looks over. Good board state. Great opening hand. Keep. No mulligan. For an hour I'm a normal person on a date. She grew up in Portland, on Larchmont Avenue, across from Rosa Parks Elementary, and the part of me I pay a therapist to uninstall whispers: *street you grew up on, first school, check.* Her first car was a '98 Corolla named Gerald — *check.* First concert, Fall Out Boy, still has the shirt — *check.* Her birthday's June 14th, Gemini, "which explains a lot" — *check.* I'm screaming internally, the delighted horror of a man watching someone leave their keys, wallet, and diary on a park bench. But she's also *funny*, doing a bit about her mom's side of the family, the Delgados — maiden name, that's a full house — and I'm smiling like an idiot, and the voice goes quiet. Maybe this is what my therapist means by *presence*. Then Biscuit comes up again — the dog, in a Halloween costume, a hot dog, obviously — and she pulls out her phone and unlocks it. Down the left column. Across the bottom. An L. She does it slowly, absently, the way you do a thing you've done ten thousand times. The smudge trail glows in the candlelight like a landing strip. Before my better judgment can reach the microphone, my mouth says, "Your unlock pattern is an L." Her thumb stops. "What?" "Nothing. I just—" Options scroll past like a log file. Deny. Deflect. Fake a seizure. "You start top-left, go down, then across. It's the most common pattern there is. I could read the smudge from here. You should change it." Beat. "Not that I was *trying* to. I just see things." The silence resolves like a counterspell. The next table is definitely listening. Fair. I would too. Some part of me wants to double down — explain smudge attacks, cite statistics, really commit — which is how arsonists put out fires. "I used to be a hacker," I say instead, because apparently we're doing this now, over burrata, on a first date. "Reformed. White hat. Companies pay me to think like a criminal so criminals don't get to. It's a reflex — the exits, the cameras, your phone. I haven't touched anything that wasn't mine in six years. I just read the world like it's full of typos." Long look. Control player, I think wildly. She's letting the silence sit. "So you were staring at my hands." "When you say it like that—" "Creepier or less creepy than if you were trying?" "Less?" Half a laugh. I'll take it; I'll topdeck that and call it a win. And here's the play that either saves the game or gets me disqualified: I lean in and say, "Think of it as a friendly stranger responsibly disclosing a vulnerability. There's a bounty for this bug. The bounty is dessert. I'm buying." She studies me. Then, without breaking eye contact, she wipes the phone on her napkin and redraws the pattern — slow, deliberate, some zigzag thing with a diagonal. "Rate that one." "Better. You used the center dot twice, which is an illegal move. Judge." "Judge," she agrees, grinning, and fixes it, and the board state swings back. Over the tiramisu I'm literally paying for as a bug bounty, she says, "Okay. What else did you notice?" Danger. Untapped mana across the table. I could lie. Instead: "Biscuit. Larchmont Avenue. Rosa Parks Elementary. Gerald the Corolla. June fourteenth. Delgado." I watch her do the math, eyebrows climbing. "You've narrated every security question your bank has ever asked. I didn't collect them. You *dealt* them to me." "That's terrifying." Still smiling, though. Mostly. "Yeah. So here's what I can do instead." I mean it more than anything I've said all night. "I can forget them. On purpose. Gerald the Corolla is about to take years off my life. It's the closest thing to a vow I know how to make." She walks me to the train, which is either a good sign or she doesn't trust me near her neighborhood — honestly, respect. At the platform: "Same time next week? I'll tell you my blood type and my childhood best friend's nickname." "I already narrowed your blood type to two," I say, which isn't true, but it gets the full laugh this time. On the ride home I think about her new pattern, which I did not let myself memorize. Trust, I'm learning, is the one system I don't want a way into. Some vulnerabilities you don't patch. You just decide not to run the exploit.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in tackling a complex narrative challenge. The author successfully maintains the hacker protagonist's voice throughout, using internal monologue effectively to reveal both technical expertise and personal vulnerability. The Magic: The Gathering metaphors are particularly well-integrated, serving as both character development and structural device rather than mere window dressing. The characterization is nuanced and believable. The protagonist's compulsive pattern recognition feels authentic, and their struggle between old habits and genuine human connection creates compelling internal tension. Maya emerges as more than a plot device - her control player tendencies and measured responses feel consistent and real. The dialogue crackles with wit and authenticity, particularly in the exchanges about the unlock pattern and the "bug bounty" joke. The prose strikes an excellent balance between technical precision and emotional resonance. Phrases like "Some deprecated subsystem in my skull lights up" and "I read the world like it's full of typos" capture the protagonist's mindset perfectly. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining vivid imagery and metaphorical richness. The story's structure is tight and purposeful. The setup efficiently establishes the character's background and current struggle, the conflict emerges naturally from established traits, and the resolution feels earned rather than forced. The ending's cautious optimism works because it acknowledges the ongoing nature of the protagonist's challenge rather than suggesting a complete transformation. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the technical metaphors feel slightly forced, and some security details that might not hold up to expert scrutiny. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness of the piece. The emotional complexity is particularly strong - the protagonist's simultaneous horror and delight at Maya's security vulnerabilities, their genuine attraction warring with ingrained habits, and their ultimate choice to "not run the exploit" creates a satisfying character arc within the constrained timeframe. + First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.7
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Overthinking It The projector squeaks on slide six, and slide six is the one that rearranges my life, because slide six is the partner list. "Maya, you're with Marcus," Mrs. Park says, and clicks forward. *Squeak.* "Balloon cars. Newton's third law. Materials tray at the front." Be normal, is my first thought, which is already a bad sign. People being normal never have to think it. Marcus Webb. Who I have liked since October in a way I have confessed to exactly one person — Priya, sworn, blood oath — and now we're a "we," for two class periods, building a car out of straws, and everything has to seem accidental, because effort is the tell. The goal is to act like I have never once imagined this moment, which I have imagined four hundred times. Here's what nobody tells you about being like me: inside, there's a whole person. She's funny. She talks half a second after the other person finishes, which is apparently when you're supposed to. She has charming, lightly teasing opinions about balloon cars. She is, honestly, a delight. The problem is the interface. Somewhere between her and my mouth there's a checkpoint where everything gets stopped and searched, and by the time a sentence clears, the moment has died of old age. I carry my tray across the room monitoring: am I walking weird? I'm thinking about my arms, and now my arms know, and they've forgotten their job. I arrange my face into "neutral collaborator" and immediately can't tell if it reads as "furious." Marcus is sketching in the corner of his notebook — little rockets, little machines — which I know because he once left it open, not because I study him. I don't study him. I notice things. Noticing is a skill; they put it on report cards. "Hey," I say. Rehearsed. Normal pitch. We're off to a reasonable start, says the girl in my head, who remains somehow optimistic. *Squeak.* Slide seven: the rubric. I have three openers prepared, because I had a feeling about the partner list, and my feelings being occasionally right is worse than never right, because it keeps me listening. Opener A: "So. Balloon cars." Casual, shared mission. Problem: it's the conversational equivalent of pointing at a horse and saying *horse.* Opener B: "I bet we can beat everyone." Confident. Problem: I have never beaten anyone at anything. I lost the sixth-grade spelling bee on *familiar* — I said it right, panicked, and redid it wrong — so my competitive trash talk would land like a ransom note. Opener C: "Do you think the wheels matter more, or the balloon?" A real question; shows interest in his brain. Problem: what if he says "dunno," and the question lies on the table between us like a dead thing, and it's mine, I have to dispose of it. While I decide, Marcus says, "So I guess we're building a car," and all three evaporate. He's used up the topic. The checkpoint has nothing to clear. "Yeah," I say. Yeah. One syllable. I contain essays — I wrote two pages on the ending of *The Giver* for fun, unassigned, for no one — and what Marcus Webb gets is yeah. Replays available on request: "Yeah, this'll be fun" (desperate). "Yeah, hope you can engineer" (who talks like that). "Do you want to cut or tape?" he asks. This is not a hard question. I still need a moment, because cutting means my hands will be visible and employed, and taping means leaning in close over the chassis near his head. "Cut," I say, because it lets me sit back, and then I worry "cut" sounded curt, like I'd rejected tape and, by extension, him, and I nearly add "but tape is also good," which no human has ever said, and I don't say it, and I'm proud. That's the level I operate at: proud of not saying the demented thing. *Squeak.* Slide eight: timeline. Forty minutes today, forty tomorrow. A tomorrow. Terrifying. Also — fine, okay — good. We work. Our hands do not touch, and I am aware of that the entire time, tracking his hands like air traffic control, and when we both reach for the tape I recoil so fast it's like the tape bit me, and now it's a thing, the tape is charged, and I say "sorry," out loud, to no one, to the concept, and Marcus says "go ahead," and I don't know if he means the tape or life. He slides the good scissors toward me without looking up, which I record as evidence of — nothing. It's scissors. Data I keep: he glanced at me twice. Hypothesis: he was looking at the window behind me. Alternative hypothesis: something on my face. I checked with my hand, casually, a full-face casual scratch, like a normal person. Then he test-inflates the balloon and it escapes and does the noise — the long, dying, deeply personal noise — and before the checkpoint can stop it I say, "It's shy," which isn't even a joke, it's barely English, and Marcus laughs. Actually laughs. "Then it's gonna love being taped to a car," he says, and I laugh too, a real one, and immediately audit it (too loud? donkey?) until I realize the audit is stealing the moment — the actual moment, happening now, while it's still warm. Conclusion, pending more data: maybe okay. By cleanup I'm tired in a way that has nothing to do with sleep. It's the double shift: doing everything, and watching myself do it. *Squeak.* Slide nine. At the bell Marcus says, "Same time tomorrow," like we're people with a plan, and I say "Yeah" — again, yeah — but I add, "I'll bring more straws," which is nothing, it's straws, and he grins like it counts. In my head, all the way out, I say brilliant things to him, perfectly timed, one after another, a highlight reel for an empty theater. Behind me the projector squeaks. I know exactly how it feels: everything good coming out with a noise attached.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in capturing the authentic voice of an anxious 8th-grade girl through internal monologue. The writer successfully creates a believable character whose intelligence shines through her overthinking, while her social anxiety creates the central tension between her articulate inner voice and her stilted external expression. The adherence to instructions is nearly perfect - the narrative is almost entirely internal monologue, focuses on the science project pairing, and authentically portrays the exhausting cycle of rehearsed conversations and self-monitoring. The character's voice remains consistently that of a bright teenager, with age-appropriate observations like "effort is the tell" and "I contain essays." The prose is elegant and precise, with memorable phrases like "somewhere between her and my mouth there's a checkpoint" and "the audit is stealing the moment." The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining sophistication. The metaphors feel natural and enhance rather than distract from the narrative. Character development is nuanced - the protagonist is neither purely sympathetic nor frustratingly passive. Her self-awareness about her condition creates complexity, and her small victory at the end feels earned rather than artificially positive. The gap between her internal eloquence and external "yeah" responses is painfully authentic. The pacing is controlled, building tension through the class period structure marked by the squeaking projector slides. This device provides both temporal anchoring and symbolic resonance. The dialogue, though minimal, serves the story's purpose of highlighting the protagonist's communication struggles. The emotional engagement is high due to the universality of social anxiety and the specificity of the 8th-grade experience. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - a small moment of connection that doesn't magically cure her anxiety but offers genuine hope. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly too polished for a teenager's thoughts, though this is minimal. The piece maintains coherence throughout and avoids meandering despite its stream-of-consciousness style. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 90.2
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Rehearsing Rejection Eleven percent. I've been staring at the battery icon like it owes me money, like if I watch it hard enough it'll climb back up out of spite. Charger's at home. Home is forty minutes back. Mara said seven, it's six-twenty, and I've already had the whole conversation twice, the one where she ends it. Version three: She sits down and doesn't touch me—that's the tell—and she does the smile, the closed one, the work one, and says, *"I'm really glad we're doing this."* Which means the opposite. Which means this is a kindness appointment. Which means she's here to do it gently, in person, because she's decent. God, she's so decent. That's the worst thing about— No. Focus. Order of operations. She'll say, *"I've been thinking."* That's how it starts. Nobody's ever been thinking anything good. And I'll say—dignified version: "I appreciate your honesty." Practiced it in the mirror, kept my whole face still, didn't even blink. Devastating version: "Wow. Three weeks to come up with that? I'd have ghosted me for free." Honest version, the one I'll never say out loud: *please don't, I'll be better, I'll be less, I'll be whatever the distance was about, just—* The bus is late. Nine percent. I've drafted and deleted "no worries if you need to bail!" four times now. Bait. She'd take it or she wouldn't, and either way I'd get data. I pull up the thread instead. Her last text, Thursday: *"Can't wait to see you!!"* Two exclamation points. I've read this message maybe eighty times. The first ten, it was a life raft. Somewhere around forty it started looking like overcompensation. Nobody double-exclamations a true thing. Or everybody does. I asked my sister once, casually, how many exclamation points she uses when she means it, and she looked at me like I was on fire. Okay, version four, the one where she says the actual thing, the thing under the thing: *"You know I love you, but it's a lot. You're a lot."* And I've got the response loaded—I've had it loaded for years, it works on anyone: *"You're a lot."* I'd laugh. "God, you people. You want me until I'm real. Everybody wants the trailer. Nobody can sit through the movie." That's good. That's really good. I almost hope she says it so I can— And there's the cold water, right there. *I almost hope.* Dr. Feld has a phrase for this. Rehearsing rejection. She says I script funerals for living relationships, grieve things that haven't happened as practice for things that might. Check the facts, she says. Fact: Mara texted first, twice this week. Fact: she picked the place, the place with the corner table I like. Fact: she said *can't wait.* And then my brain, helpful as a knife: fact, she took three days to answer you Tuesday. Fact: she was different after. After. Three weeks since we crossed that particular street, and she's been quiet. Quieter. Fact: people leave—that one's got citations going back to—anyway. Seven percent. So fine. If she's ending it, I don't need her. List it out. She's a coward about feelings, always has been—she says "I'm busy" the way other people lock doors. She laughs when she's uncomfortable, that awful little *ha,* and she'd rather manage me than know me, and honestly the kissing was—I mean, it was fine, it was— Oh my god. I can't even do it. I can't even lie about the kissing. I'm standing at a bus stop in the cold taking back things I said inside my own head. Look what she does to me. Look what *I* do to me. The bus comes. I take the window seat and ration the phone like oxygen: screen off, low power mode, one check per stop, like a nun counting beads. Six. Five. If it dies I have no map home, no sister, no receipts. I've been meaning to screenshot the *can't wait* message for three days—for the folder, the one I open at 2 a.m., the one with no name. Proof of life. I screenshot it now and watch the battery drop to four for my trouble and think: *there. When I stop believing it happened, at least the phone will remember it happened.* She's already there when I arrive. Corner table—she got the corner table—and she's standing before I'm fully through the door, and hugging me before I've decided whether hugging is happening, and it's a long one, a real one, and the relief is so big it's humiliating. It's oxygen. I want to crawl inside it and live there. I want to shove it off me and check it for wires. "I got you the thing," she says. The chai, the one with the—she remembered. "I missed you," she says. "Work ate me alive, I'm so sorry I went quiet. You know it wasn't you, right? It was never you." And here it is, the split second, the fork: her face is wide open, nothing in it is lying, I *know* this face, I keep a whole archive of this face. *It wasn't you.* She said the exact right sentence. She said the exact right sentence like someone who'd read up on me. Like someone managing the patient. Like someone who loves me. All three at once. Every one of them airtight. Three percent. "Missed you too," I say, and my voice only cracks a little. Under the table I press the side button and watch the screen stay black. Dead. No exit, no receipts, no folder. Just her, across the table, talking about next month—"we should finally do the coast, before it gets cold"—*next month,* she's planning next month like I'm going to be in it. I'll build the case against that sentence tonight. I know me. I'll lose and I'll win and both will take hours. But right now—battery gone, nothing between me and the evidence—I let it be true. I just let it be true.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of borderline personality disorder and the internal experience of anticipatory rejection. The author successfully creates an authentic first-person narrative that captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone caught between desperate need for connection and protective self-sabotage. The structure is particularly effective, using the declining phone battery as both a practical concern and metaphor for the narrator's emotional depletion. The "versions" of imagined conversations show how the mind rehearses rejection scenarios, while the actual interaction reveals the gap between perception and reality. The writing captures the cognitive dissonance beautifully - how validation is simultaneously craved and immediately questioned. The voice is consistently authentic throughout, with natural speech patterns and thought processes that feel genuine rather than clinical. The author avoids the trap of making the character too self-aware while still showing moments of recognition ("Rehearsing rejection. She says I script funerals for living relationships"). The internal monologue flows naturally between anxiety, defensive anger, desperate hope, and brief moments of clarity. The imagery is subtle but effective - the battery percentage creating urgency, the "folder with no name" suggesting a collection of saved evidence of love, the metaphor of rationing phone checks "like a nun counting beads." These details feel organic rather than forced. The ending strikes an appropriate balance. The narrator doesn't suddenly overcome their patterns, but finds a moment of allowing themselves to accept love without immediately dismantling it. This feels earned rather than artificially positive - it's a small victory that acknowledges the ongoing struggle. The piece successfully shows rather than tells the experience of BPD, letting readers feel the exhausting cycle of thoughts rather than simply describing it. The emotional complexity is maintained throughout without becoming overwrought or melodramatic. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 19 Overall Impression: 18
