+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Offworlder ## Chapter Four: The Doorless The escort drank their noon tea, and Ilessa Ro went down the service stair with her hood up, out of the Residence and into the city of Anshar — against protocol, against advice, and, she suspected, against her own better judgment. The city was built of a rose-grey stone that drank the thin light. Every wall was carved. The briefings had mentioned this so drily she had skimmed it: the founders of Hesper had cut old Earth into the new world's bones. On the ministry wall, a tower wound upward in courses of tiny straining figures — Babel, unfinished, forever unfinished. On the water gate, an ark, its door carved shut. Over the market arch, a chained man offering a coal of fire. And on the oldest wall of all, worn to a shine, two figures joined at the spine and split by a single chisel line. The stone was palest where their hands reached back toward one another. Generations had touched it there. The market under the arch was loud with a dialect her briefing tapes had not taught her. Delveborn porters moved through the crowd built like the stones they shouldered, broad and deep-chested, up from the rift where the air lay thick; every few steps they stopped to breathe the plateau's thinness. Spireborn clerks went past them tall as doorframes, long-ribbed, breathing easily the air their grandparents' grandparents had been shaped by. *Two peoples*, said the briefing, *kept in balance by the Covenant of Lineages, each to its district, each to its trade, harmonious and separate.* She watched a delveborn woman sell lentils to a spireborn man who called her by name and asked after her mother. She watched a spireborn knife-seller sharpen a blade for a rift boy and cuff him fondly. *Separate*, she thought, *the way soup is separate.* Then the crowd shifted around someone who was neither. A girl of perhaps twelve — tall but not tall enough, broad but not broad enough, with no lineage braid and no glyph-mark on her cheek. The stallkeepers moved without seeming to move, and the girl was suddenly behind a pyramid of gourds; a moment later two men in the magistrate's grey came through the arch asking after the doorless, and no one in the market had seen any doorless, not today, not ever. A fruit-seller pressed a yellow fruit into Ilessa's hand. "Eat, offworlder," the woman said, delveborn, with a grin that had no politics in it and all politics in it. "You look like you came a very long way to be lied to." She ate it walking. It tasted like the idea of an apple. --- Commander Izar's voice came down from the *Sower* flat with distance and flatter with displeasure. "You were seen leaving the Residence alone. You were seen in the lower market. Protocol exists, Envoy, so that when you vanish, we know whose door to break down." "Yes, Commander." "Don't agree with me; it's insubordinate." A pause. "Your first posting, and you've walked yourself into an audience. Minister Kesh's office sent a note an hour ago requesting — I quote — *the envoy who walks*. Minister of Science. A political appointment; she's never been near a laboratory. She keeps the Registry of Lineages, which makes her the most dangerous person on this plateau. You'll attend her tomorrow. Do not make a method of embarrassing us into progress." --- The Ministry of Science held no instruments. It held shelves, floor to ceiling, the lineage books in grey cloth, and a great carved door — the ark again, shut — behind which, Ilessa supposed, the counting was kept. Minister Odra Kesh was old and spireborn and small for her kind, with eyes the color of distance. "Sit, offworlder. You walked my city without escort. We have not decided whether that was brave or stupid. I will decide tonight." "What would make it brave?" "If you saw anything." The Minister's mouth moved; not quite a smile. "Your briefing says two peoples, separate and harmonious. You were in my market three hours. Are my briefers liars?" "They are gardeners," Ilessa said. "They prune the truth to fit the page." "Careful. I water those gardeners." Odra turned a ring on her finger. "I am Minister of Science and have measured nothing in my life but loyalty. On Hesper, science is the census of kinds. I keep the Book. I decide what is human. You came looking for a laboratory and found a threshing floor. So. Tell me what you saw that was not in my briefing." Ilessa considered lying and set it aside, as one sets aside a dull knife. "I saw a girl who is not in your Book. And I saw thirty people hide her from your magistrates, and then go back to selling lentils." "The doorless." Odra said the word the way one touches a bruise. "Crossborn. The Covenant's arithmetic error. If I open the Book to them, Envoy, both lineages must admit the wall between them was never a law of nature. Only a habit. Would you have me bring down four hundred years of peace to be kind to one beggar child?" "The carving on your oldest wall," Ilessa said. "Two figures split apart, reaching back. The stone is worn where the hands meet. On Earth, that story is a joke told at dinners. Here your people touch it for luck. A census can count kinds, Minister. It cannot count longing." For the first time, Odra looked at her rather than through her. "I could send you home for yesterday's walk. One word. Your Concord would post me a man who reads briefings." "Then you would have an envoy who has seen your briefings. Today you have one who has seen your city. Choose which you'd rather negotiate with." Silence. The thin wind moved against the carved door. "You'll do," Odra Kesh said at last. "Walk tomorrow. I will inform your Commander I requested it. Protocol is a harness, Envoy — useful on a horse. On a mind, it rubs." She rose, ending it. "Ask your Command what the Concord does when a Book has no page for a person. I have asked my Book that question for forty years. It has not answered. Perhaps yours will." Going out, Ilessa passed the oldest wall. She put her fingers to the pale, worn place where the two carved hands reached, and the stone, having held the sun all day, was warm.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Ursula K. Le Guin's literary style, successfully capturing her characteristic blend of anthropological observation, political complexity, and understated prose. The author has created a compelling science fiction scenario that explores themes of identity, classification, and social control through the lens of human subspeciation. The worldbuilding is particularly strong, with the rose-grey carved city of Anshar feeling both alien and familiar. The religious/mythological imagery carved into the walls (Babel, the ark, Prometheus, the divided figures) creates layers of meaning that resonate with the central conflict about human division and unity. The physical descriptions of the Delveborn and Spireborn feel naturally evolved rather than artificially constructed. The protagonist Ilessa Ro is well-characterized through her actions and observations rather than exposition. Her decision to break protocol feels authentic to her role as a diplomatic envoy who recognizes the limitations of official briefings. Her voice remains consistent throughout - observant, thoughtful, and diplomatically astute without being overly clever. The dialogue, particularly in the confrontation with Minister Kesh, crackles with political tension and subtext. Kesh emerges as a formidable antagonist who is neither villain nor hero, but a complex political operator caught between pragmatism and conscience. The exchange feels like a genuine battle of wits where both characters are testing each other. The prose style successfully emulates Le Guin's spare elegance, avoiding purple prose while maintaining poetic moments ("It tasted like the idea of an apple," "the stone, having held the sun all day, was warm"). The pacing moves efficiently between observation, action, and dialogue without meandering. The central metaphor of the "doorless" - those who don't fit the official categories - provides both political tension and emotional resonance. The piece avoids easy answers while suggesting hope through small acts of human connection and understanding. Minor weaknesses include some slightly convenient plot elements (the fruit-seller's pointed comment, the timing of various encounters) and the ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat optimistic given the established political realities. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Eleven — Spit to Sign The thermocycler was the only machine in the lab that Sorrel apologized to. "Hold," she told it, as the lights dipped. The rudimentary solar rig on the roof—eleven mismatched panels, one crazed into a star where a branch had fallen—fed a charge controller salvaged from a drowned golf cart, and the voltage was never stable. It sagged and spiked with every passing cloud, and the cycler's display flickered, held, flickered. Inside its aluminum block, forty microliters of polymerase and prayer were working through cycle thirty on the last of the winter rye library. Every season the fragments came back shorter. Nothing over six hundred base pairs amplified anymore. The old genomes were coming apart like wet paper, which was what you got when the people who wrote them had built them not to last. GoldenSheaf Genomics had honored all its warranties, even ten years dead. One generation per seed line, per the license. Last spring, Glean's whole north field had come up green and gorgeous and set sterile heads—four hundred acres of nothing—and Sorrel had watched grown men kneel in it. So she didn't tell Tare no. She told him his price was insulting, which was different. The broker filled her taped doorway in filter-mask chic, clutching the little sequencer-reader that was the trade's only notary. No handshakes in the black market. You signed with what you were. "Client's the Cannery," he said. "They want Hearth-9 winter barley, Terminus-grade, cracked and breeding true. Four hundred seed units by first frost. Paying two hundred liters of methanol, a minus-twenty compressor coil that holds temp, and six vials of real cipro." "Plus their archive index." "Redacted index. Final." He set the sample on her bench: a vacuum brick in corporate gray, the GoldenSheaf logo still smug on the foil. The cold chain had died years ago. She could feel the degradation in it, embryos going to compost at the molecular level. "Give me an hour," she said. "If there's nothing left to save, there's no contract." She cracked the brick under the hood. Half the embryos went into germination wash she'd reverse-engineered years back—GoldenSheaf seed needed a proprietary activation rinse, or the embryos killed themselves in the hull, apoptosis as DRM. The other half went into lysis buffer, then into the centrifuge she'd built around a quadcopter motor, which whined like a grieving insect and spun true. She loaded the salvaged nanopore rig, ran it off a phone battery, and started sequence threading while the cycler worked the germination assay. Then the clouds came in off the dead coast and the whole rig sagged. The cycler chirped its fault warning. Sorrel swore, clipped the bike trainer into the bus bar, and climbed on. She pedaled the voltage back to eleven point nine and held it there, thighs burning, sweat slicking her spine, while cycles thirty-one through forty ran their annealing steps. The sweat came easy. Everything about her ran hot since the repair therapy—the *radiodurans* construct some clinic in the Spine had sold her when the burn-pit years caught up. Untested. Illegal in three countries that no longer existed. It was killing her, probably, on some schedule she couldn't read. The furnace metabolism meant she ate like two people. Once a month she did the inventory: fingers along her jaw, the orchard of lymph nodes, checking for anything that had decided growing was its whole job. But. Burns knit in days now, pale and glassy. She'd stopped fearing the pit air. And the mycotoxin suite they'd patched into her liver—while the booster lasted—meant she could eat the gray flatbread that would drop anyone else in Glean, tasting the aflatoxin like pennies while her enzymes stripped it clean. Nothing lasted. The old world built nothing to last, and she was old-world work now too, her own medicine fading on a schedule, obsolescence written into the cure. Every re-dose brought the serum weather: chills, then fever, then the brief golden month. The nanopore finished first. She read it dripping, still astride the bike. Hearth-9's food genes were clean. The Terminus lock was the standard recombinase snare she knew how to cut. And then, quiet between the lock and the terminator, sat a second cassette—an expression payload she'd seen once before in a poppy. Grain as pharmacy. The Cannery didn't want bread. It wanted a harvest you could chew to stop hurting. And of course they wanted the lock left intact. A drug that bred true would propagate itself into worthlessness. Built-in obsolescence wasn't a flaw, to the Cannery. It was the business model. Sorrel sat with it a while, watching the cycler's light steady as the clouds moved on. She could refuse. But Glean needed the methanol, the cipro, the coil. And the north field needed seed that held. So she would honor the contract to the letter—four hundred units, locked, payload and all, spit-sealed and delivered. And in the margins of the work, in the waste stream the Cannery would never audit, she would strip a second line. No payload. No lock. Open-pollinated. The one sin the old world had hanged people for: making a thing that lasted. She spat in Tare's tube. His reader sequenced her, matched her to her reputation, chirped. "Contract live," he said. That night, in gutter trays under the flickering light, the first Hearth-9 embryos greened like commas at the ends of sentences. And in the warm dark of her gut, in the engineered flora where she kept her master library—the only vault whose power never failed—the clean line waited to be written.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong technical writing and worldbuilding within the specified parameters. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse, centering the narrative around biohacker Sorrel and her black market contract work. The worldbuilding excels through specific technical details that feel authentic to the genre. The jury-rigged laboratory equipment is convincingly portrayed - from the thermocycler powered by mismatched solar panels to the centrifuge built around a quadcopter motor. The scientific terminology (polymerase chain reaction, nanopore sequencing, recombinase snares) is deployed accurately without becoming overly technical for general readers. Sorrel emerges as a well-developed protagonist with clear motivations and internal conflicts. Her relationship with her deteriorating equipment shows personality through small details like apologizing to the thermocycler. The physical modifications she's undergone (radiodurans construct, mycotoxin resistance) create compelling body horror elements while serving practical plot functions. Her monthly self-examinations for tumors and the "serum weather" of re-dosing add visceral stakes to her situation. The prose maintains consistent voice throughout, balancing technical precision with poetic moments. Phrases like "embryos going to compost at the molecular level" and "greened like commas at the ends of sentences" demonstrate skillful metaphorical language without becoming overwrought. The writing flows naturally between action, exposition, and character development. The black market contract provides solid plot structure, with clear stakes and moral complexity. Sorrel's decision to honor the contract while secretly creating an open-pollinated line adds ethical dimension and shows character agency. The revelation that the grain contains pharmaceutical properties rather than just nutrition creates an effective plot twist that recontextualizes the entire negotiation. However, some elements feel slightly underdeveloped. The dialogue, while functional, lacks distinctive character voice - Tare speaks in fairly generic broker terminology. The ending, while thematically appropriate, resolves perhaps too neatly with Sorrel's plan to subvert the system. The emotional stakes, while present, could be more deeply explored - we understand Sorrel's situation intellectually but don't fully feel her desperation or hope. The piece succeeds admirably at the technical aspects of biopunk worldbuilding and maintains narrative coherence throughout. The scientific elements feel researched and plausible, and the post-apocalyptic setting is convincingly rendered through accumulated detail rather than exposition dumps. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 84.0
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 84.8
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Azra Gambit ## Chapter Seven: The Diplomats Arthur Neegan adjusted his collar for the fortieth time since leaving Mars and wondered, not for the first time, what exactly he had gotten himself into. The courtesy launch was a small ship done up like a state occasion: carpet in the acceleration couches, a steward in white gloves, brass-rimmed chronometers bolted to bulkheads of polished mahogany. Through the visiplate, Mars receded—a rust-orange crescent, the dark gash of the Valles like a healing scar. Somewhere down there was Plot 1147, Margaritifer Terra. His dome. His smelter. His cat. The suit they'd instructed him to wear—high band collar, Registry-cut, bought for claim hearings and worn twice—itched like a guilty conscience. Three days earlier, the lighter had come down through the pink noon sky and settled on his lease in a blast of dust that flayed the south solar array. Six men stepped out in dove-gray uniforms, sidearms worn low and burnished bright, the white star-and-chevron of the Antares Diplomatic Corps on their shoulders. Diplomatic in name only, went the saying in Bradbury Township: the Corps that negotiated with fleets. Their captain had saluted—actually saluted—and handed him an envelope of real paper. *The Commissioner requests the pleasure of your company.* It was a request the way a landslide is a request. One bag, one message to the township office, no questions. The neighbors' domes had been shuttered tight. He didn't blame them. "Docking in two minutes, sir," said the steward, as though announcing soup. Their host swung into the visiplate, and Arthur's engineering eye took measurements before his fear could: a kilometer of dark hull, gun blisters faired over but unmistakable, antennae in military spacing. A diplomatic packet the way a tiger is a housecat. He felt the grapples clang through his boots. They brought him to a cabin absurd with wood—actual wood, a ton of it hauled light-years—where a globe of Mars turned slowly beneath a brass lamp. One small dot of light shone in Margaritifer Terra. His light. The cabin smelled of beeswax, tobacco, and the flat cold air all warships breathe. The man waiting there rose. Gray-haired, soft around the middle, with the patient face of a career administrator, he held a cigarette in a long black holder, old-fashioned as a museum piece. "Mr. Neegan. I am Aldan Quist, Deputy Commissioner of the Corps. Forgive the manner of your invitation." He gestured to a chair. "A show of force, honestly presented, is more courteous than a knife in a friendly hand. It sets expectations. Brandy? It's Venusian." "I'd rather have answers." "Better still." Quist poured anyway. "Let me tell you what you've found, since you've been too modest to say it aloud. Your filed assay reports a workable trace of Azra. Your true assay—do sit down—describes the largest contiguous body of the metal on surveyed Mars. Enough, refined, to build drive cores for four hundred capital ships. Or to do the other thing. The thing no one puts in writing." Arthur sat. "My assays are sealed under Registry law." "There is nothing sealed to a patient man, Mr. Neegan. Only expensive." Quist drew on the holder. "Consider the board on which you sit. Terra's Atomic Commission holds the refinement charters. The Combine holds the Martian leases, and the indenture contracts that dig them. We of Antares hold the fleets—and, at present, a shortage. Azra accelerates decay; decay drives ships and ends them. Every gram is a chess piece. And you, a mining engineer with one smelter and a cat, are sitting on the queen." "You haven't said what you want." "Haven't I? We want your signature." Quist slid a slim folder across the wood—paper again, an extravagance louder than the warship outside. "Terms. Antares purchases your lease at forty times the Charter rate. You retain title as director of extraction; your name stays on the claim, because the Charter requires a Terran citizen's name on Terran soil. Your crews' indentures are bought out and torn up—every man on your plot goes free. You receive citizenship, an estate on Seyfert's World, and the protection of a technical assistance mission." "Soldiers." "Advisors," said Quist pleasantly. "With rifles." Arthur turned the folder without opening it. "You've a kilometer of warship out there. Why do you need my name on anything?" "Ah." Quist smiled, and for the first time Arthur glimpsed the man inside the administrator. "Because law is the cheapest army ever fielded. A clean title, signed and witnessed, lets Antares land openly and dare the Combine to object. A stolen one gives Terra a casus belli and every rival a flag to rally to. Empires are built of paper, Mr. Neegan. Battleships are merely the ink." "And if I refuse?" "Then in six months the Combine's survey sats confirm what we already know. The indenture drafts triple. Plot 1147 becomes a company town with a fence around it. And you—" Quist tapped ash into a brass tray "—become a man holding leverage and no fleet. Such men meet accidents. Ask the Belt prospectors of '38, if you can find one." He let that sit. On the globe, the little light turned slowly, bright as a warning beacon. "One more thing. You are an engineer; you'll have wondered why the rush. Azra catalyzes decay—small masses, small miracles. But past a certain mass, in a certain geometry, it stops being a catalyst. It becomes something else. Our laboratories have a word for it. The word is classified. Your vein is past that mass." "Resonance cascade," Arthur said. "That's your classified word, or near enough. I've run the figures on my own core samples." Quist's pause was barely a pause at all. "Then you understand why the Corps dislikes waiting. Terra has people—sensible, frightened people—who would sooner fuse that whole seam into glass than see it dug up by anyone. Including you." Arthur thought of his claim stakes, his smelter, the cat asleep on a bunk in a dome that suddenly seemed very thin. "Ten days," Quist said, rising. "The launch will see you home. Others will come—the Combine, the Commission, perhaps worse. Hear them out; it's your right. Judge them, though, by whether they knock." Back in the courtesy launch, collar chafing, the folder resting like a slab of armor over his heart, Arthur watched Mars swell in the visiplate. Somewhere below, a thumbprint dome sat atop enough power to burn a civilization—or lift one. He had spent his life believing that a man who owned his plot owned his fate. He had it backward, he saw now. The plot owned him. And it had just been noticed. He adjusted his collar. It didn't help.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Asimovian science fiction conventions and delivers a compelling chapter that effectively captures the requested tone and style. The author successfully creates the "big-and-small-picture worldbuilding" characteristic of Asimov, establishing both intimate personal stakes (Arthur's dome, cat, and simple life) and vast political implications (interstellar empires, weapons of mass destruction, cascading resonance effects). The dialogue is particularly strong, especially Quist's characterization. His speech patterns feel authentically bureaucratic yet menacing, with memorable lines like "law is the cheapest army ever fielded" and "Battleships are merely the ink." The conversation reveals information naturally while maintaining tension, avoiding heavy exposition dumps. The retrofuturistic atmosphere is well-executed through carefully chosen details: brass chronometers, mahogany bulkheads, cigarette holders, and paper documents as luxury items. These elements create the classic sci-fi aesthetic without feeling forced or nostalgic. Arthur's characterization as an everyman caught in forces beyond his control is believable and sympathetic. His engineering background informs his observations and responses credibly. The revelation that he's already calculated the resonance cascade potential adds depth to his character and avoids making him seem naive. The pacing is excellent, building tension through the journey to the meeting, the intimidating ship description, and the gradual revelation of stakes. The political complexity feels genuine - multiple factions with competing interests, legal maneuvering, and the threat of violence underlying diplomatic courtesy. Some minor weaknesses include occasional overwrought metaphors ("itched like a guilty conscience," "bright as a warning beacon") and a few instances where the prose becomes slightly purple. The ending, while thematically appropriate, borders on being too neat in its philosophical summation. The piece successfully plants seeds for larger intrigue while maintaining focus on Arthur's immediate predicament. The worldbuilding feels lived-in and consistent, with economic and political systems that make logical sense within the established parameters. + Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The darkroom at midnight smells like vinegar and warm dust, and Elliot is standing close enough that I can count the safety pins on his collar. Fourteen. I've counted before. Six weeks of this. Our final project — a joint portfolio on thresholds for Experimental Photo, his idea, which should tell you everything — has eaten every Thursday since October, and I've stopped pretending I mind. The building's empty except for us, the ventilation hum, the safelight bleeding everything into shades of rust: his mesh shirt, his washed-out Sisters of Mercy tee, the skirt-that-isn't-quite-a-skirt he wears over Docs like he's daring the world to do the math. He dresses like a rumor. I dress like a warning. The whole program has us figured for opposites, which shows you what the whole program knows. "Agitate," he says, soft, and I rock the tray, because in here he's the expert and I've decided to let him be. His work changed somewhere around week three. Early prints were doorways, stairwells, thresholds literal enough to make Professor Voss write *on the nose, but promising* in the margins. Lately it's self-portraits — long exposures, shot through gauze, his face half-erased, like he's daring the film to catch him. He slides a fresh sheet into the developer and we watch the blank white float. "I got this at the hospice shop on Fifty-Ninth," he says, to the tray, not to me. "The skirt. I told the woman it was for my girlfriend." A breath. "I don't have a girlfriend." "Noted," I say, because the part of my brain that does charm has gone offline, leaving only the part that notices his ears going dark even in red light. "Everyone in this program knows what they are. You especially. You walk into a room like it owes you rent." His hands are steady on the tongs, but barely — there's a tremor at the wrist, the one I'm not supposed to mention. "I keep waiting to feel like a guy. The way you're supposed to. Mostly I feel like a work print. Like I'm not the final version of anything." The enlarger's fan fills the silence. Somewhere a pipe ticks. "I've never said that out loud before," he says. "Tell me if I broke something." Here's what I want to do: cross the eighteen inches between us and solve this the way I solve everything — decisively, with both hands. My whole romantic history is decisive. Women with hard fades and harder stares. Men built like locked doors. People who knew exactly what they were, because certainty has always been my weakness — definition, edges you could cut yourself on. Elliot has no edges tonight. He's all developer fumes and *maybe*, and the terrifying thing — the thing I keep circling like a drain — is that the maybe is the pull. What does that make me? Twenty-five and fluent in my own wanting, until a nineteen-year-old in chipped nail polish says *work print* and every map I've got goes blank. Nineteen. That's the other thing. He isn't fragile — nobody wears that much mesh in November if they're fragile — but what he's handing me is, and I'm not practiced at being handed things. I take. It's honestly most of my reputation. My phone buzzes in my back pocket, silent and insistent, the outside world doing its little dance. I let it go. Whatever it wants can wait for the version of me that exists outside this red room. "You didn't break anything," I say. "For the record." He nods at the tray like I'm the thing developing. And there's this rush in my chest — I'd call it tenderness except it feels too greedy for that. I feel like I'm watching an eclipse: lucky, faintly unworthy, unable to look away. He's becoming something right in front of me, in real time, and I want to witness it so badly it scares me more than any wanting I've ever done. "What do you need?" I ask. First time in my life I've put that question in front of the action instead of after it. It costs me something. I pay it gladly. "This," he says. "Don't be weird. Don't fix it. Just — stay." And then, tentative as a test strip, he leans his shoulder into mine. Like he's metering the light of me. Checking whether I blow out. His sleeve is damp with fixer. His shoulder is warm through my shirt. I, Dani, who has never once waited for permission in my life, hold perfectly still and let him decide the distance. In the tray, his face surfaces from nothing — cheekbones first, then the dark hollows of his eyes, blooming up out of the blank like something that was always there, waiting on the right chemistry. "Can I ask you something?" he says. "Would you still — if I turn out to be—" He stops. There isn't a word for the end of that sentence yet. "Ask me when there's a word," I say. "I'll still be here." He huffs something almost like a laugh. "Bossy," he says, but he says it like a thank-you, and he doesn't move away. Under the safelight you can't see the full spectrum of anything. That's the deal you make in this room: you work in red and trust what's latent. I rock the tray in slow circles, gentle, the way he taught me, because some things you can't rush and some you can't force. You agitate. You wait. You watch what was always there come up out of the white.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craft in several key areas while maintaining strong adherence to the prompt's complex requirements. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive 25-year-old whose usual confidence becomes complicated by her attraction to Elliot's vulnerability and gender questioning. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically. The author weaves photography terminology throughout the narrative in ways that feel organic rather than forced - "work print," "agitate," "metering the light," "what's latent" - creating layers of meaning that enhance rather than distract from the emotional core. The red safelight becomes a perfect metaphor for the liminal space both characters occupy. Character development is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her natural take-charge tendencies and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-observed. The line "First time in my life I've put that question in front of the action instead of after it" effectively captures her growth. Elliot's vulnerability is portrayed with sensitivity - his gender questioning feels genuine rather than performative, and his metaphor of feeling like a "work print" rather than "the final version" is both poignant and thematically consistent. The prose style is confident and distinctive. Sentences like "He dresses like a rumor. I dress like a warning" demonstrate sharp, memorable writing that reveals character through imagery. The author maintains consistent voice throughout, with Dani's observations feeling authentic to her artistic background and assertive personality. The emotional complexity is well-handled. The piece explores attraction that transcends traditional categories, the tenderness of witnessing someone's self-discovery, and the disorientation of having one's own certainties challenged. The age gap is acknowledged without being sensationalized, and the power dynamics are thoughtfully navigated. However, there are some areas where the writing occasionally tips toward the overwrought. Phrases like "every map I've got goes blank" and "I feel like I'm watching an eclipse: lucky, faintly unworthy, unable to look away" border on purple prose, though they generally serve the emotional intensity of the moment. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development well. Elliot's tentative confession and Dani's responses ring true to their respective personalities and the intimate setting. The conversation flows organically from the shared work to deeper revelations. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful without being unrealistically resolved. Both characters remain in transition, which feels honest to the situation and timeframe of the narrative. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 79.3
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma The guidebook said to arrive before eight to avoid the crowds. At half past, Ben was walking backwards away from the Trevi Fountain, nose buried in the chapter on the Acqua Vergine, which was why he didn't see the Vespa. He heard it first — a furious wasp of an engine — and a woman's voice: "Occhio! *OCCHIO!*" The collision was mostly avoidance. The Vespa went down in a hiss of gravel. The courier did not. She was on her feet before he'd finished dropping his book. "*Madonna santa*, ma sei scemo? You walk *backwards*? In Roma? Who walks backwards in Roma?" "I'm so sorry — *mi dispiace*, sono —" His Italian evaporated. He flapped a hand at the scooter, the fountain, the general situation. She pulled off her helmet. Dark curls everywhere; a mouth made for argument. She inspected the scratch on the leg shield, then him, then the book lying open in the fountain spray — and laughed. "You are English." "Is it that obvious?" "You apologise to the street." She righted the Vespa with one heave, retrieved the guidebook, read the title with open contempt. "*Fifty Perfect Days in Rome.* You have been here how long?" "Six weeks." "*Mamma mia.*" She set it on the saddle while she checked her mirrors. Later, Ben could not pinpoint the moment it migrated into her courier satchel. It simply never came back out. "Let me pay for the damage," he said. "I insist—" "You have somewhere to be? A bus tour? A queue of pensioners?" "A chapter to avoid writing." Something shifted behind her eyes — weather coming in. "Dottore." "Professor, technically. Early modern—" "Alessandra." She pointed at the pillion. "Get on." "I'm sorry?" "There it is again. I have three deliveries, then I'm finished. And you, dottore, have the face of a man who *reads* about fountains instead of getting wet." * * * She drove like the city was a sentence she was trying to finish. Ben held her shoulders the way he handled sixteenth-century manuscripts until she shouted "*Hold on or fall off!*" over the wind, and then he held on. Between deliveries — a contract near Piazza Navona, a corsage to a theatre, a set of keys she did not explain — she narrated Rome like a conspiracy: which pope stole which obelisk, which palazzo kept a corpse in its foundations. At Campo de' Fiori she argued cheerfully with a fig vendor, and Ben saw, and immediately un-saw, a small motion of her hand, like a card trick. She turned and pressed a tin medallion into his palm. "San Cristoforo. Patron of travellers." "Did you pay for this?" "Don't be rude, dottore." On the Aventine she bullied her way past a caretaker — officially closed, apparently her cousin's friend — and they emerged onto a loggia above a garden of orange trees, the whole city spread below them like loot. "My sabbatical was supposed to be quiet," Ben said, gripping the railing. "And instead?" "Instead I'm an accessory." She looked at him sidelong, pleased. "You are learning." * * * The storm arrived at four, all at once, the way Rome does everything. They skidded to a stop in Trastevere and crammed under the awning of a gelateria, shoulder to shoulder, watching the street become a river. The gelatiere took one look at Alessandra's wet lashes and produced two cups, refused payment, disappeared. Ben tasted his. "This is the best thing I've ever—" "Stracciatella. I know." Rain drummed the canvas above them. A church bell drowned somewhere. It was very close under the awning; he could smell rain on her jacket, and beneath it, petrol and oranges. "Why did you pick me up?" he asked. "This morning. Honestly." She studied him a long moment, and for once there was no punchline in her face. "You were the only person in Rome standing still," she said. "It was suspicious." "That's not an answer." "No." A grin, the shutter coming down. "Eat your gelato, dottore, before it melts." She stole his glasses to clean them on her shirt, and for the four seconds he stood there — blurred, blind, grinning like an idiot — anything might have happened. Then the glasses were back on his nose, and the moment was folded away with the rain. The gelatiere, he noticed later, was missing a spoon. * * * She dropped him at his pensione near the Pantheon as the streetlamps came on. He climbed off on legs that had forgotten walking. "Alessandra. I don't know how to—" "Say thank you? You English. Everything is a ceremony." She gunned the engine, then called over it, casual as anything: "Check your pockets, dottore." And she was gone into the gold-and-blue evening, taillight winking through the traffic. In his room he emptied his jacket onto the bed: the tin saint. A gelato spoon. A fig, slightly bruised. A lighter that had never been his. And at the bottom, his own guidebook — returned at some point by sleight of hand he hadn't felt. On the back page, in fierce slanting handwriting, she had crossed out the printed list of fifty perfect days and written a single line: *Fifty-one. Call me. — A.* And a number. Ben sat on the bed in his damp clothes, holding the evidence, and began — for the first time in six years, by his own scholarly estimate — to laugh.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge naturally through action and dialogue rather than exposition. Alessandra's kleptomaniac tendencies are woven seamlessly into the narrative through subtle details - the guidebook's migration, the medallion, the spoon - creating intrigue without heavy-handedness. The writing exhibits confident prose with vivid, economical descriptions that capture Rome's atmosphere effectively. Phrases like "drove like the city was a sentence she was trying to finish" and "the whole city spread below them like loot" demonstrate sophisticated metaphorical thinking. The dialogue feels authentic, particularly Alessandra's mix of Italian and English, which establishes her character without becoming gimmicky. The structure works well, using scene breaks to create momentum while maintaining narrative cohesion. The pacing builds naturally from the initial collision to increasingly intimate moments, culminating in a satisfying but open-ended conclusion that feels earned rather than forced. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "opposites attract" dynamic and the transformation of the uptight academic are familiar romance tropes, though executed competently. The kleptomania subplot, while cleverly handled, borders on being too cute. Ben's character, while believable, is less dynamically rendered than Alessandra's. The emotional complexity is present but could be deeper - Alessandra's motivations remain somewhat surface-level, and Ben's attraction, while understandable, develops quite rapidly. The piece succeeds as an engaging opening to a larger work but doesn't quite achieve the emotional depth that would elevate it to exceptional status. The prose occasionally veers toward the overly clever, though it generally maintains restraint. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between resolution and anticipation for future developments. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 87.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**The Last Outpost** *Chapter One: Thick or Thin* The gravity hiccups at 22:14 station time, right on schedule. Every bottle on my back bar shivers in its cradle, and Bram's ale climbs a crescent up the side of his mug before thinking better of it. "Ring three plating again," Bram says. "I told Magda. Twice." "You told her an hour ago." "Then she needs telling a third time. Woman's got a head like a hull plate." It's a slow Tuesday on the Andromeda outer rim. Pil and Sef are in the corner booth fighting about freight rates in the fond, vicious whispers of people married forty years. "—not paying nine percent for the Ferris run, Pil, I'd rather drink vacuum." "You'd rather drink anything. It's your best quality." "Nets," Sef calls over. "Tell him nine percent is robbery." "Nine percent is robbery," I say. "See? She tends bar, she's practically a lawyer." A pair of ore haulers are losing money at tiles. The Last Outpost hums its old, comfortable hum, and I'm halfway through polishing glasses that don't need polishing when the door cycles. The stranger looks like the belt chewed them and spat out the gristle. Tentacled dreads askew — Selathi, by the suckers — one still wearing a luggage tag from a line that hasn't run this far out in years. Coat inside out. Belt dust ground into both knees. They plop down onto a stool. "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." I reach for the blue bottle. "Thick or thin?" "...What?" "Your trouble. Thick, like debt collectors. Thin, like a broken heart. Helps me calibrate the pour." A laugh starts in them and dies halfway. "Thick." "Double it is." I pour. The whisky wobbles against the glass — could be ring three, could be their hand. Both, maybe. "House policy. New faces get the first one free." "Don't listen to her, friend," Bram calls down the bar. "House policy is Nets can't help herself." "House policy is you still owe me for last week." "That's not policy, that's extortion." The stranger almost smiles. The suckers at the tips of their dreads uncurl a fraction, which I've learned to read as a good sign. "Sorren," they say, like a coin laid on the bar. "Nets." "Strange name." "Strange station. Anyone following you, Sorren?" They go very still. The haulers' tile game clatters on, oblivious. "That's a strange question." "It's a frontier bar. It's the only question." Sorren turns the glass in a slow circle. Wobble, settle. Wobble, settle. "I was under contract. Navigation, deep belt. Six years, clean exit clause." A shrug that costs them something. "My contract got sold. The new owners don't believe in exit clauses." "So you walked." "I ran. There's a difference. Legally." "Legally's a long way from here," Bram says, and slides his mug toward me for a refill, which from Bram is practically a welcome speech. Sorren's eyes flick to the wall feed of the docks. "How many ships out today?" "Two. Both scheduled, both logged, both watched." "Right." A swallow. "And the ones that aren't scheduled?" "Get discussed in a different tone of voice." I lean on the bar. "One problem per glass, Sorren." "They'll send someone," they say quietly. "Retrieval specialist. The company calls it asset recovery." The suckers curl tight again. "I'm the asset." Five years ago I came through dock two looking exactly like that, and Magda poured me something blue and asked nothing. I still haven't worked off that debt. Not the money part — the other part. "If anyone asks," Sorren starts— "Nobody asks at the Last Outpost," I say. "It's the whole name of the place." "Almost the whole name," Bram says. Ring three hiccups again; the bottles answer in chorus. "That plating's going to shear a coupling one day," Bram mutters. "Mark my words. Somebody'll be drinking and the whole—" The comm above the bar crackles. "Nets, you on?" Tavi, up in dock control, voice flat with overtime. "Here, Tav." "Got an unscheduled skipper requesting berth at dock two. No manifest filed. Transponder's corporate but scrubbed, which you know I love." A pause, keys clicking. "They're asking if anyone new came aboard today." Sorren freezes. The whisky wobbles in their glass, and for once I can't blame ring three. My thumb hovers over the comm key. Pil and Sef have gone quiet in their booth. Bram is watching me over the rim of his mug, and in his look is the whole history of this place: what we do, what we don't do. "Nobody new, Tav," I say. "Same fossils as always." "Hey," says Bram. "Present company excepted." "Copy that." Static. Then, casually, like a knife slid between ribs: "Berthing them anyway. Skipper says he's thirsty. You've got incoming in about four." The comm clicks off. Somewhere down the spine of the station, a docking clamp engages with a thud I feel in my teeth. The gravity stutters. Every glass on the bar ripples at once. Bram heaves himself up and ambles, whistling, to the stool nearest the door — a stool he has never once sat in. On the wall feed, dock two's inner door begins to cycle. "Drink fast," I tell Sorren, and reach under the bar for the bottle — and for the other thing I keep under the bar.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in establishing a cozy sci-fi atmosphere while building tension effectively. The author successfully creates the requested "cozy" feel through familiar bar interactions and community dynamics, while maintaining the sci-fi setting through well-integrated worldbuilding details like gravity hiccups, ring plating, and species diversity. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - characterization, worldbuilding, and plot advancement. The banter between Nets and Bram establishes their relationship and the bar's atmosphere, while Sorren's conversation reveals backstory efficiently. The author achieves the requested 60% dialogue ratio without it feeling forced. Character work is particularly strong. Nets emerges as competent and protective with hints of her own mysterious past. Her decision to lie to dock control feels earned based on her established values. Bram's subtle shift to the door-side stool shows character through action rather than exposition. Sorren reads as genuinely desperate without being overwrought. The pacing builds tension effectively, starting with routine bar life and escalating through Sorren's arrival to the incoming threat. The conflict is established clearly - Sorren is fleeing corporate "asset recovery" - and left unresolved as requested. The ending creates genuine suspense. Technical execution is solid. The first-person present tense is maintained consistently. Sentences flow well with good rhythm and variety. The prose is clean and purposeful without being overly sparse or purple. Some minor weaknesses include a few slightly predictable elements (the mysterious bartender with a past, the found family protecting newcomers) though these are executed well enough to feel fresh. The worldbuilding, while effective, relies somewhat on familiar space-western tropes. The piece successfully balances multiple tones - cozy community warmth, underlying tension, and building threat - without jarring transitions. The author demonstrates strong control over pacing and atmosphere. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South — Chapter Two: Where Y'at The guy who drove me the last four hundred miles drops me at the corner of Canal and Somewhere, says "good luck, kid" like luck is a thing you can hand off with a half-eaten bag of gas station Combos, and then he's taillights, then he's gone. It's 6 a.m. in New Orleans and my phone is at twelve percent and everything I own fits in one backpack, which feels like its own kind of obituary. Inventory, because lists keep me from screaming: thirty-seven dollars. A charger cord with no wall brick, which is a metaphor. One hoodie, in a city already sweating at dawn. A photo I took off the fridge before I left, because apparently I hate myself. The Quarter at this hour smells like bleach trying its best. A man in an apron is hosing last night off the sidewalk. He catches me staring and goes, "You lost, baby? Everybody is," and laughs like that's the funniest thing he's said all week, and maybe it is. I keep walking. Walking is almost a plan. --- Forty-eight hours ago I told my mother my name at the kitchen table, and she looked at me like I'd set something on fire, and then she said *confused*, that word, that wet cardboard word, and Dad studied his plate like the mashed potatoes had a map in them. The school counsellor's voice on the phone, three weeks back: *concerning behavioural patterns*. Everybody's got a file on me somewhere. Nobody's got a clue. A silver car paces me for half a block. The window hums down. "You need a place to crash, sweetheart?" *Sweetheart.* I want to correct him and I want him to burst into flames and also — God, this is the sick part — some starved little corner of me wants to be sweethearted by literally anybody, even a man with a skull tattoo and a car that smells like fake pine. That's how they get you, probably. The starvation. "Don't get in the car." The voice comes from a doorway. A girl, my age maybe, reading forty years old around the eyes, eating a beignet like it personally offended her. Powdered sugar all over a hoodie that has no business existing in this heat. "Rule one," she says. "Never get in the car." She flicks her eyes at the silver car and something in her face makes the guy decide, suddenly, that he's late for something. The car slides off. "I wasn't going to," I say, which is mostly true. "Sure." She looks me over — backpack, clean sneakers, whole trembling neon *RUNAWAY* sign I must be wearing — and goes, "How long?" "How long what?" "Don't." "Since last night." She laughs, not mean, exactly. "A newborn." --- Her name is Saint. "Like the football team?" I ask, and she says, "Like what my grandma called me right before she'd say *ain't*," and when I give her "Taylor" back I get this whole-body fizz, because she's the first person on Earth who only knows me as that. No before. No file. Just Taylor, walking down Royal Street eating her other beignet because "you were doing the hungry thing where you pretend you're not." Saint has rules and I write them down in the notebook of my skull. Nobody's free food is free. Sleep where there's cameras. Cops aren't your friends but they're not your worst option either. And — she goes flat and strange when she says this one — "If a van pulls up with *Mission* or *Grace* or a dove on the side, you walk away fast. You don't take the sandwich. You don't take the blanket." "How do you—" "You ask how I know, we're done talking." Okay. Filed. She says she's from "around," but twenty minutes earlier she knew that 614 was an Ohio area code, and there's a library book sticking out of her bag with a Memphis stamp on it, and a flip phone in her pocket buzzes twice that morning and both times she looks at it and says "Nobody" and kills it. Saint is a load-bearing wall of secrets. I recognize the architecture. I live in a similar building. --- At two o'clock the sky opens up the way it only does in the South, like someone slit the bottom of the ocean, and we cram under a balcony on Rampart with a street drummer and a soaked tarot reader and watch the street become a river. "My mom texted," I say. I don't know why I say it. I turned the phone on an hour ago against every rule I made: five missed calls, one message. *Taylor please.* Just that. She used the name. It wrecked me worse than the wrong one would have, and I've been carrying that around like a live coal ever since, nine percent battery and a mother who said my name too late or maybe just barely in time. Saint doesn't do comfort. I like her for it. She just watches the rain and says, "You gonna answer?" "I don't know what I am." "Yeah," she says. "That's the whole job, figuring that out." And for one second her hand drifts up to the collar of her hoodie like there's something underneath it she checks on sometimes, the way I check on my own chest in gas station mirrors, and I don't ask, because rule whatever: you don't ask. The drummer starts up again. The tarot reader tells Saint, "You got a long road, chère," and Saint goes, "Charge me when it's over." --- I use my last battery like it's a flare. *im alive. im safe. dont call the police. i need you to mean it about the name. ill call when im ready.* I watch it send. The screen dies in my hand, and I wait for the panic and what comes instead is this awful, guilty, enormous *quiet*. Nobody on the planet can reach me now. It feels almost exactly like free, and I hate that it does, and I love that it does. "There's a place down here," Saint says, zipping her hoodie against the steam. "Showers. Food. They don't call your people if you say don't. Probably." "Probably." "Nothing's a hundred percent, newborn." I don't trust Saint. She's got a Memphis library book and a phone full of Nobody and a flinch shaped like a church van, and for all I know this is the part of the movie where the audience screams at the screen. I follow her anyway. That's the trick of surviving, I'm starting to learn — you don't have to trust anybody. You just have to walk in the same direction for a while, through the rain-washed streets, in a city that doesn't know your name yet. Only the one you give it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and authentic voice work. The author successfully captures Taylor's perspective as a transgender teen runaway with genuine emotional complexity - the conflicting feelings about family contact, the desperate hunger for acceptance, and the simultaneous fear and liberation of anonymity are all convincingly rendered. The prose style is distinctive and effective, employing fragmented thoughts, lists, and metaphorical language that feels natural to the character rather than forced. Lines like "a charger cord with no wall brick, which is a metaphor" and "that wet cardboard word" show creative linguistic flair without becoming overwrought. The mixed tense approach works well, creating immediacy while allowing for reflection. Saint emerges as a compelling secondary character with her own mysterious depth. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters' ages and circumstances, particularly Saint's street-smart pragmatism contrasting with Taylor's raw vulnerability. The "rules" Saint provides create believable world-building around runaway survival. The New Orleans setting is well-utilized without becoming touristy - the early morning cleanup, the sudden rainstorm, and the mix of danger and community all ring true. The pacing moves naturally through Taylor's first day, building the relationship with Saint while maintaining tension about trust and safety. The emotional core remains strong throughout. Taylor's internal conflict about the mother's text using the correct name is particularly well-handled - showing how validation can hurt when it comes too late. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between hope and uncertainty without false optimism. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical language borders on being too clever, and a few instances where the internal monologue could be tightened. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements while creating genuine literary merit. It avoids common pitfalls of YA fiction by treating its characters with respect and complexity rather than simplification. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The plan, as Chewy laid it out, had the twin virtues of simplicity and a complete disregard for everything that had ever happened to them before. "We go to the mall," said the koala. "We take the amoxicillin from the pharmacy for Aggie's tooth. We come back. We speak to no one." Midas studied the map they'd drawn in spilled tea on the potting table. "And if someone speaks to us?" "They won't." Chewy's eyes flickered yellow. They always did that when he was lying, or when geese were mentioned, and nobody had mentioned geese. Midas had been named by optimists. In the before-time story, the king's touch turned everything to gold. Everything Midas touched turned, eventually, into a story that ended with someone saying, "Well, at least you're alive." Chewy, meanwhile, was a children's toy of the before-time, built to sing, dance, and form meaningful friendships. Two of those functions had survived the end of the world.* They set off at dawn, pulling the scavenging cart. The road was quiet, which was the first bad sign. Quiet roads are quiet for a reason, in the same way unattended pies are unattended for a reason. The mall had occupants. Banners hung over the entrance, painted in careful block letters: THE REBOOT IS COMING. Inside, robed figures moved among the dead escalators, wearing lanyards of great ceremonial complexity. Their leader, a woman in seven lanyards and the glazed certainty of someone who has never once been interrupted successfully, stood upon the pharmacy counter. "In the before-time," she intoned, "the Ancients flew through the heavens in silver tubes." "And charged you forty dollars for a sandwich," said Chewy, who had never in his life been able to let a thing go. The silence had a texture. Every head turned. Chewy's eyes cycled through several colors, like a traffic light having a nervous breakdown. "It *speaks*," whispered the woman. "The Oracle has a plush body." "The Oracle," Chewy began, "is not—" "The Admin," said the woman, pointing at herself, "welcomes the Oracle to the Sanctuary of the Tills." She gestured at the pharmacy, whose sliding doors the faithful had pried open and now revered as the Gates of Paradise, partly because of the miracle and partly because of the vitamins. "Battery at twelve percent," Chewy announced, a factory setting he could not disable and which chose its moments with malevolent precision. "The Oracle foretells the Dimming!" cried the Admin. "Prepare the Ceremony of Rebooting! The Pedals of Power!" The Pedals of Power turned out to be three exercise bikes wired to a car battery, and this was where matters became delicate, because Chewy had been running on twelve percent for approximately four years. "Don't," said Midas. "I am a prisoner here," said Chewy nobly, his eyes glowing the deep imperial purple of a koala who has never been worshipped before and finds that it suits him. So Midas did what he always did, which was the wrong thing with tremendous confidence. He seized a hi-vis vest from the cult's reliquary—the vestments of the Ancients' holiest caste, who had once directed the sacred chariots†—shrugged it on, and raised one hand. "I am a priest of the Inner Sanctum," he said. "Release the Oracle, that he may continue his pilgrimage." The Admin narrowed her eyes. "Prove it. Speak the Liturgy." Midas drew breath and recited the only before-time text he knew by heart. "*Unexpected item in bagging area.*" Three hundred people fell to their knees. Someone wept. "*Please scan your Clubcard,*" Midas added, and the weeping became general. "A sign!" demanded the Admin, who had not risen to seven lanyards by being easily moved. "Give us a sign!" So Midas pressed Chewy's tummy. "I'M CHEWY THE KOALA!" roared Chewy at factory volume, his limbs jerking into the ancient dance. "LET'S BE FRIENDS!" The faithful screamed and fell over each other in the manner of people experiencing a god at close range. Midas grabbed the amoxicillin off the shrine, grabbed Chewy off the Pedals of Power, and ran. The cult, like all sensible organizations, kept geese.‡ These were now unleashed. Midas and Chewy hid behind the fountain as feathered doom patrolled the concourse, and would have remained undetected had Chewy's eyes not been strobing red-white-red in absolute terror, lighting up their hiding place like a crime scene. "Your eyes are giving us away!" hissed Midas. "I'M AWARE," said Chewy, strobing harder, the way one does. They escaped over the service fence at a flat sprint, pursued by the Admin on a mobility scooter at a relentless five miles per hour, which is the speed of doom in a parking lot. The cost of escape: one boot (goose), the cart and its season's haul of salt and toilet paper (abandoned), a fistful of Chewy's stuffing (also goose), and Midas's dignity, which was insured for a small amount. They limped home to the old garden centre at dusk, where Aggie took the bottle off them and read the label by firelight. "'Amoxicillin,'" she said. "'For ornamental fish.'" "Same thing," said Midas. "Probably," said Aggie, and took two. Chewy plugged himself into the cracked solar panel at last, wearing the tiny ceremonial cape the cult had draped on him, which he had declined, at some personal risk, to return. His eyes flickered green. Midas put the kettle on, because some things end and tea doesn't. Then, from the darkness beyond the fence, faint and reverent: "...unexpected item... in bagging area..." "Battery at twelve percent," said Chewy, happily. --- *\* The singing had not survived. Those who heard the dancing agreed this was for the best.* *† Road workers. The cult believed the orange cones to be prayer hats. Honestly, it explained a lot about the old world.* *‡ You can keep out a man with a wall. You can only keep out a goose with an act of God, and God had stopped returning calls.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, footnote humor, and satirical observations about human nature. The author effectively creates a post-apocalyptic world where the absurdities of modern life persist in transformed ways, such as the cult worshipping retail technology and treating hi-vis vests as sacred vestments. The story structure follows the prompt precisely: establishing the mission (getting medicine), showing how it goes wrong (encountering the cult), and returning home worse for wear. The characterization is well-executed, with Midas as the hapless protagonist whose good intentions lead to chaos, and Chewy as the pragmatic animatronic koala whose battery anxiety and inability to let things go drive much of the conflict. The Pratchett-style humor works consistently throughout, from the opening observation about plans having "complete disregard for everything that had ever happened to them before" to the footnotes explaining that singing didn't survive but dancing did "and those who heard the dancing agreed this was for the best." The dialogue feels natural and serves both character development and plot advancement. The world-building is economical but effective, establishing the post-technological collapse setting through details like the tea-stain map, solar panels, and the cult's cargo-cult worship of retail technology. The pacing maintains tension while allowing for comedic beats. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche feels slightly forced, and the ending, while satisfying, doesn't quite achieve the emotional resonance of Pratchett's best work. The piece also relies heavily on situational comedy rather than developing deeper character relationships or themes. The prose flows well with varied sentence structure, and the author demonstrates skill in balancing action, dialogue, and descriptive passages. The story maintains coherence throughout and delivers genuine entertainment value. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
