+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Love in the Limelight — Bevan's Books** The rain over Castle Arcade had settled in for the evening, ticking against the glass roof like a metronome with nowhere to be. Eleri Bevan was pricing a box of battered Penguins when the door of her bookshop burst open and the weather came in wearing a two-thousand-pound coat. "Hide me," said the coat. "This is a bookshop." "Perfect. No one looks for anyone in a bookshop. Paparazzi — three of them, maybe four—" He was already scanning the shelves like a man choosing a trench. "Is there a—" "Have you murdered someone?" "Worse. I smiled at a hen do on St Mary Street." She leaned to see past him. Sure enough: lenses. Sighing, she pointed. "Welsh interest. Crouch." He crouched behind *The Mabinogion* with the professionalism of a man who had done battlefield work on camera. A photographer pushed the door open, damp and hopeful. "Seen a tall fella? Coat like—" He gestured at everything. "Only tall fella today bought a thesaurus," Eleri said. "Didn't say thank you." The door shut. The man rose, shook the rain from his hair in a way that had definitely been rehearsed, and deployed the smile. "I'm Rhys. Rhys Maddox." "I know," she said, going back to her pencil. "My niece has your face on a mug." "A mug." "Bone china. Dishwasher safe." "Mugs are good," he said, recovering. "Mugs mean longevity. You don't put a flash in the pan on a mug. Graham Norton told me that, actually—" "Is there something you want to buy? Only the hiding's free, but the loitering's a fiver." He laughed — the laugh from the interviews, head back, eyes crinkled. She'd seen it on the mug. In person it was… well. She pressed the pencil harder than necessary. "Right. Yes. Books." He spread his hands at the shelves. "Big fan. Some of my best friends are books." "What do you read?" "Scripts, mostly. Scripts and—" he gestured nobly "—books in general." "Books in general." "You sound like my agent." "Then your agent's underpaid." She pulled a paperback from the display by the till and held it out. The familiar silver cover. *Ironwinter.* "Funny," he said. "Very good." "Have you read it?" "I've read the scripts. Four series of scripts. I basically *am* Caedmon. I know him better than—" "Page one," she said. "He has a sister." A pause. "He has a sister?" "Had. It's rather the whole— you know what." She made to put it back. "He had a SISTER?" He caught the book. "Four years, and nobody— what happened to her?" "Read it and find out. That's the usual arrangement with books." Something had happened to his face. The smile machinery had stalled, and underneath it was a man genuinely scandalised by a plot twist in his own life. Eleri found, annoyingly, that she didn't mind it. "Right," she said, and moved through the shop, taking down a thriller — "something to talk about that isn't yourself" — and a slim R.S. Thomas — "for looking tragic by windows. I imagine that's a professional requirement." "You think I look tragic by windows?" "I think you've practised." He opened his mouth, shut it, and followed her to the till like a duckling in a designer coat. She was aware of him watching her hands. Her hands, traitorously, were aware of being watched. She put the thriller in the poetry section and had to put it back. "Can I ask you something?" he said. "The loitering's still a fiver." "Do you not like me?" It came out smaller than everything else he'd said. She looked at him properly for the first time. "I don't know you." "You know the mug." "I know the mug." "It's just — everyone likes me. That's sort of the whole—" he waved at himself "—thing. It's the job. And you—" He stopped. Actually ran out of words, did Rhys Maddox, there at her till. "That sounds tiring," she said, more gently than she meant to. He laughed then, and it wasn't the mug laugh at all — shorter, surprised. "God. It's *exhausting*." The rain had softened. She glanced at the window. "Your fan club's moved on. Probably up to the castle. There's a thing there tonight." "I know. I'm meant to be at it. Speeches, auction, small talk with a lord mayor." He didn't move. "I'll take the lot." "Even the Thomas?" "Especially the Thomas. I've got windows all over my hotel." She rang up his purchases and, on impulse — or possibly against every instinct she possessed — dropped a loyalty card into the bag. "Ten stamps. Free coffee." "There's coffee?" "Machine in the back. It's terrible." "Then I'll need all ten to be sure." He tucked the card into his inside pocket, carefully, like a prop letter. He was at the door when he turned. "Eleri." She hadn't told him her name. Her eyes went to the till sign — *E. Bevan, Prop.*, in her grandfather's paint — and back. "Chapter one by Thursday," she said, "or don't come back." He grinned, and it was neither the mug smile nor the interview laugh but something crooked and unscripted, and Eleri Bevan's heart did a small, undignified thing that she decided, firmly, to invoice later. "Thursday," he said, and the bell argued with the rain behind him. She gave it a full minute. Then she straightened *The Mabinogion*, told herself she absolutely would not look him up, and looked him up before the rain stopped.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining consistent quality throughout. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario that feels both fresh and believable, avoiding many common romance tropes while still delivering the requested chemistry. The dialogue is particularly strong, with each character having a distinct voice. Eleri's dry, no-nonsense Welsh wit contrasts effectively with Rhys's initially performative charm that gradually gives way to genuine vulnerability. Lines like "Some of my best friends are books" and "The loitering's still a fiver" feel natural while revealing character. The conversation about the book series he stars in but hasn't read is both humorous and character-revealing. The Welsh setting is well-integrated without being heavy-handed. References to Castle Arcade, St Mary Street, and R.S. Thomas feel authentic rather than tourist-brochure obvious. The bookshop atmosphere is convincingly rendered through specific details like the Penguin classics and loyalty card machine. Character development follows a believable arc. Rhys's transition from practiced charm to genuine confusion when his usual tactics fail feels earned rather than sudden. His smaller, more vulnerable question "Do you not like me?" marks a clear shift. Eleri's internal conflict between surface resistance and growing attraction is well-handled, particularly in moments like her hands being "traitorously aware of being watched." The prose style is confident and economical, with effective metaphors that don't overreach. "The weather came in wearing a two-thousand-pound coat" is clever without being precious. The pacing moves well between banter and quieter character moments. Some minor weaknesses include a few moments where the wit feels slightly forced, and the ending, while charming, edges toward predictable romance territory. The "small, undignified thing" her heart does is perhaps the most conventional moment in an otherwise fresh piece. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements: the paparazzi setup, the character dynamics as specified, the dialogue-heavy approach, and the underlying chemistry. The word count appears appropriate for the scope. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 87.8
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Underhold The door swung inward before Aya's glove ever brushed the iron. Beyond it, the underhold exhaled — cold, wet air reeking of brine, old tallow, and the sweet fat-smoke of whale oil. One by one, with the soft tick of clockwork valves, the gas lamps along the walls sputtered alight, casting long, unsettling shadows across the stone floor. The vault stretched away beneath the old try-works like the inside of some great drowned beast: pillars ribbed up into darkness, water wept down black granite, and at the far end, behind a desk of inlaid scrimshaw, sat Mother Maren. "Evening, ducks," she said. "Mind the salt lines. They're older than your grandfathers, and I'm fond of them." Aya's heart slammed once, hard. Around them the shadows unfolded into men — six, eight — rising from behind casks and rendering troughs with crossbows wound and clockwork pistols levelled. Someone had a harpoon-gun mounted on a swivel, its barbed head grinning in the lamplight. "Well," Katra breathed. "I said it smelled of a lie. I did say that." "The bluebottles raided the Floe at dawn," Simeon rumbled. "We saw the ledgers. We saw the chains." "You saw what I paid for." Mother Maren was a small, tidily dressed woman, grey at the temples, honing a flensing knife against a little whetstone, unhurried as a fishwife. "A raid costs less than a hanging judge and tells you twice as much. Shows you who your friends are, when they think the door's been left open." The stone sang along the blade. "Come all the way in, Rook. You've climbed my ranks like ivy for a year. Don't go shy at the top of the wall." Aya's mind raced down its corridors and found every one bricked up. The old Scythan knot-wards were cut into the floor in rings of salt and scrimshaw, and her gift — the cold company coiled behind her teeth since girlhood, the Hushed who answered when she called — guttered against those marks like a lamp starved of oil. For the first time in years, she could not feel them listening. She stepped forward anyway. There was nothing behind her but the drop. "Don't," she said quietly, as Simeon's huge hand crept toward his cudgel. A crossbow string went taut with a sound like a plucked tooth. "Don't, Sim. It's done." Simeon was praying under his breath — the Drowned Saints, the Grey Mother, the whole soggy litany of the whale-roads. "Smart moppet." Maren set down the stone. "Knives on the floor, all three. Katra, I know about the one in your boot. There's a lamb." Steel chimed on stone. Then Maren gestured, and a big man with a whaler's hook crossed to Aya. The hook slid beneath the edge of her mask — cold iron against her cheekbone — and with a flick, cut the strings. The mask fell. The lamplight did its work. A sound went through the room, small and collective, and one of the guards — dock-bred, by his tarred nails — whispered, "Christ of the crows. That's the Vayne girl." Mother Maren went very still. Then she smiled like a tide coming in. "Lady Aya Vayne." She savoured it. "Half the oil in this city comes off your father's hooks, and here's his only daughter crawling my cellars in a bird mask." Her eyes narrowed; memory arriving like a ship at harbor. "You've got her face. I stood at Gallows Quay and watched your mother dance the hempen jig. Pretty thing. She called on things at the end, did you know? Nothing came." *Something comes every night,* Aya thought wildly. She could feel the Hushed clawing at the wards from the far side, circling like sharks beyond the glass of a bathysphere — and for the first time since she was twelve years old, she was alone inside her own skull. So she did the only thing left. She drew herself up the way her governess had drilled her — spine, chin, the drawing-room voice deployed like a sabre. "Then you know precisely who I am, and precisely what I'm worth. Kill me in this cellar and I'm a scandal for a week. Keep me breathing, Mother Maren, and I am a fortune. My father will pay any sum named to have me back — *quietly*. And you've enemies who'd pay near as well to learn Lord Vayne's daughter ran with the Morska." Her voice held steady, and she hated it, because every word was a small death. Her name, spoken aloud down here, could never be unspoken. It would follow her up into the light — to the waltzes, the Athenaeum lectures, her mother's shadow lengthening behind her wherever she went. "Dead, I'm worth the weight of a coffin. Alive, I'm worth a fleet." "Ransom," Maren mused. "Or your father pays double to be sure you never surface at all. Tidy girl like you — you're a liability with a pulse." "Then sell me to his enemies and let him bid against them. You're a businesswoman. Auction me." Katra made a strangled sound. "Aya—" "Hush, kitten." Maren rose, came around the desk, the flensing knife loose in her fist. She looked Aya over the way a purser appraises a catch. "They said the Rook never met a lock she couldn't sweet-talk. That doors *opened* for her." Her voice dropped into the old terror of the docks — the fear that lived below decks, in the hold, in the black water under the keel. "Men in this guild trust brass and oil and steel, my lady. They do not care for the other thing. If I learn you've brought the other thing into my house—" "Your house is warded to the rafters," Aya said. "You tell me what I could possibly bring in." A long, cold silence. Then Maren laughed — one short bark — and turned away. "Salt cells. All three. Feed them, even. We've a lady of quality to keep plump for market." As hooks and pistol barrels herded her toward the dark, Aya's gaze caught on the cradle behind Maren's desk: glass, salt-ringed, and within it the thing they'd come to steal — a spiral of ancient horn, banded in green gold, long as a man's leg, humming so faintly she felt it in her teeth rather than heard it. The Scythan Horn. And as the guards took her arms, it seemed — it *almost* seemed — to know her name.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through evocative descriptions like "the underhold exhaled — cold, wet air reeking of brine, old tallow, and the sweet fat-smoke of whale oil" and maintains consistent nautical/whaling imagery throughout. The adherence to instructions is excellent - all story beats are hit (capture, unmasking, negotiation), the confrontation is painted vividly, and the requested elements (dialogue, internal monologue, gritty Victorian aesthetic, gothic horror) are well-integrated. The author avoids writing a fight scene as requested and maintains third-person past tense throughout. Character voices are distinct and authentic to the setting. Mother Maren's dialogue feels particularly strong with lines like "Evening, ducks" and "There's a lamb" - casual menace delivered in period-appropriate vernacular. Aya's transformation from thief to aristocrat when unmasked is believable and well-executed through her shift to "drawing-room voice deployed like a sabre." The prose quality is high, with sophisticated sentence construction and evocative imagery. Phrases like "memory arriving like a ship at harbor" and "the Hushed clawing at the wards from the far side, circling like sharks beyond the glass of a bathysphere" demonstrate skillful metaphorical language that enhances rather than overwhelms the narrative. The emotional complexity is notable - Aya's internal conflict about her identity being revealed, the weight of her mother's execution, and her desperate calculation in the negotiation create genuine tension. The piece maintains appropriate darkness without veering into overwrought territory. Some minor weaknesses include occasional density that might slow pacing, and while the worldbuilding is rich, it occasionally borders on being overly elaborate. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall quality. The ending with the Scythan Horn provides an intriguing hook while maintaining the scene's tension. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gunpowder Rendezvous A lazy ceiling fan barely stirs the smoky air inside the bar. Outside, the dockside street sweats neon and diesel, but in here it's all shadow and slow time. Spike Spiegel sits at the far end of the counter with a glass of something amber that tastes like kerosene and costs more than he's carrying. For an hour he's watched the door in the mirror behind the bottles. The tip said Varga's people drink here. The tip cost him his last decent cigarette. Then the door opens, and the room changes pressure. She walks in like she owns the air—boots, shorts, black top, a dragon coiled down her right arm, two pistols riding her thighs. The locals look, then look away fast, which tells Spike more than any wanted poster. She takes the stool beside him out of eleven empty ones, and he understands that as a message. "Rum," she tells the bartender. "Bottle. Not a glass." She studies him the way you'd study a snake in your path. "You've been watching the door." "Watching the mirror," Spike says. "The door just happens to be in it." Something almost like a smile. "Cute." She drinks straight from the bottle and sets it down between them like a challenge. "Revy." "Spike." "I know. Bounty hunter. You look broke." Her eyes flick over him, lazy and thorough, pausing on his left eye—the one that doesn't track quite right. She files that away. "Let me guess. Domingo Varga." He slides a folded photo across the bar. Same face she's carrying in her back pocket. For a moment they just look at each other, two professionals recognizing the shape of the same bad luck. "Back off, cowboy," Revy says. "This one's mine." "Can't. Rent's due." He lights a cigarette off the bartender's trembling Zippo and orders whatever's fried, because he's starving and the bill is a problem for later. "We could split it." "I don't split. I don't share. I don't play well with others." "You're a regular children's story." She laughs—short, sharp, surprised by it—and hates herself a little for it. They drink. She orders tequila to test him; he matches her shot for shot, which she files under arrogance and can't stop watching. He moves like water even reaching for a glass. Nothing about him is afraid of her. She's not used to how quiet that makes a room feel. The door bangs open at midnight. Five men, coats too heavy for the heat. The mirror gives them away a half-second before Revy's instincts do, and half a second is a fortune. Spike flows sideways off the stool as the first shot takes his glass. Revy kicks the table up into a shield and comes up with both Berettas singing—left hand and right hand choosing different targets, like her body is two people and both of them are angry. The bar turns to noise and strobing light: bottles bursting, the fan chewing a stray round, spinning mad. Spike breaks a man's arm with an economy that looks almost gentle, takes his gun away like a borrowed lighter, puts him down with an elbow. "That's three," Revy says. "I'm not counting." "You are now, asshole." It ends the way it always ends. Four down, one talking. The talker gives up a motel—the Palms, room six, where Varga's sweating out the night with a case of bearer bonds and a head worth two million woolongs, dead or alive. They take Varga's room in eleven seconds. She goes low, he goes high, and afterward they stand in the wreckage breathing hard, both of them grinning, both of them pretending they're not. Then it's just the case on the bed, and the two of them. Revy's pistols come up first. Spike's Jericho is already level with her heart—he never seemed to draw it at all. The air conditioner rattles. Neon bleeds red through the blinds, painting them like a crime scene that hasn't happened yet. "One gun against two," she says. "Bad math, Spike." "One's enough. I've got a depth-perception problem. Everything looks close." He isn't smiling, quite. "You'd have to be faster than you are." "You'd have to be bulletproof." "Nobody is." The standoff stretches. Sweat slides down her spine. She's killed men for less, killed them for nothing, and her finger knows a trigger's weight like a prayer. But he's standing inside her reach without flinching, and his one good eye holds her steady, and somewhere between the gun smoke and the rum she realizes she's closer to him than a clean shot needs. So is he. "So which is it," he says. "You the good guy or the bad guy?" "I'm not a good person. Not a bad person." Her thumbs rest on the hammers. "I'm just here." "Huh." The faintest crooked tilt at the corner of his mouth. "Same." "You're standing close enough to kiss me," he says, mild as weather. "Makes the shooting awkward." "Shooting's easy. It's the other thing that's complicated." Her pulse is a fist on a door. "I don't trust anybody. That's kept me breathing." "Me neither. Works great. Look how happy we are." He's seen her before, he thinks—not her, but the shape of her. All edges, nowhere soft to land. Maybe that's why he doesn't step back. Maybe that's exactly why he should. Somewhere below, a siren dopplers past and dies. Varga groans, forgotten, tied to the minibar. The case sits on the bed between two million woolongs and two people who built whole lives out of never blinking first. Neither lowers. Neither fires. The neon buzzes. Her mouth is right there; so is the muzzle of his gun, and she can't tell which one she's thinking about, and from his smile, neither can he. "Whatever happens," Spike says, "happens." Nothing does. Everything could. The night holds its breath, and so do they.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding. The author captures both Spike and Revy's voices authentically, incorporating their signature mannerisms, speech patterns, and psychological profiles effectively. Spike's fatalistic philosophy and laid-back demeanor contrast well with Revy's aggressive, trust-averse nature, creating compelling tension. The prose style is notably sophisticated, employing noir-influenced imagery and atmospheric details that establish mood effectively. Descriptions like "the room changes pressure" and "flows sideways off the stool" demonstrate elegant economy of language. The action sequences are well-choreographed and maintain clarity despite their intensity. The story structure follows a classic noir progression from setup through confrontation to standoff, hitting all requested elements: seedy bar setting, mutual attraction, trust issues, gunplay, and motel room tension. The sexual tension builds organically through their interactions rather than being forced. However, the piece suffers from some stylistic overreach. Phrases like "boots, shorts, black top, a dragon coiled down her right arm" read as overly compressed, and some metaphors feel strained ("like her body is two people and both of them are angry"). The dialogue occasionally becomes too self-consciously clever, particularly in the final exchanges. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity without forcing resolution, which suits both characters' natures. The piece avoids the trap of unearned emotional breakthroughs while still delivering on the promised tension. The action sequences demonstrate good pacing and visual clarity, though some descriptions border on overwrought. Character motivations remain consistent throughout, and both protagonists retain their essential natures while showing subtle attraction. The piece successfully balances the requested elements without feeling mechanical or checklist-driven. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 87.2
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Útiseta I was fifteen the autumn Papa's job moved us from Stockholm to Björkåsen, seven hundred kilometres north of anywhere I'd ever been. Two years, they promised. Then home. My mother said it like a prayer and my father said it like a verdict, and I stopped asking which of them I believed. At fifteen you'll believe almost anything if the alternative is admitting you're bored. This was 1995. The village had one bus, one Konsum, and Astrid. By October I had played all her midnight games: the mirror under the bed that showed you who was lying; the knotted string you slipped into someone's desk to make them think of you. Harmless things. Astrid treated them like smoking behind the gym — half mischief, half inheritance. Her grandmother had taught her, she said. The old ways. But útiseta was different. I knew that before we'd even left the road. We met at the crossroads where the bus turned around. Astrid looked me over like a border guard. "Hair clip." I handed it over. "Ring. Keys." She buried them under the milestone with her own. "Iron locks doors," she said. "Tonight we want them open. You haven't eaten since dark? Good. The dead are hungry. You shouldn't smell of food." She carried a bottle of milk and, in her pocket, her grandmother's silver coin. On the way up past the Lindgrens' meadow she gave me the rules in the same flat voice she used for homework. Sit back to back on the crown. Don't sleep — what sleeps on a mound doesn't always wake alone. Don't say any names, not mine, not yours. Whatever you hear, don't answer. "Even if it's me," she said. "Especially if it's me. And don't leave until the first bird." "Where did your grandmother learn it?" "Mormor wouldn't teach me this one." She almost sounded proud. "The church made it a crime, did you know that? Four hundred years ago, and they were still frightened of it. I listened at the door when she told her sister." The mound stood on the ridge above the meadow — Kungshögen, people called it, though no one could say which king. The birches had already turned; their leaves clicked overhead like small bones. Mist had come up off the bog and lay thick against the mound's flanks, glowing faintly under the moon, so it rose out of the white like an island, only the bare crown showing. We climbed into clear cold air and sat down back to back. Astrid poured the milk in a ring around us and set the coin at the northern point. "Hold your question," she said. "Don't speak it. And Linnea—" her voice caught once, the only time that night — "ask the one you want. They answer the one you mean." I had chosen to ask whether Johan Bergkvist liked me. It took an hour on that mound to learn what I actually wanted to know. At first there was only the night working: a fox barking down in the dark, wind worrying the birches, the bog muttering to itself. My back went numb against Astrid's. The stars wheeled. I thought of my warm bed, of how stupid we'd feel at school on Monday. Then the bog stopped. All of it, at once, the way a room goes quiet when someone walks in. Something moved in the mist below us. Slow steps. Circling. Never climbing, never quite approaching — patient, the way water is patient. Astrid's spine went rigid against mine. I told myself: fox. Deer. Wind. I had a whole list and I read it like a prayer. "Linnea." My mother's voice. From down in the mist, from everywhere at once, with her exact patience in the syllables — the way she'd call me in from a yard when I was small. My mother, asleep in the village, four kilometres away. "Linnea. Come down here. I know what you asked." And God help me, the question that rose up in me was not about any boy. It was: *will we ever go home?* It had been under everything for months, and the voice took hold of it like a hand closing on a string. "I know whether you're going home," it said. "Ask me. It's allowed." Then, behind me, from exactly where Astrid sat, it spoke in Astrid's voice: "It's all right. You can turn around now." The real Astrid made no sound at all. She only pressed back harder. My mouth opened. I felt the word shape itself— Astrid's hand clamped over my mouth, hard enough that her ring would have cut me, if she'd been wearing it. She held on. We sat like that, shaking, while the mist waited. Then, somewhere down towards the village, a thrush started up — one thread of song — and the slope below us was only a slope, and the mist was only mist, thinning towards morning. We didn't speak going down. At the crown's edge Astrid stopped: the milk ring was still there, wet on the grass. The silver coin was gone. "Payment accepted," she said, the way people say things they're hoping are true. At the milestone we dug out our keys with numb fingers. "I didn't answer," I said. Astrid looked at me then, and she looked older than her grandmother. "You started to. They count that." We never played the midnight games after that. Or Astrid never played them with me. We did move back to Stockholm, in the end, the following spring. I got my answer. I've spent thirty years deciding not to ask what it cost. I've explained that night to myself a hundred ways — cold, suggestion, two girls drunk on their own daring. Foxes take shiny things. Some days I believe all of it. But no one has spoken my name in the dark since, without my going still.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the delicate balance required for effective folk horror. The author successfully captures the specific atmosphere of 1990s rural Sweden through authentic details like the single Konsum store and the cultural displacement of a Stockholm teenager. The narrative voice is particularly well-executed, maintaining the promised transparency of adult recollection while preserving the immediacy of teenage experience. The characterization of both Linnea and Astrid feels genuine and nuanced. Linnea's progression from boredom to genuine terror is believable, and her internal conflict between skepticism and fear rings true for a displaced teenager seeking belonging. Astrid emerges as more than a simple catalyst - her mixture of teenage rebellion and inherited knowledge creates an appropriately unsettling dynamic. The detail about her grandmother refusing to teach útiseta, forcing Astrid to eavesdrop, adds psychological depth to her motivations. The prose quality is consistently high, with particularly effective imagery in passages like the mound rising "out of the white like an island" and leaves clicking "like small bones." The author demonstrates restraint in avoiding overwrought descriptions while still creating vivid atmospheric details. The pacing builds tension effectively through the ritual preparations and the gradual escalation of supernatural manifestations. Most impressively, the piece maintains genuine ambiguity about the supernatural elements without feeling evasive. The voice calling Linnea's name could be psychological projection, but the missing silver coin provides a concrete detail that resists easy explanation. The author earns this ambiguity through careful attention to both psychological realism and folkloric authenticity. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose well. Astrid's matter-of-fact delivery of ritual instructions effectively contrasts the mundane and the mystical. The supernatural voice's manipulation - answering the question Linnea means rather than asks - demonstrates sophisticated understanding of folk horror tropes. The ending strikes an appropriate note of lasting unease without overstatement. The adult narrator's admission that "no one has spoken my name in the dark since, without my going still" provides a haunting conclusion that suggests lasting psychological impact without requiring belief in literal supernatural consequences. Minor weaknesses include some exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated and occasional moments where the adult perspective intrudes slightly on the teenage experience. However, these are relatively minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully fulfills the prompt's complex requirements. + Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 89.8
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Therapeutic Boundaries The lavender was her idea. Everything else in the treatment room smelled honest—alcohol, latex, the chemical ghost of ultrasound gel—but the diffuser on the shelf breathed out something floral and untrue, as if a body could be negotiated with. It was Tuesday, session twenty-two of the forty the settlement required, and Dana stood reading my chart with her hair pinned up the way she'd worn it at nationals six years ago, when she was the fastest training partner I ever had and I was still becoming whatever it is I've stopped being. She'd left the sport intact at twenty-six, walked away with all her tendons, gone back to school. Retirement had filled her out the way stopping fills people out—she'd lost the hungry look we used to share, and it suited her, and I resented that too. I resented everything recovery-adjacent. Me, I'd felt the tendon go 180 meters into a final, a kick delivered by nobody, and hopped twice before the track came up. "Shoes off," she said. Not unkind. Precise. Knee-to-wall first. Right foot—my foot, the one with the pale zipper of a scar behind the ankle—set against the tape line. "Drive the knee over the toes. Heel down." I drove, sliding the foot back along the floor until the heel hovered like a thing with its own loyalties. She measured: seven centimeters of big toe to drywall, and recorded it. Left foot: twelve. She recorded that too, and the pencil scratch sounded louder than it should have. Numbers were the room's native language. We both spoke it so we wouldn't have to speak anything else. Then the table. Prone, face in the cradle, ankles hanging off the end. Her palms found the gastrocnemius—the upper belly of the calf, where the muscle still remembered being a sprinter's—and worked downward in slow, loaded strokes toward the tendon. My hamstring braced before her hands even landed. "Stop guarding," she said, and I couldn't. At the repair site she switched to short cross-fiber friction, thumb rolling the thickened cord like she was reading it. The pain ran a four that flared to six; she asked the number and I lied, and she said, "I can hear you not breathing, Marcus. Four?" "Four." Liar's arithmetic. Her other hand steadied my heel, and the treacherous thing—the thing that would never appear on any insurance form—was that my body had started arriving early to her touch, warming toward it, gooseflesh climbing the back of my thigh half a second before her fingers got there. She saw it. I know she saw it. She recited anatomy to cover it: "The soleus doesn't cross the knee, so we'll take it bent next"—spoken to no one, a shield built out of facts. When she finished, she pressed two fingers into the hollow behind my inner ankle, just below the medial malleolus. Posterior tibial pulse. Purely clinical—a circulation check—except her fingertips sat directly on the evidence. My pulse struck against her skin: fast, caught, obvious. Neither of us mentioned it. Her hand left a beat late. Supine then, leg straight, Dana at the foot of the table cradling my heel in one palm, the ball of my foot braced against her collarbone. "Push against me. Thirty percent. Hold—five, four—relax." On the relax she leaned her weight through my leg and drew the ankle up toward my shin, into the long bright stretch that climbed the calf like water climbing a shoreline. "Six," I said, honest for once, and she held me there at the edge, trembling faintly with the effort of holding my whole leg, or with something else. The lavender was on her skin now, in the heat of her exhale. My hand found her shoulder for balance—the first time I'd touched her since 2019—and we both went still. "Breathe," she said. It was genuinely unclear which of us she meant. Last: the step by the window. Balls of both feet on the edge, rise up, shift the weight right, and lower the heel below the step over a count of three. The calf shook—visibly, humiliatingly—and her hand came to my hip to steady it. "That's not weakness," she said. "That's recruitment." Down again. Burn like a lit fuse. "What's the point," I said, "of healing to sixty percent of something?" Her hand stayed. "You think recovery is a consolation prize." "Isn't it?" "No. It's a different race. You keep trying to run it like the old one." She'd known me since we were seventeen; she'd earned the sentence. Down, three seconds, the tendon holding. Then, quieter: "You don't hate the pain. You hate that it works." She wrote the plan. I re-laced my shoe over my new, ordinary ankle, the heel staying down a centimeter longer than last week—six degrees gained, and I hated every one of them, and I wanted every one of them. "Thursday," she said, professional again, both of us returned to our own sides of the line we'd spent an hour redrawing. The lavender followed me into the hall. I hated it. I could already tell it was going to smell like her.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical mastery and sophisticated handling of a complex emotional scenario. The narrative successfully fulfills the prompt's demanding requirements while delivering a nuanced exploration of loss, identity, and reluctant healing. The anatomical precision is remarkable - every therapeutic technique is described with clinical accuracy, from the knee-to-wall test measurements to the cross-fiber friction work on the gastrocnemius. The author clearly understands both athletic training and physical therapy protocols, creating authentic medical detail that grounds the emotional content in physical reality. The character work is particularly strong. Marcus emerges as a fully realized protagonist whose resentment toward recovery feels genuine and earned. His internal contradictions - hating the progress while wanting it, resenting Dana's successful transition while being drawn to her - create compelling psychological complexity. Dana is equally well-developed, maintaining professional boundaries while navigating their shared history and present attraction. The prose style is controlled and elegant, with precise word choices that serve the narrative's dual focus on physical and emotional precision. Phrases like "retirement had filled her out the way stopping fills people out" and "my pulse struck against her skin: fast, caught, obvious" demonstrate sophisticated craft. The writing maintains clinical detachment while building erotic tension through restraint rather than explicitness. The sensory work is exceptional, particularly in rendering pain and physical sensation. The progression from "four that flared to six" and the description of the calf stretch "like water climbing a shoreline" create visceral reader experience. The sexual tension builds naturally through professional touch and involuntary physical responses. The dialogue feels authentic to both the clinical setting and the characters' shared athletic background. Their communication through "numbers" as the "room's native language" effectively establishes how they avoid emotional intimacy while maintaining necessary interaction. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose approaches preciousness, though it generally stays on the right side of the line. The ending, while thematically appropriate, could have pushed slightly further into the emotional complexity established throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Fourteen: Mango Season **Dawn — the Danish Factory** The knock came in with the heat, both through the door at once. "Jamie. It's past six. Captain's boat goes to Whampoa on the tide, and you're on the manifest." I sat up inside the mosquito net and let my hands stop shaking before I spoke. "Tell Venn I have the flux." "You had the flux Monday." "Then it has me again. Come in, Tom. Don't hover in the passage like a creditor." Hargrave entered smelling of shave-soap and coffee, two things I could no longer afford in the same week. He looked at me the way a man looks at a gun that has failed to fire. "Christ, Jamie. You're grey." "Canton air." "Canton *something*." He set a dollar on my sea-chest and pretended he hadn't. "I'll say the surgeon's seen you. But Venn keeps a tally, you know. He keeps a tally of everything." "I'll stand the next one." "So you said." He paused at the door. "Whatever it is that's eating you — eat it back, man." When he'd gone I lay doing the day's arithmetic, which in Canton is worked in a single denomination. The dragon, as Pike calls it, does not take promises. It takes Spanish silver, and I had one dollar and the King's coat on my back. **Morning — a chop-house off Old China Street** Pike had been a Company clerk once, before the Company found him out; now he brokered shame for men with better tailoring than his own. We ate rice under a fan pulled by a boy on a string. "I need ten dollars by dark," I said. "You need ten by noon, Jamie. I've seen your eyes." He sucked a grain of rice from his knuckle. "There's a boat goes down the reach tonight. Six small chests, papers all in order save that the papers are lies. Apo wants a King's officer in the stern sheets — uniform coat — to be England if the Hoppo's cruisers come sniffing. Two hours of sitting. Eight dollars." "Ten." "Eight, and I'll stand your slate with the Widow." He leaned in; his breath was cloves over rot. "You needn't do anything, man. That's the beauty. You needn't even look at the cargo. Just be the flag." "The flag costs twelve." "The flag costs eight, and it's robbery at the price for a flag that sweats like you do. Take it. The dragon doesn't keep office hours." He advanced me two dollars against the night, and I told myself the lie I kept for daylight hours: one pipe, to steady the hand, and the rest saved whole. **Afternoon — below the city wall** The alleys toward the wall had gone overripe with June. The scent of rotting mangoes permeated everything — split fruit weeping amber on the stalls, skins trodden to sweet paste underfoot, a smell like rum gone wrong that lodged at the back of the throat and stayed. From a doorway came the click of the fan-tan cup. A joss house burned sandalwood against the stink, and lost. The Widow kept her rooms behind a shuttered fan-maker's, up a stair that remembered better tenants. She was fifty, painted like a bride, sharp as a seller of nails. "Lieutenant." She never used my name; rank was my name now. "Two dollar buy you one. Maybe." "It bought two in spring." "Spring, King's ship at Lintin, navy busy, price low. Now navy men all hide, price go up." She took my dollars, weighed them like a fishwife. "Your coat get thinner every moon, Lieutenant. One day you come with no coat. What then?" "Then I'll come as a common sailor and pay a common sailor's price." She laughed — the only true laugh I'd get that day — and shouted down the stair for the lamp. The room held six couches and the sweat of every man who'd ever lain on them. She cooked the pill on its needle over a flame the size of a tear, turned it, coaxed it, set it in the bowl, and I drew the first honest breath of the day: burnt honey, flowers gone to ash, the river and the mangoes and Canton itself let out of me on the exhale. Beside me a Parsee merchant slept the sleep of the well-organized. The boy fanned the charcoal. Time stopped keeping its tally. **Night — a creek below the factories** Apo's boat was twenty feet of fast tarred pine, six oars, and a cargo under palm mats that I did not look at, because I had agreed not to look. "Tea," Apo said, grinning with one tooth. "Don't tell me." We slipped down the reach past the hulks at Whampoa, where my own ship's riding lights burned like a reproach. Off the second creek a lantern rose out of the black and a voice hailed us. "Hoppo's man," Apo murmured. "Stand up, Lieutenant. Be big." I stood in the coat I had sweated through across two oceans and raised my face into their light. "His Britannic Majesty's dispatches for the Commodore at Lintin," I said, in the tone I once used on midshipmen. "Delay this boat, and you explain it to the men-of-war." A long pause. Then laughter, and a string of cash flipped across the water into their boat — Apo's toll, not mine — and the lantern dipped away. "They eat too," Apo said. **Midnight — the factory steps** Pike paid me under the British flag: eight dollars, the Widow's two off the top before I touched them, and three more I carried straight back through the mango-stink to her stair, because midnight in Canton is longer than any watch I stood at sea. Hargrave was waiting on the steps with his pipe. "Venn asked for you twice. I told him the surgeon bled you and forbade it." "Tom—" "Don't." He knocked the pipe out on the rail. "Stand your watch Thursday, Jamie. Stand it if it kills you. Because whatever this is — it will." Up in my room I put three dollars under the floorboard with the others I never saved, and lay listening to the tide turn for Lintin. Tomorrow, I told the dark, I'd be the man I was at Trafalgar. The dark, which knew Canton's arithmetic better than I, said nothing at all.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and atmospheric writing, successfully creating an immersive portrait of addiction and moral decay in 1830s Canton. The author effectively uses the requested structure of brief vignettes throughout a single day, each anchored by dialogue that reveals character and advances the narrative. The historical setting feels authentic and well-researched, with period-appropriate details like Spanish silver currency, the Hoppo's cruisers, and references to Trafalgar. The sensory details are particularly strong - the rotting mangoes that permeate the afternoon section create a perfect metaphor for decay while establishing vivid sense of place. The opium den scene avoids sensationalism while conveying the drug's effects through subtle, poetic language. The dialogue feels natural and period-appropriate, revealing character relationships and social dynamics efficiently. Pike's mercenary pragmatism, Hargrave's concerned friendship, and the Widow's business-like approach to vice all come through clearly in their speech patterns. Merrick's voice remains consistent throughout - educated, self-aware, yet increasingly desperate. The character work is nuanced, avoiding simple moral judgments. Merrick is neither romanticized nor demonized; he's a man caught in circumstances partly of his own making, using his remaining authority as currency while lying to himself about control. The supporting characters feel like real people with their own motivations rather than mere plot devices. The prose style is elegant without being overwrought, using metaphor effectively ("looked at me the way a man looks at a gun that has failed to fire") and maintaining period-appropriate formality. The pacing moves well between scenes, and the circular structure - beginning and ending with Merrick in his room, making promises he won't keep - reinforces the addiction's cyclical nature. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical language borders on being too polished for the gritty subject matter, and the ending, while thematically appropriate, doesn't offer much surprise. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances historical authenticity, character development, and atmospheric writing. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Humour: Sisyphus Gives Advice — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Sisyphus Gives Advice Write a comedic scene in the style of Terry Pratchett featuring Sisyphus, who has started an advice column while continuing his eternal boulder-pushing. Rather than being bitter about his punishment, Sisyphus has developed a comfortably zen emotional intelligence and wisdom from his centuries of repetitive labor. Not in the cliche ways you might expect -- he's actually very grounded, warm & connected with the everyday human plight. He dictates responses to "Crow," his feathered familiar who serves as both scribe and mail carrier. The piece should include Sisyphus thoughtfully answering several advice-seekers' letters about everyday problems (relationship troubles, work-life balance, dealing with difficult people, life purpose doubts) while periodically returning to his boulder-pushing duties. His advice should be surprisingly insightful and practical. The humor should emerge naturally from the messages he receives, from mundane to risque. Sisyphus should genuinely enjoy helping others, finding meaning in this new pursuit while maintaining his boulder routine. The humour should be *understated and not forced*. Contrary to expectation, almost none of his advice involves boulder rolling metaphors (though he does sneak *one* subtle in there), nor a treatise on stoicism. We are also going against the obvious fish-out-of-water trope; in fact, he's surprisingly a natural at this. In all of this , it's important to *show, don't tell*. The format should be largely epistolary, with the occasional interjection for scene setting. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Sisyphus Gives Advice The boulder reached the summit at midmorning, paused with the air of an actor certain of his cue, and rolled back down. Sisyphus watched it go. The descent was the quiet part of his day, and over the years he had learned to do his best work in it. "Post, Crow," he said. The crow landed on his shoulder, dropped the mailbag, and coughed up a brass button, which he kept.¹ ¹ Crow was not, technically, a familiar. He preferred "editorial consultant," and was paid in shiny things and absolute veto over spelling. --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *My wife and I have been married fourteen years and are happy except for the dishwasher. She loads it like a raccoon falling down a staircase. She says my way takes half an hour. This is the hill we have chosen to die on. No pun intended.* *— Forks Up, Fresno* "Dear Forks Up," Sisyphus dictated, walking. "The argument is not about the dishwasher. It is about whether the person you married respects your way of doing things. So: whoever loads the dishwasher owns the dishwasher. The other person says thank you and leaves the room. And remember that a badly loaded dishwasher still washes the dishes. There is no comparable mercy in a badly loaded marriage. — Yours, S." Crow scratched it down in the looping script that mortal readers assumed was a typeface. --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *My boss emails at midnight and marks everything URGENT. Last week I answered one at my grandfather's funeral. Grandad would have understood. That is the problem.* *— Drowning, Duluth* "Dear Drowning. The work will always ask for more. That is what work is for. It is not what you are for. No employer has ever sent a note saying 'That is plenty now, well done, go outside.' You will have to write that note yourself. Choose a stopping time. Tell everyone. Keep it. Answer nothing after six for a fortnight, and I predict three days of thunder, then weather. — S." --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *How many times a week is normal? Asking for my husband, who is standing right here.* *— Pink, Peoria* "Dear Pink. Whatever the number is, someone will tell you it is wrong. They are selling something. — S." --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *There is a woman in my book club who corrects everyone's pronunciation. Last month she corrected the author. The author was dead, and present only as an audiobook.* *— Well Read, Woking* "Dear Well Read. You cannot cure her, and it is not your job to try. You are not obliged to attend every argument you are invited to. Let the corrections land the way rain lands on a roof. If you must act, do this: thank her, warmly, for one correction that genuinely helped you. It costs nothing and will confuse her for months. Kindness, deployed precisely, is the most unsettling weapon there is. — S." --- At the summit the boulder paused again. Sisyphus leaned on it a moment — not pushing, just leaning, the way one leans on a fence with an old friend. Then it went down, and so did he, and Crow read the next letter on the wing.² ² The column had begun with a misprinted address in a mortal newspaper. The postal service of the Underworld, which had not admitted an error in six thousand years, delivered the letters anyway. The mail, as is well documented, always gets through. --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *For months now my ——— has been ———. It is enormous. Every night it ——— and my wife just ———. I have tried ———, but the neighbours are starting to ———. The pigeons are frightened. I am desperate.* *— ———* "It got rained on," said Crow. "Or wept on." "Snoring," said Crow. "Could be the dog. It says 'enormous.'" "Great Dane, then." "Could be the other thing." "It is never the other thing," said Crow. "It is always worth answering," said Sisyphus, "as though it might be. Dear Sir or Madam. Your letter arrived in some distress, and parts of it remain between you and the gods. I shall answer all three versions. If it is snoring: a physician, and sooner than you think. If it is the dog: training, patience, and a better fence. If it is the other thing: speak honestly to your wife, who loves you and, I promise, already knows. In all three cases, apologise to the neighbours, and stop doing whatever you have been doing to the pigeons. — S." --- *Dear Sisyphus,* *I am thirty-six. I have a decent job, a flat, and a cat that tolerates me. By every measure I should be content, and instead I lie awake wondering what any of it is for. You, of all people, must have wondered. How do you stand it? What is the point?* *— Adrift, Aberdeen* Sisyphus was quiet for a while, which for him meant several switchbacks. "Dear Adrift," he said at last. "I am wary of the question 'what is the point.' It is too big to answer all at once, and asking it gets you no nearer an answer. Try a smaller one: what needs doing today that I would be glad to have done? Then do that. Feed the cat. Ring your mother. Answer a stranger's letter. Meaning is not something you find, like a dropped key. It accumulates, the way sediment makes a coastline. "Not everything worth doing stays done — the dishes teach us that daily — and we do them anyway, because the doing is the life. The done is just the souvenir. "P.S. A cat that tolerates you is not nothing. Cats do not bluff." --- At the foot of the hill the boulder waited with the patience of geology. Crow tucked the finished letters into the bag — signed, sealed, and claw-marked — and took off for the surface. Sisyphus rolled his shoulders, set his hands against the stone, and started up again, whistling.³ ³ Crow maintained the whistling was the strangest part of the whole arrangement, and he had once delivered mail to the Fates.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the complex prompt requirements. The author successfully captures Terry Pratchett's distinctive style through footnotes, understated humor, and wry observations about bureaucracy and human nature. The epistolary format works brilliantly, allowing Sisyphus's wisdom to emerge through his responses rather than exposition. The characterization of Sisyphus is masterful - he's genuinely warm and insightful without being preachy, and his advice feels earned through experience rather than philosophical posturing. The prompt's specific requirement to avoid obvious boulder metaphors is honored, with only one subtle reference ("the doing is the life. The done is just the souvenir") that feels natural rather than forced. Crow serves as an excellent foil, providing comic relief and practical grounding. The dynamic between them feels authentic and lived-in. The advice letters range from mundane (dishwasher loading) to intimate (marital relations) to mysterious (the rain-damaged letter), creating variety while maintaining coherence. The prose is elegant and economical, with Pratchettesque touches like "landed on his shoulder, dropped the mailbag, and coughed up a brass button" and the footnote about Crow preferring "editorial consultant." The humor emerges naturally from situations and character interactions rather than forced jokes. The piece shows rather than tells Sisyphus's transformation into a wise counselor. His advice is genuinely practical and insightful - particularly the responses about work boundaries and dealing with difficult people. The final letter about life's meaning allows for deeper philosophical reflection while maintaining the established tone. The ending strikes the right note - Sisyphus returning to his boulder while whistling, suggesting contentment without forced positivity. The transformation feels earned through the accumulated evidence of his wisdom and genuine care for others. Minor weaknesses include some advice responses that could be slightly more distinctive, and the piece occasionally borders on being too neat in its resolution of the central concept. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Coming of age: Lost & Found in Osaka — Score: 81.2
Prompt:
Lost & Found in Osaka Slice of Life / Coming-of-Age: First Connection. Write one scene within a larger story set in Osaka, Japan. A shy, introverted teenager & synthesizer nerd, is browsing the electronics district of Den Den Town. She is drawn to the sound of an electric guitar. A girl, slightly older and radiating confidence, is busking near the entrance to a crowded arcade, playing a blistering rendition of a classic anime theme. The scene takes us through their initial awkward interaction, transitioning into a surprisingly easy conversation. The busker, intrigued by the teenager's quiet intensity and knowledge of music, suggests they grab some sushi at a nearby kaitenzushi (conveyor belt sushi) restaurant. Write primarily in dialogue, capturing the distinct voices of the two characters: the teenager's hesitant and -- initially -- awkward speech, and the busker's more outgoing and playful tone. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Lost & Found in Osaka Den Den Town in July smelled like hot asphalt, takoyaki sauce, and the warm dust blowing out of arcade doors. Nao clutched her Joshin bag to her chest — a secondhand Volca Keys, her treasure for the month — and let the crowd carry her past Super Potato, past the gacha machines, past nothing she planned to stop for. Then she heard the guitar. It cut through the arcade jingles like a knife through static — *A Cruel Angel's Thesis*, but faster, meaner, the bridge played with a harmony line nobody ever bothered with. Nao stopped walking. People flowed around her. She didn't care. The girl on the amp was maybe eighteen, all elbows and grin, a battered sunburst Strat slung low, playing like the song owed her money. When the last note rang out and died, Nao clapped — a half-second too late, alone in the space between songs. "Whoa, careful," the girl said. "Any more enthusiasm and I'll have to file paperwork." "S-sorry. Um. It was—" "Was?" "...Good. Really good. The—the bridge. You played the harmony line. Everybody skips it." The girl sat back on her amp and stared. "Okay. Wow. Hello. Do you know how many people a day stop here? Fifty. They film the chorus and leave. You're the first person all summer who waited for the bridge. I was starting to take it personally." "I didn't—I mean—" "Who are you." "N-Nao." "Nao." She tried the name like a chord. "Kana. Second year, no fixed genre, kind of a big deal within sight of this vending machine." Nao dropped her coins in the open guitar case. "Ooh, and a patron. Go on, Professor. Give me the full review." "It's not—" Nao's face went hot. "The second verse. You rushed the pickup a little. But like the '95 live version, so it felt... intentional? Sorry, that's probably—" "Do not finish that sentence with 'weird.'" "...Annoying?" "Worse. It was *accurate.*" Kana leaned forward, elbows on knees. "Do you play?" "Not—um. Not guitar." Nao lifted the Joshin bag an inch. "I just got this. A Volca. Keys." "A what?" "A synthesizer. Analog, three voices, polyphonic—" She caught herself. "Sorry." "Your whole face just changed." "What?" "The thing. Where you become a completely different person." Kana flicked the guitar's volume knob with her thumb. "Do it again. Say more synth words." "I—okay. Um." A breath. "It has a sixteen-step sequencer, so you can loop patterns, and I've been trying to recreate the brass stab from the intro — the theme, the one you were playing. It uses a really fast envelope decay, and the Volca's filter isn't a ladder filter, so you have to fake the resonance by—" She stopped. "Sorry." "Stop saying sorry! That's the most interesting thing anyone's said to me all week and I understood maybe a third of it." "That was... the boring version." "Then I *demand* the interesting version." Kana squinted at her. "Where are you from? You talk like you're apologizing to the air. Tokyo?" "Osaka. Um. Fukushima ward." "Osaka! And still this shy? *Akan.* We'll fix that." She pointed at the bag. "Show me." "What? Here?" "Yeah, here. It's Den Den Town. A guy walked past me an hour ago wearing a full mecha helmet. Nobody will blink." Nao's hands were shaking, but her hands always did the thing before her brain gave permission. She set the Volca on the edge of the amp, flipped the power, hit a key. The brass stab blurted out of the tiny speaker, distorted and heroic. Kana barked a laugh. "That's terrible! It's terrible and I love it. Do the melody!" "It's not finished, the patch is—" "Nao. Melody." Nao played the melody. Twenty seconds in, Kana yanked the Strat over her head. "Okay, okay, okay — wait—" The world exploded. Both of them ducked as the first firework cracked over the street, then three more, white and red, rattling every window on the block. A car alarm started screaming. The arcade crowd cheered like this was scheduled. "Wh-what—!?" Nao had her hands over her ears. "PACHINKO PARLOR!" Kana yelled, pointing across the street. "ANNIVERSARY! OR A COUP! HARD TO SAY!" Another barrage. Gold sparks rained behind the neon signs. Nao looked at Kana, Kana looked at Nao, and something about the absurdity of it — the smoke, the alarm, the abandoned guitar — punched a laugh out of Nao before she could stop it. "IS THAT A LAUGH?" Kana shouted. "I CAN'T TELL! IT'S VERY LOUD!" Silence came back all at once, the way it does after fireworks — enormous, ringing. Smoke drifted between them. Nao realized she was still grinning. "Anyway," Kana said, calm as anything. "That was my outro. I charge extra for pyro." She started coiling her cable. "Okay. I can't compete with explosives, and you know the bridge harmony, so. You hungry?" "I—um—" "There's a kaitenzushi place in the basement of that building. Plates start at a hundred thirty yen, and the tamago is actually good, which is how you know a place is *serious.*" She snapped the guitar case shut around the coins. "My treat. Well — technically your coins. So really, you're treating me. Thanks, Nao." "That's—I don't think that's how—" Nao gave up. "...Okay." "Okay? Whole sentence, no apology. Progress." Kana hoisted the amp, then paused and held out the Strat. "Carry this. Don't drop it." "I'll—um—" "You'll be fine. It's tougher than it looks." A grin. "Same as you." Nao took the guitar like it was a baby bird and fell into step beside her. "So," Kana said, as they descended toward the basement restaurant, toward the little conveyor belt glowing under fluorescent light. "A ladder filter. Is that a real thing, or did you sneeze earlier?" "It's—it shapes the harmonics. It's from Moog. It's—" Nao stopped. "You don't care." "I care about how you talk about it." Kana pulled the door open, and cool air and the smell of vinegar rice rolled out to meet them. "Tell it to the salmon, Professor. First round's on you."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates two distinct character voices - Nao's hesitant, apologetic speech patterns contrasted with Kana's confident, playful tone. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple functions: character development, world-building, and plot advancement. The setting of Den Den Town is vividly rendered through sensory details - the smell of takoyaki sauce, the sound of arcade jingles, the visual chaos of neon and crowds. The author shows strong restraint in description, allowing the environment to emerge through character interaction rather than exposition dumps. Character development is particularly well-handled. Nao's transformation from extreme shyness to cautious engagement feels organic, triggered by finding someone who appreciates her niche knowledge. The moment where she becomes animated discussing synthesizers is a perfect example of showing character depth through passion. Kana's character avoids the trap of being merely "the confident one" - her genuine interest in Nao's expertise and her playful but not condescending manner create depth. The fireworks sequence serves as an effective catalyst, providing external chaos that mirrors and releases internal tension. This moment allows both characters to share genuine laughter and creates a natural transition to the invitation. The prose style is clean and efficient, with occasional flourishes that enhance rather than distract ("cut through the arcade jingles like a knife through static"). The pacing moves well between dialogue and action, maintaining momentum throughout. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The ending, while sweet, edges toward predictable territory - the confident girl taking the shy girl under her wing is a familiar dynamic. The transformation, while well-executed, happens perhaps slightly too smoothly. Some of Kana's dialogue occasionally borders on being too perfectly supportive, though it generally stays within believable bounds. The technical music details add authenticity without becoming overwhelming, and the author handles the cultural setting with apparent familiarity, incorporating Japanese elements naturally rather than as exotic decoration. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
