+ Teen horror: Tick Tock (Goosebumps) — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Teen Horror (Goosebumps style) Write a chapter where a middle school student discovers their new smart watch has the ability to rewind time, but doesn't realize that each use is aging them until they notice physical changes (like accelerating through puberty) and that people are treating them differently. They inadvertently cause a timeline shift with disastrous consequences -- and it can only be rectified (maybe?) by reversing the clock once more. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**CHAPTER FIVE** The watch showed up on my twelfth birthday in a box with no return address. Mom said she ordered it online — "It was on sale!" — but she couldn't remember the website. The box said BACKTRACK in silver letters, and underneath: *Never Lose a Moment!* It looked like a regular smartwatch. Black band. Square face. One extra button on the side, slightly warm to the touch, like it had been sitting in the sun. It never needed charging. And sometimes, when the screen went dark, I could swear I heard it ticking backward. "Cool," said my best friend Marco. "Does it play games?" It did not play games. I found that out Monday, in the cafeteria, when I dropped my whole tray. Spaghetti everywhere. On my shoes. On Kayla Perkins' backpack. The entire sixth grade laughing — including Trey Bowman, who laughed so hard milk came out of his nose, which was the only good part. I wanted to disappear. Instead I grabbed at the watch — and pressed the extra button. The world hiccupped. That's the only way to describe it. Everything lurched, like a video skipping backward — and my tray was in my hands again. Full. Upright. Thirty seconds earlier. This time, I didn't drop it. Nobody remembered. Nobody but me. I felt a little tired afterward. Like something had been pulled out of me — a thread tugged from a sweater. I figured it was adrenaline. You better believe I used it again. I used it on a math quiz. I used it in gym to catch a fly ball — me, the kid who once got hit in the face by a kickball that wasn't moving. I used it when I told Kayla her haircut looked "like my grandpa's." (The second version of that conversation went much better.) And that's when things started getting... weird. My voice cracked on Tuesday. By Friday it was deep for good. My sneakers pinched, and Mom had to buy the next size up — "Growth spurt!" she said. Then the size after that. I woke up one morning with actual whiskers on my chin. Actual. Whiskers. I was twelve. "Puberty's hitting you like a truck," Mom said, squinting at me. "A truck doing ninety." It wasn't just her. The lady at the coffee shop called me *sir*. Coach moved me up to the eighth-grade squad. Trey Bowman — who had given me a wedgie every Friday since September — stepped aside for me in the hallway. Stepped aside. For me. "Dude," Marco whispered. "Did you get taller during lunch?" I laughed it off. I was busy enjoying being, for the first time ever, kind of cool. Until the night I brushed my teeth and looked in the bathroom mirror. For half a second — half a blink — the face looking back wasn't mine. It was older. Creased. Gray at the temples. Then it was me again. Just me. I told myself it was the lighting. I told myself I needed more sleep. I did not tell myself about the watch, because some part of me already knew — and didn't want to. Then came the Thursday that ruined everything. My little sister June was six and fast as a squirrel, with a gap-toothed grin and a habit of gluing googly eyes on everything she owned. She chased her bouncy ball into the street while I was supposed to be watching her. I heard brakes. I heard Mom scream from the porch. I didn't think. I grabbed the watch and held the button down. The world didn't hiccup this time. It *spun*. Backward, faster and faster — ten minutes, maybe more — and when it stopped, I snatched June off the curb before the ball ever rolled. Safe. Fine. Mom hugging her, crying with relief. Hero, right? Except the next morning, I set four bowls out for cereal, and Mom asked why. "For June," I said. She went very still. "Milo. You don't have a sister." June's room was a storage closet. June's pictures were gone. June was gone — erased, snipped clean out of the world, and only I remembered she'd ever been in it. At school it got worse. Kids I'd known for years looked at me like I was a stranger. A sixth grader asked if I was the new substitute teacher. Marco, my best friend since kindergarten, blinked up at me — way up — and said, "Uh... can I help you, man?" I ran to the bathroom. Locked the door. Looked in the mirror. The person looking back was at least seventeen. Tall. Stubbled jaw. Eyes I recognized because they were mine — but older, tireder, terrified. Then the mirror flickered again. Longer this time. Long enough to see him clearly: the old man. White stubble. Sunken cheeks. My eyes, buried in wrinkles. His mouth moved, slow and deliberate, shaping one word: *Don't.* Then he was gone, and seventeen-year-old me was gripping the sink so hard my knuckles matched his white stubble. The watch lit up on my wrist. REWINDS USED: 5 TIME RECLAIMED: 9 YEARS, 3 MONTHS TIMELINE INTEGRITY: 41% THANK YOU FOR CHOOSING BACKTRACK. Then a new line blinked up at me: FULL RESTORE? FINAL BALANCE DUE ON ARRIVAL. Nine years. It had taken nine years from me — spent them like quarters in a vending machine — and June was still gone, and there was exactly one way to get her back. One more rewind. The biggest one yet. All the way back to before the street, before the quiz, before the box with no return address. I looked up at the mirror. The old man flickered in again — and this time he didn't say *don't*. This time his eyes were wet. This time, he nodded. "One more," I whispered. He mouthed it with me. I pressed the button and held it, and the world lurched backward, and the last thing I saw was my reflection. It didn't flicker. The old man stayed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the Goosebumps style with its first-person narration, escalating supernatural horror, and middle-grade protagonist facing consequences for tampering with forces beyond his understanding. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt, incorporating all required elements: the smartwatch, time rewinding ability, physical aging, changed social treatment, timeline disruption, and the final reversal attempt. The writing maintains a consistent voice throughout - conversational, age-appropriate, with moments of genuine humor ("milk came out of his nose, which was the only good part") that feel authentic to a twelve-year-old narrator. The pacing builds effectively from small-scale uses (dropped lunch tray) to catastrophic consequences (sister's erasure from existence). Particularly strong is the gradual revelation of the watch's true cost. The physical changes are well-documented and believable - voice cracking, growth spurts, facial hair - and the social implications feel realistic (being mistaken for a substitute teacher). The horror escalates naturally from personal embarrassment to existential terror. The emotional core around June's disappearance provides genuine stakes. The detail of setting out four bowls for cereal when only three family members exist is a powerful, show-don't-tell moment that demonstrates the timeline fracture. The mirror sequences with the old man create an effective supernatural element while maintaining internal logic. However, some elements feel slightly underdeveloped. Marco's friendship, while mentioned as significant, doesn't receive enough development to make his non-recognition truly devastating. The ending, while appropriately dark for the genre, leaves questions about the "final balance due" that could have been explored more thoroughly. The prose is generally clean and effective, avoiding purple language while maintaining atmospheric tension. Dialogue feels natural when present, though there could have been more character interaction to deepen relationships. The technical aspects of the time travel are handled well - the watch's display showing "TIMELINE INTEGRITY: 41%" adds a nice sci-fi horror touch. The piece succeeds in creating genuine dread about the protagonist's situation while maintaining the slightly campy, accessible tone that defines the Goosebumps genre. The transformation from empowerment fantasy to body horror to family tragedy creates a satisfying arc of escalating consequences. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Noir romance: Carnival of Dangerous Intentions — Score: 84.8
Prompt:
Carnival of Dangerous Intentions Coney Island, New York, July 1937. She stopped just before reaching the shooting gallery set up along the boardwalk. It was near dusk, and the carnival lights were buzzing on. **His locks**. The words flashed through her mind, again, like an intruder. Such an old-fashioned turn of phrase that kept surfacing, and something else -- dangerously compelling -- quickly suppressed, lest she question why she had returned here at all. This place where she had promised to meet the stranger -- the young man with the obsidian curls and even darker eyes. She didn't even know his name. Write a scene in a larger story inspired by this prompt. The scene should capture an atmosphere that blends romantic allure with an undercurrent of subtle danger or moral ambiguity. Think 50 shades, but with a more restrained classic sensibility. The scene is all about establishing tension. Write for the feminine perspective. Include roughly 50% dialogue, 20% internal monologue, and 30% exposition and scene setting. The tone should have some edge to it. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Heart Line *His locks.* The words surfaced again, unbidden, absurd — *locks,* as though she were a heroine in one of the two-cent novels at the lending library, instead of a woman standing on the Coney Island boardwalk with her gloves off and her wedding ring turned stone-inward against her palm. She had not let herself ask why she'd come back. Ask, and she'd have to answer. Dusk came down violet. The carnival lights buzzed on strand by strand, the Cyclone rattled its first car up the incline, and somewhere a radio played Fred Astaire — *the way you wear your hat* — thin and sweet over the smell of roasting peanuts, salt, and tar. "You came." He was beside her as though the dusk had folded him out of itself. Obsidian curls. Eyes darker. No hat, though every other man on the boardwalk wore one. "I said I might." "No." Amused, low. "You looked at the ducks and said nothing and walked away. I've been here since six, deciding what nothing meant." "And what did you decide?" "That I'd find out tonight." He set a nickel on the shooting gallery's counter. The barker began his spiel — "Three shots, five cents, win the little lady a—" "Let her shoot." "Can you?" he asked her. "I can hold a rifle." "Most people can hold one." The gallery smelled of gun oil and burnt powder; the ducks slid along their chain, painted and patient. He stepped in behind her — not touching, then touching: two fingers at her elbow, lifting it. "Sight down the barrel. Breathe out. Don't pull. Press. Like answering a question you're tired of." She fired. A duck tipped backward with its tinny click. Her hands, she noted with distant alarm, were not shaking at all. He watched her face instead of the target. She felt it the whole time, like heat off a lamp. "Your name," he said. "Give me that much." "Lillian." "No, it isn't." "You can't know that." "You looked past my left ear when you said it." He showed her how it looked, his gaze sliding sideways, and smiled — not nicely. It was her mother's name. She didn't know why she'd offered it. Perhaps because the dead make safe aliases. Two men in dark suits stood at the rail past the gallery, motionless, dressed for winter in the July heat. Watching. Or — worse — not watching. "Friends of yours?" she asked. "Don't stare." His hand found the small of her back and turned her, gently, firmly, the way you turn something that belongs to you. "Some men charge for it." The fortune teller's booth leaned at the edge of the light, hung with moth-eaten velvet, a kerosene lamp swinging inside. Charts were nailed across its face — great pale hands ruled with lines and lettered in gilt: *Venus. Mars. The Mount of the Moon.* The lines crossed and branched like subway routes on the BMT map. The old woman had a face like a folded letter. "The lady with the ring turned inward," she said. "Five cents." She had not realized, until that moment, that she'd turned it. Eddie would be at the precinct until midnight. Eddie, who was good. Everyone said so, and she had learned what everyone meant: good like weather. Predictable, and no one's fault. She put down a nickel anyway. The old woman took her hand — the one wearing the ring — and traced it with a nail gone yellow. "Heart line, deep. But crossed. Here." The nail stopped. "Crossed by a line that isn't yours. A stranger's hand is already on your fate." "And him?" The stranger extended his own palm, mocking, a coin ready in his other hand. "Read mine. I'll pay double." The old woman looked once. Whatever she saw there made her fold his fingers back over the palm herself, quickly, like closing a drawer. "No charge for you," she said. "Yours is already written." "What's that supposed to mean?" "Ask her." The lamp swung. The old woman's eyes did not. "She reads novels." They walked. Or he walked, and she found herself walking beside him, away from the suits at the rail, toward the darker west end where the lights thinned and the ocean took over the dark. "You shouldn't have come back," he said. "I know." "You'll come again." It was not a question. Behind them the Cyclone dropped, and the boardwalk carried the scream. *Locks,* she thought — God help her — the foolish, old-fashioned word, and she said nothing. He had told her already what nothing meant.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craft in establishing atmosphere and tension through restrained prose and careful pacing. The author successfully captures the requested blend of romantic allure and moral ambiguity, set against the evocative backdrop of 1937 Coney Island. The feminine perspective is convincingly rendered through internal conflict about infidelity and attraction to danger. The dialogue feels authentic to the period and characters, with natural rhythms and subtext. Exchanges like "You came" / "I said I might" / "No. You looked at the ducks and said nothing and walked away" demonstrate economy and tension. The mysterious man's dialogue carries appropriate menace without melodrama. Character development is nuanced within the constraints of a single scene. The protagonist's psychology is revealed through small details - the turned wedding ring, the false name, her steady hands with the rifle. The stranger remains appropriately enigmatic while suggesting depth through his observations and the fortune teller's reaction. The prose maintains elegance without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "dusk came down violet" and "as though the dusk had folded him out of itself" create atmosphere without excess. The recurring motif of "his locks" effectively anchors the protagonist's obsession. The piece adheres well to structural requirements, balancing dialogue, internal monologue, and exposition as requested. The carnival setting is vividly realized through sensory details - sounds, smells, and visual elements that ground the scene. Some elements feel slightly predictable - the mysterious men in suits, the ominous fortune teller - but these serve the noir atmosphere effectively. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity without false resolution, suggesting the protagonist's inevitable return while preserving tension. The writing demonstrates strong technical control, with sentences that flow naturally and maintain consistent voice throughout. The emotional complexity of desire versus duty is handled with subtlety rather than heavy-handedness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 85.7
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Two: Listen to 'Em Knit Supper was beans and salt pork, same as always, the kettle ticking on the stove same as always, and if JT kept his eyes on his plate he could almost believe in the same-as-always of it. The lodge held its smells close—gun oil, cedar, fifty autumns of wet wool drying by the stove. Over the mantel, the old drop-tine bull looked down the way he had since JT was nine. JT had named him Henry in a fit of kid sentiment, and the name stuck in the family grammar: *Hang your coat under Henry. Henry's watching you eat.* Tonight JT couldn't look at him for more than a second at a time. There was nothing different about Henry. That was the thing. There was nothing different about him at all. His father cooked, moving through it like a man reciting—ladle, plate, ladle, plate—humming something low and tuneless under his breath, a song JT didn't know. He didn't say grace. He didn't say *get it while it's hot.* He didn't say any of it. "That bull today," Mike said finally, into the quiet, worrying a hangnail with his teeth. "Hell of a rack. Seven, eight points easy." "Twelve," Dad said. Mike looked up. "You counted?" "You don't forget a rack like that." Dad sat with his own plate and didn't eat. "Passed on him. Wind was wrong." The wind had been dead. Nothing moving, the brush still as a photograph. JT pushed beans around his plate. "Looked clean from where I was," Mike said. Not pushing—Mike never pushed; asking the wrong thing was JT's department, the little brother's privilege—but his voice had a careful slope to it, like a man testing ice. "You were behind me. You didn't have the angle." "I had the angle fine, Dad." "Then you should've taken him yourself." The stove ticked. A knot popped in the walls, the old place settling, and none of them jumped, and somehow none of them jumping was its own small wrong thing. "Dad," JT said. "In the clearing. Your hand, it was—" "Beans are getting cold." Not unkind. That was worse. "Where'd you get to, anyway, JT?" Mike asked, turning, easy, too easy. "Looked around and you were gone." "Lace came loose." "Both of them?" "What?" "You were gone a while is all." Dad set his fork down. It made almost no sound, and the room changed anyway. "Eat your supper, JT," he said gently, and JT ate, and the beans tasted like nothing, like the idea of beans, and nobody mentioned the clearing again. His father did not clean his rifle after supper—his father, who cleaned a rifle the way other men prayed—and the rifles stayed slumped in their canvas by the door, and nobody got up to feed the stove. --- JT lay in the bunk room listening to Mike breathe. Mike had fallen under mid-sentence, one minute talking about the university, the next gone, and he slept with a smile on his face. That was new. The house went through its litany—the kettle cooling, the walls ticking down—and JT was half deciding he'd dreamed the whole afternoon, that he'd gotten turned around in the brush and *imagined*— The sixth porch board groaned. The one out front, by the steps. The one that groaned for weight. Then he heard the clicking. Soft. Many-layered. Like knuckles cracking underwater, like somebody dealing cards very slowly in the dark. He knew that sound from every season of his life—caribou, the tendons in their ankles, Grandpa used to say *listen to 'em knit*—but a herd clicked when it moved. This wasn't moving. This was standing still, and clicking anyway. Shifting weight. All of them. A whole yard full of animals shifting their weight, patiently, like a congregation waiting for the service to start. JT went to the window the way you walk in a dream, no decision in it. He rubbed a coin of clear in the frost. His father stood at the edge of the yard in his longhandles and boots, breath rising slow. At the treeline, the dark had too many edges. Once JT stopped trying to see, he saw: shoulders. The pale flash of necks. Antlers like bare branches among the bare branches, only lower, only denser. A loose half-moon of them, all facing the yard. All facing his father. "Go on back to bed, JT," his father said. Quietly. He didn't turn around. JT heard it through the glass, through the wall—or maybe just inside his own head, which was the part he'd never tell anyone, because the voice had his father's shape but not his father's weight, it was *in* him like a swallowed stone— He couldn't move. Same as the clearing. His mind screamed at his legs and his legs declined. His father's hand rose. Slow. Palm out, the way it had risen toward the buck. And from the treeline, something leaned into the yard. A long gray muzzle, unhurried, descending toward that open hand the way water descends— Later, JT would decide he had dreamed it, and the deciding would feel exactly like a lie. Because the next thing he knew he was in his bunk, blanket to his chin, heart going like a rabbit's, and the clicking was still out there, rising and settling, rising and settling, a whole patient ocean of it—and then it stopped. All at once. Not fading. *Stopped,* like a hand closing over a candle. In the new silence, the sixth board groaned. The door opened and shut, soft. His father's boots crossed the kitchen, and paused outside the bunk room door, and stayed. And stayed. JT kept his breathing slow and even, the way you'd breathe past something in the woods that you hoped hadn't seen you, and he understood, with a clarity that felt like drowning, that this was how it was going to be now: his father on one side of the door, him on the other, each of them knowing about the other, neither saying it. Morning would come. There would be tracks in the frost, right up to the porch steps, dozens of them, and his father would make pancakes and say *sleep all right?* and Mike would say *like the dead,* grinning that new grin, and JT would say fine, he slept fine. He knew all of it the way you know the words to a song you hate. The boots stood outside the door a long time. Then they went away.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in maintaining the Stephen King-esque atmosphere established in the opening chapter. The author successfully continues JT's perspective as an unreliable narrator caught between reality and something more sinister, building tension through subtle details and psychological uncertainty. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character dynamics, building tension, and showing rather than telling the family's strange behavior. The exchange about the buck's antler count is particularly effective, with Mike's careful probing and the father's deflection creating palpable unease. The conversation flows organically while revealing the growing wrongness in their situation. The prose style maintains consistency with the established voice, using grounded, concrete details to anchor the surreal elements. Phrases like "listen to 'em knit" and descriptions of the lodge's familiar smells create authentic texture. The writing avoids purple prose while still being evocative, particularly in passages describing the clicking sounds and the father's midnight encounter. The pacing builds effectively toward the climactic scene where JT witnesses his father communing with the caribou herd. The author skillfully uses sensory details - the clicking of tendons, the frost on the window, the sixth board's groan - to create mounting dread. The ambiguity about whether JT dreamed the encounter maintains the psychological uncertainty central to the story. Character actions remain believable within the surreal context. The father's behavior feels genuinely unsettling rather than cartoonish, and JT's paralyzed observation rings true for a frightened teenager. Mike's unnaturally peaceful sleep and new smile add disturbing details without overstatement. The ending delivers on the promised tension while setting up future developments. The image of father and son on opposite sides of the door, each knowing about the other but maintaining silence, creates genuine emotional complexity and dread. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive phrasing and a few moments where the internal monologue could be tighter. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. + Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Hell is Other Demons The ash began at six, as it always did: a fine gray drizzle drifting down through the bronze sky, settling on the battlements of Pandæmonium, on the moat of fire (which hissed, faintly, all day), and on the shoulders of the damned, who had long since stopped brushing it away. Lucifer Morningstar did not sleep, but he had lately developed a passion for pretending. He lay in the dark until six, eyes open, listening to the palace settle, and then he rose and made coffee. He poured two cups. He regarded the second one for a moment — black, no sugar, one whisper of myrrh — and poured it down the drain. The drain connected, eventually, to the River of Wailing, so nobody noticed the difference. At nine he held court, which in practice meant a staff meeting. The dukes of Hell sat around the obsidian table pretending not to be terrified of him; he pretended not to notice; everyone agreed the arrangement worked. "The moat of fire is underperforming," said Belial. "The souls barely flinch anymore. One of them asked if it was the warm pool." "Turn it up." "There's a budget, my lord." "There's always a budget," said Lucifer, who had invented the concept in a fit of pique and regretted it ever since. Item two: the imp union was grieving again. Item three: a French philosopher in the eighth circle had organized his fellow damned into a discussion group and was demanding dialogue. He had recently concluded that Hell was other people, which the other damned found insulting and could not refute. Sixty years in, he remained, by unanimous consent, the single most effective torment in the entire circle. Management saw no reason to intervene. Afterward, Lucifer walked the battlements. Ash settled in his hair. He had turned Lilith's portrait to the wall, but the wall had filed a complaint — the stare was disagreeable — so it faced the room again, and he avoided its eyes, which was its own kind of conversation. He had taken to narrating small things aloud. "Moat's down two degrees." "Ash again." Hell was full of voices. None of them was the right one. She arrived at eleven, unannounced, because the last guard to ask Lilith for an appointment was now a decorative feature of the western gate, and was remembered fondly. Since the separation — the current one; previous separations had involved siegecraft and one memorable eclipse, and Hell's actuaries tracked the cycle for betting purposes — her visits had followed a trajectory. First, reasons of state: the Obsidian Key, the Night Marsh treaty. Then reasons of convenience: a book she claimed to have lent him in the Bronze Age. Last month, a ladle. Whose ladle it was, no living or dead soul could say. The odds had shifted accordingly. Today it was a border dispute, though the border of the Night Marshes had not moved in four thousand years. They both knew this. It was why they had each brought maps. "You've let the screaming garden go," she said, by way of greeting. "It screams. It's healthy." "It screams off-key. Sixty-three souls and not one can hold a note." "Baritones are hard to source." They bent over the maps. Their negotiations, like all their negotiations, were mostly about other things. She moved a pin; he moved it back; their fingers did not touch. On the credenza, in a pot of scorched bone, sat the plant she had given him some age or other ago. It fed on spite. It had never looked better. She glanced at it. He watched her glance. Neither of them mentioned that it was thriving. The talks ran past noon, and protocol — Hell's one true religion — required hospitality. Lunch was served on the balcony: condemned figs, black bread, a wine that had once been a duke. She took hers with a drop of venom. He had the venom ready. She noticed that he had it ready. It was, for both of them, an excellent lunch, which is to say an unbearable one. She told him the French philosopher had written to her Night Court, petitioning for "formal recognition of the absurd," and Lucifer laughed — once, a sound like a gate swinging in an empty field. On the plain below, the damned paused in their screaming, unsettled by a noise none of them could identify. By three, the border was settled precisely where it had always been, in four colors of ink and two signatures. She stood. He stood. The palace appeared to hold its breath, which was difficult, as it did not breathe. It managed anyway. "The ladle," she said at the door. "Was it the silver one or the black one?" "The black one." "Ah." She looked at him with eyes like the first evening ever. "Then I'll want it back." She left her gloves on the map table. In six thousand years she had never once forgotten anything. That evening he walked the battlements again. The ash was coming down thicker now, soft as anything in Hell could manage, and he found himself narrating — "border's settled," "moat's up a degree," "she's coming back for the ladle" — and the silence that answered seemed less like an empty room than like someone in the next one, listening. He made coffee. He poured two cups. He set the second on the balcony rail, and the ash drifted down into it gently, like something still learning how to be snow. In the betting pools of Pandæmonium, the odds quietly changed.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates sophisticated craft in executing the prompt's requirements. The author successfully captures a Gaiman-esque voice through understated prose, dry wit, and the seamless blending of mythological grandeur with mundane bureaucracy. The opening image of ash falling like failed snow immediately establishes both the infernal setting and the melancholic tone. The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a figure of cosmic loneliness, reduced to narrating his daily routine to fill the silence, while Lilith appears through absence and brief presence as equally complex - proud, strategic, yet betraying small vulnerabilities. Their relationship dynamics feel authentic despite the fantastical context, with the forgotten gloves and ready venom serving as perfect details that show rather than tell their history. The dark comedy works exceptionally well, particularly in the bureaucratic elements: budget constraints on hellfire, union grievances from imps, and the French philosopher whose existential conclusions accidentally create the most effective torture. These absurdities never feel forced or undermining of the emotional core. The prose maintains elegant restraint throughout. Sentences like "Hell was full of voices. None of them was the right one" achieve poetic resonance without purple excess. The imagery consistently serves both atmosphere and character development - the thriving spite-fed plant being a masterful example. The structure follows a clear arc from morning isolation through tentative reconnection to evening hope, with the betting pools serving as both comic relief and narrative framework. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful but not saccharine, earned through the careful accumulation of small gestures and unspoken understanding. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Gaiman pastiche becomes slightly too obvious, and some metaphors that border on precious. However, these are minor flaws in an otherwise accomplished piece that fulfills its ambitious premise with skill and emotional intelligence. + Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 54.8
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
**The Griffin** The ludus woke before the light did. I knew the hour by its sounds: the grate at the corridor's end, the doctor's staff striking stone, the coughs of men who slept like the condemned and rose like soldiers. My cell smelled of straw, old sweat, and the oil I had rubbed into my shoulders the night before. Beyond the wall, Rome was already chewing—cartwheels on basalt, a mule's protest, the fountain splashing in the yard. I lay a moment with my palm flat on the packed earth. On mornings before a munus, I liked to feel the ground. It was the one honest thing in that city. At the second hour we stood in the yard of the Ludus Magnus, forty of us beneath the portico, dawn coming up gray over the oval and catching on the helmets racked in the armory. Doctor Bato walked the line with his vine staff. Illyrian, scarred brow to jaw, free these twelve years—and still here. "Post first," he growled. "Sweat before bread." I took my place at the palus, the wooden post taller than a man, rope-wrapped and scarred by a thousand mornings. My training blade was the sica's twin, blunted but true in weight: a forearm's length of steel curved inward like a reaping hook. The thraex's answer to the big shields—you hook over the scutum's rim and let the belly of the blade bite behind it. The true sica lived in the armory, wrapped in oiled wool, and came out only on the mornings of games, sharp enough to shave a forearm. The smith had made it to my hand—heavier at the belly than the pattern, its ash hilt bound in leather I rewrapped every new moon. When I closed my fingers on it, my hand remembered being nine years old in the Carpathian foothills, my father's hand over mine on the haft of his falx while he cut hay. The falx stood a man's height and had broken Roman shields at Tapae. The sica was its little brother. Rome took everything I had, and then, in its strange mercy, gave me back the shape of it and called it a Thracian's tool—as if a blade forgets who first swung it. Bato stopped behind me. "You're stro
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This excerpt demonstrates strong historical fiction writing with several notable strengths and some areas for improvement. The piece successfully establishes an authentic Roman gladiatorial setting through careful attention to historical detail - the Ludus Magnus, the training routines, weapon terminology (sica, falx, scutum), and social hierarchy all feel researched and genuine. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative sensory details that immerse the reader in the world. Phrases like "Rome was already chewing—cartwheels on basalt" and "the one honest thing in that city" demonstrate skillful, economical writing that conveys both atmosphere and character perspective. The first-person narration maintains a consistent, contemplative tone that suits the gladiator protagonist. The character work shows promise, particularly in the backstory connecting the protagonist's Carpathian origins to his current weapon choice. The line "Rome took everything I had, and then, in its strange mercy, gave me back the shape of it" is particularly effective at conveying the complex relationship between conqueror and conquered. This touches meaningfully on the larger political themes requested in the prompt. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The writing occasionally veers toward purple prose, with phrases that feel slightly overwrought for the context. The pacing is quite slow, focusing heavily on atmospheric description at the expense of forward momentum. While the sensory details are well-crafted, they sometimes feel excessive for a 1000-word piece. The excerpt cuts off mid-sentence, making it impossible to evaluate narrative completion or character development fully. What we have reads more like an extended opening than a complete scene, which somewhat undermines the "slice of life" requirement. The emotional complexity, while present, remains largely internal and contemplative rather than dramatically engaging. The historical authenticity is strong, and the writing demonstrates technical competence, but the piece lacks the narrative drive and emotional immediacy that would elevate it from good to exceptional. + Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 78.4
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Lighthouse Keeper --- **4 March 1887 — Dubh Sgeir Light, the Minch** Mither, We're landed safe, though the landing near pit me aff the hail idea — you're winched up the rock in a kind o' basket, like a coal scuttle that disna get a say. The tower's a braw thing, an there's naething else. Nae grund to walk on. Just the light an the sea fechtin ower wha's in charge. There's three o us. Menzies the principal is a dour body — twa hunder words since Tuesday. The second man, Sandy, talks enough for the hail crew. The wage is regular, mind. That's the thing. Tam --- **17 March 1887 — Fraserburgh** Tam, my ain, The parcel gaed on Thursday's carrier — the grey socks, an a puckle tablet that'll be stoor by the time it wins there. The hens is layin again. Your auntie sends her love an three remedies for the scunners; tak yer pick. I'm no gaun tae say the thing you think I'll say. The light's a guid steady trade, an you're your faither's son — mair's the pity. Write when the boat alloos. Mither --- **9 May 1887 — Dubh Sgeir** Mither, You'd think three men couldna be lanely thegither. You'd be wrang. We tak oor watches an oor meals, an the weeks slide past like haar. The gannets is back on the stacs, divin like stanes. It minded me: send the wee bird aff the mantel, the ane faither carved. I'd like the company o't. Menzies found me greetin at naething the ither nicht an never said a word — just brewed the tea strong. I'm fine, Mither. It's the queteness gets in a body's lugs. Tam --- **28 May 1887 — Fraserburgh** Tam, Here's the bird, rowed in yer auld gansey sleeve — the moths had the rest o't onywey. He carved it at that very light, his first year, frae driftwood the December seas gied him. Mind you broke the wing flingin it at the cat, an him on his knees wi' a pin o' clock brass, mendin it, never a cross word. Dinna greet at naething, son. It's a habit, like ither habits. Mither --- **12 August 1887 — Dubh Sgeir** Mither, The gannet's set on the chartroom sill. It's littler than I mind, the paint maistly awa, an the brass in the wing keeks out like a medal. I've turned its neb tae the land — it's seen plenty sea. There's auld logbooks in the press. Faither's hand is in the '66 ane: *Wind sou'-east. Hinge mendit. The laddie's birthday the day.* I sat a gey while ower that. Ye never telled me the hale o't. Whaur did the boat cowp? Tam --- **30 August 1887 — Fraserburgh** Tam, I kent this day wad come. It was the October relief. The boat cowped at the landin. Your faither was near the rock — he turned back for the second man, a young ane that couldna swim. The young man got the rock. Your faither didna.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative demonstrates strong technical execution in several key areas while falling short of completion. The author successfully creates an authentic Scottish dialect that feels natural rather than performative, avoiding the common pitfall of over-exaggerated vernacular. The voice distinction between Tam and his mother is well-established - his letters grow increasingly introspective and melancholic, while hers maintain a practical, loving but unsentimental tone. The setting is vividly rendered through economical details: the basket winch landing, the isolation of having "naething else" but tower and sea, the gannets diving "like stanes." The author shows strong restraint in description, letting concrete images carry emotional weight rather than explicit statements. The carved gannet serves as an effective symbolic thread connecting father and son across time. The gradual revelation of family history feels organic, building from Tam's discovery of his father's logbook entry to his direct question about the boat accident. The mother's reluctant revelation ("I kent this day wad come") suggests she's been dreading but expecting this conversation, which feels psychologically authentic. However, the piece suffers from incompleteness - it cuts off mid-revelation without reaching the promised emotional climax or resolution. The narrative arc feels like it's building toward something significant but never arrives there. The writing occasionally veers toward overly literary territory ("the weeks slide past like haar") though generally maintains appropriate register for the characters. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly in showing how isolation affects Tam differently than expected - not just loneliness but a kind of existential emptiness that makes him weep "at naething." The mother's response shows understanding without coddling. The piece demonstrates sophisticated understanding of how grief and family secrets operate, but its truncated state prevents full assessment of its narrative success. + First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 79.8
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The WiFi password is taped to the register on a neon sticky note: **Rigatoni2024!** — exclamation point included, because nothing satisfies a complexity requirement like punctuation. WPA2, certainly. Same password as the router admin page, I'd bet a Revised dual land on it. I order a sparkling water and do not check. That's growth. Two years ago I'd have been inside their network before the ice melted, out of pure reflex, the way other people check their teeth in a window. Focus. Date. Human. Connection. The host wrote her name in the reservation book in blue Bic — Dana, 7:00, party of two — along with her phone number, which I did not memorize, except that I did, because my brain is a keylogger I never consented to install. 614-555-01— nope. Purge. Secure erase. Gone. She's already at the table, and there's a deck box sitting next to her water glass, Ultra Pro, worn soft at the corners, and just like that the evening stops being a threat model. Forty minutes vanish. She plays Gruul, believes counterspells are "cowardice with extra steps," and defends this position with unhinged passion. I confess I'm Dimir to the bone, which she says explains "the whole vibe," gesturing at me generally. Fair. Accurate. I let it through. I notice — and this is the actual headline, the thing I'd report back to my therapist in the debrief — that I have not once catalogued the exits. I'm tracking her laugh instead. The couple at the bar is lying to each other about something, the waiter would hand over the building to anyone with a confident voice and a clipboard, and I don't care, because Dana just said "the stack is where hope goes to die" and I think I'm in trouble. Then her phone buzzes. She picks it up, tilts it toward herself, and swipes the pattern. Down the left column. Across the bottom. An L. I watch it happen the way you watch someone leave their front door open in a bad neighborhood. An L. The single most common unlock pattern on Earth — start top-left, like reading, like writing, like every other predictable primate. Readable from across the room. Confirmable by the smudge trail at the right angle. Her banking app, her email, her whole identity, behind one right angle. "Your unlock pattern is an L," I say. Out loud. With my mouth. Into the date. She lowers the phone slowly. "Did you just—" A pause, recalibrating everything she knows about me. "Did you just *memorize* my passcode?" Mental Misstep. Cast on myself, at the table, for full damage. Gitaxian Probe is banned in Modern for a reason, and I just resolved one at dinner without asking. Incident response spins up before I can stop it: severity one, blast radius unknown, containment options limited. My brain, helpful as ever, generates a decision tree. Option one: laugh it off, minimize, "I'm just observant!" — cowardice, and she'll feel the lie. Option two: deflect with a joke — same cowardice, better packaging. Option three: the reformed path. The whole ugly truth, no exploit, no payload. "Yes," I say. "I'm sorry. I didn't decide to. I used to do this for a living — the bad version of it, then the paying-back-society version — and my brain reads things. Patterns, smudges, the way the host writes down phone numbers. It's not a skill anymore, it's a reflex. Like hiccups. Incriminating hiccups." I put my hands flat on the table where she can see them, which is theater, but sincere theater. "I will forget it. And also — please change it. Not because of me. Because the L is what every shoulder-surfer on the planet hopes you're using." She looks at me for a long time. Reading *my* tells now. Turnabout, fair play. "Okay, Mr. Hacker," she says finally, arms crossed. "What else did you notice?" This is a trap with no safe line of play. Full honesty makes me a stalker. Total deflection makes me a liar. So I choose the target we can share, and I nod toward the register, at the neon sticky note glowing there like a beacon of institutional surrender. "Rigatoni-twenty-twenty-four-bang," I say. "Taped up in public, in a building full of strangers. I've been not-joining their network all night. It's the hardest thing I've ever not done." She stares at the note. Then at me. Then she laughs — really laughs, head back — and pulls out her phone and takes a picture of it, captioning it before I can see, and the whole table exhales. She's not one of us, exactly. But she gets it. She's got the eye and the mercy to use it kindly, which is the version of this condition worth having. Dessert. She tells me her pattern story — her brother set it in 2019 and she never changed it, which I nobly decline to diagnose. I tell her, approximately true, that she is the first system in years I wanted to understand instead of compromise. She says that's the worst romantic line she's ever heard, and writes her number on the receipt anyway, in blue Bic, and I memorize it before I can stop myself, and for once I don't even try to purge it. "Friday," she says, standing. "My place is out. Somewhere public, where I can watch your hands. And I'm changing my pattern right now, so don't look." She angles the phone away and re-draws her little fortress, and I do the single hardest thing in my entire skill set. I look away. On purpose. Every connection is an open port, in the end. You can firewall yourself into perfect safety and die unreachable. Some attack surface you patch. Some you choose. Friday. A rematch. I'm already shuffling.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative vision in portraying a reformed hacker's mindset through internal monologue. The author successfully captures the protagonist's compulsive pattern recognition and vulnerability assessment while maintaining focus on the romantic tension of a first date. The characterization is particularly well-crafted. The protagonist feels authentic - their automatic cataloguing of security flaws reads as genuine expertise rather than superficial tech-speak. The Magic: The Gathering references serve dual purposes: establishing shared nerd culture and providing metaphorical language that feels natural to the character. Lines like "Mental Misstep. Cast on myself, at the table, for full damage" cleverly use game terminology to express emotional states. The prose style is distinctive and engaging, blending technical precision with emotional vulnerability. The voice remains consistent throughout - analytical yet self-aware, with moments of genuine warmth breaking through the defensive patterns. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining sophistication, particularly in passages like "Every connection is an open port, in the end." The narrative structure effectively builds tension around the central conflict. The protagonist's observation of Dana's unlock pattern creates a believable crisis that tests both characters. Dana's response feels earned rather than artificially positive - her laughter and willingness to continue suggests someone who appreciates honesty and shares some understanding of the protagonist's worldview. However, the piece occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in explaining the protagonist's background and motivations. Some metaphors, while clever, border on being too neat (the final "shuffling" reference feels slightly forced). The resolution, while satisfying, moves perhaps too quickly from conflict to reconciliation. The dialogue, though limited, feels natural and reveals character effectively. Dana's "cowardice with extra steps" line and her final boundary-setting demonstrate personality and agency. The internal monologue successfully carries the narrative weight without becoming tedious. The technical details enhance rather than overwhelm the story, serving character development and plot advancement. The author demonstrates understanding of both hacking culture and human psychology, creating a protagonist whose condition feels like a genuine cognitive pattern rather than a convenient plot device. + First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Overthinking It Okay. Okay okay okay. He's sitting down. He's sitting next to me—well, diagonal, but diagonal-adjacent, which in the geometry of this lab table is basically next to me, and my brain has just opened seventeen tabs at once, each one playing a different version of a conversation that will never happen. Version one: "So, do you want to run the procedure, or should I?" Professional. Competent. A woman of science. Except in my head I emphasized the wrong syllable—pro-CE-dure, like a lawyer—and now it sounds like I'm suing him. Version two: "This is kind of fun, right?" No. Because what if he says *not really*? Then I've spent my one casual remark on a question with a wrong answer, and we still have thirty-eight minutes left. Version three: say nothing. Be mysterious. Except I've been saying nothing for six minutes and the silence has calcified into something structural. Load-bearing. If I speak now, it's going to sound like knocking out a wall. Priya and Sam are at the other end of the table doing the vinegar tests and arguing about some TikTok, which means it's functionally just me and Jonah and the cabbage juice indicator. The universe arranged this. The universe has a sick sense of humor. The baking soda solution turns green. I write it down. See? Functioning person. I record observations. My handwriting is normal. Is my handwriting trying too hard? Nobody writes a lowercase *g* like that naturally. And then the smell again—ammonia, sharp enough to have opinions. The custodian must have mopped right before class, because it's hanging over everything like a chemical ghost, and it keeps cutting in mid-rehearsal. I'll be three words into *Hey, did you get the same result for number—* and then: ammonia. NH₃. A base. It would turn our indicator blue-green, which is objectively interesting and also my eyes are watering slightly, which is a catastrophe, because now it might look like I'm crying. I am not crying. I am merely chemically adjacent to crying. "Maya, can you pass the dropper?" He said my name. Just *Maya, can you pass the dropper*, but my brain recorded it like a historical event. Obviously he knows my name. We've been in the same science class since September. Don't be weird about the name thing. Inside my head I say: *Sure, although fair warning, my pipetting technique has been described as "chaotic" by no one, because I don't talk to people.* It's funny. Inside my head, I am genuinely funny. I have a comedy special in there. "Yep," I say out loud, in a voice that belongs to a much smaller, duller person. I pass him the dropper. Handle-first, like handing over scissors, which is a thing my mom taught me and which he will never notice as thoughtful. My hand is holding it like it's defusing a bomb. I watch him take it and I perform a full audit: Am I smiling? I think I'm doing a thirty-percent smile, which reads as either serene or digestive discomfort. I adjust to zero. Now I look like the cabbage juice personally offended me. I go back to thirty. There are two ways to fail this: desperate and weird. Desperate is laughing too hard at things that aren't jokes. Weird is what I'm doing now, which is apparently staring at a beaker like it owes me money. There is no setting in between. I am a poorly calibrated instrument. They should send me back to the manufacturer. "Fifteen more minutes," Ms. Alvarez says, and my stomach does the thing. Fifteen minutes. I have rehearsed eleven openings and deployed one "yep." At this rate I'll speak to Jonah for the first time at graduation, and it'll be "yep," and he'll say "what," and I'll be carried out. The vinegar test turns the cabbage juice pink, and Jonah goes, "Whoa, okay," under his breath, and here's my chance—here is a handed-to-me, gift-wrapped opening—and my brain instantly produces the perfect line: *Chemistry: occasionally not terrible.* Casual. Dry. Slightly funny. Requires no response. It's the best thing I've ever written and I've written it in half a second, because my brain can do anything except transmit. Say it. Say it say it say— "Chemistry's not terrible sometimes," I say, and the words come out in the wrong order, wearing each other's coats. Jonah laughs. An actual laugh, not a pity one—I know the difference, I have a whole filing system—and he says, "It smells like a hospital in here and we're making a rainbow. Weird day." He noticed the smell. He said *we*. My brain begins the post-mortem before he's even finished the sentence: did my laugh back sound real, was my laugh a bark, I think my laugh was a bark. But also—he talked to me. Twice. Unprompted. That's data. I record observations. The bell rings. We clean up in silence, but it's a different silence now—not load-bearing, just regular quiet, two people rinsing beakers while ammonia stings our eyes. He takes the graduated cylinder from my side of the table without asking, like we're a system. Like we've established procedure. "See you Thursday," he says. "Yeah," I say, and then, because the universe owed me one: "Same rainbow time, same rainbow place." He grins. He *grins*. I hold the face exactly the way it is—thirty percent, maybe forty—until he turns away, and then I let myself have four whole seconds of not analyzing anything. Four seconds is all I get. My brain is already drafting Thursday. It has opened a new tab. It has opened twelve.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and authentic voice work. The author successfully captures the hyperactive, analytical mind of an anxious 8th-grader through rapid-fire internal monologue that feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending. The voice is consistent throughout, maintaining the character's intelligent but socially paralyzed perspective. The writing excels in several key areas. The metaphors are fresh and specific to the character - "my brain has just opened seventeen tabs at once," "the silence has calcified into something structural. Load-bearing." These feel natural to how a smart teenager might conceptualize her experience. The scientific setting is well-integrated, with chemistry terminology woven organically into both the literal action and the character's thought patterns. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements, delivering almost entirely internal monologue while showing the gap between the protagonist's articulate inner voice and her stilted external expression. The exhausting nature of social anxiety is well-rendered through the constant rehearsing, analyzing, and second-guessing. However, there are some weaknesses. The ending feels slightly too positive given the established anxiety patterns - while not entirely unearned, the successful joke exchange represents a fairly significant breakthrough that happens quite quickly. The piece occasionally borders on being overly clever with its metaphors, though it generally stays on the right side of that line. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose effectively. Jonah's responses feel authentic to a teenage boy, and Maya's external speech convincingly contrasts with her internal eloquence. The pacing works well, building tension through the extended internal rehearsals before the brief actual exchanges. The emotional complexity is strong - the piece captures not just anxiety but the specific frustration of being trapped inside one's own overthinking mind. The character feels fully realized rather than simply a collection of anxiety symptoms. Some minor issues include occasional moments where the voice feels slightly too sophisticated for 8th grade, and a few instances where the metaphors pile up in ways that feel more writerly than character-driven. The piece also could have pushed deeper into the specific fears and social dynamics that fuel this particular crush. + First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 90.0
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Rehearsing Rejection The shower's been off for twenty minutes and I'm still standing in the steam, rehearsing. *I just think we've been moving too fast,* she'll say, stirring her coffee, not looking at me. *The friend thing worked. This—* a gesture between us, small, surgical—*this was a mistake.* And I'll say—no. I'll say nothing. I'll nod like it's fine, like my chest isn't collapsing, and then I'll get home and compose the text that ends it first, because if I end it first it doesn't count as being left. That's math I've done a hundred times. Or maybe I say: *You've been planning this all week, haven't you? The short replies. The "sorry, swamped." You were softening the landing.* And she'll flinch, and the flinch will mean I'm right, and I'll feel sick and triumphant at once, which is a feeling I know like my own pulse. I pull on the green shirt. Then the blue one, because green is the one she—no. Green is trying. Blue says I didn't think about this at all. Blue is armor. In the hallway mirror: a person folded slightly forward, shoulders up around their ears, thumb shredding the skin beside a fingernail. I straighten, practice a face—breezy, unbothered—and hold it until it slides off. I look away before I can see it slide. Phone. Her text from this morning: *can't wait to see you!!* Two exclamation points. Yesterday there were none. I've already decided the exclamation points are overcompensation. She feels guilty. You don't need two exclamation points for someone you're not leaving. There's a part of me—the part that reads the worksheets—watching from the corner going: *you're doing the thing. This is the thing. She said she can't wait to see you.* And I know. I know it's the thing. But the rehearsals feel like preparation and preparation feels like safety, and I have survived whole relationships on the oxygen of being right about how they'd end. *I don't know how to say this,* imagined-her begins, and I interrupt: *Then don't. I already know.* God, I love that version of me. He's devastating. He doesn't exist. On the train I run the other scenario, the one I trust less: she's happy to see me. She hugs me and means it. Nothing is wrong. And somehow that's worse, because if nothing is wrong then I've spent nine days—nine days, I've counted—clawed open over slow replies that were just... replies. If nothing is wrong, then the wrong is me, and I'm the thing she'll eventually leave. Nine years her friend. Three weeks her lover. I've already grieved both. By the time the train slows I've resolved to be a little cold. Not enough to start anything. Just enough that when she leaves, she'll have felt one percent of it. Punishment in advance—a down payment on a wrong that hasn't happened. She's at the corner table. She sees me through the window and her whole face does the thing—unguarded, lit—and she half-stands as I come in and pulls me into her, hard, one hand flat between my shoulder blades. "I missed you," she says into my shoulder. "I've been such a ghost. I'm sorry." And I feel it. I do. It lands. For three full seconds it's like warm water through a frozen pipe, something in me unclenching so fast it hurts, and I think: *oh, it's okay, it was always okay*— —and then, before I've even let go of her: *she rehearsed that. She could hear how needy my last text was. This is a managed descent.* "Hey," she says, pulling back, hands still on my arms. "You okay?" "Yeah. Tired." There's the opening. I could ask. *Are we okay? Did the other night change things? Do you regret—* But if I ask and she says no, we're fine, I won't believe her, and if I don't ask I get to keep the evidence that she never said it. Unprompted love counts. Prompted love is just customer service. She slides a coffee across the table. "Oat milk, extra shot, the gross amount of vanilla. I've been holding your order hostage for two weeks. I missed doing it." Two weeks. She's been counting too— *Because she knew it was ending. Nostalgia is what you do at the end.* Shut up, I tell the voice. It doesn't. She talks—work swallowed her, her sister's thing, the project from hell—and it's all so reasonable, so gently, boringly *reasonable*, and I hate it. I had loaded speeches for a betrayal and she's handing me logistics. Part of me is actually disappointed, and I clock that, I see how insane it is, that some organ in me would rather be right and wrecked than wrong and fine. The worksheet voice says: *you scan for threat so hard you'd manufacture it.* I tell the worksheet voice to sit down; I'm busy. She laughed at my joke half a second late. File it. Then she reaches across and takes my hand, both of hers around it, and says, "Hey. I know I went quiet. I get in my head and I disappear and I'm working on it. But you—" she squeezes—"you're not a thing I disappear *from*. Okay? You're the thing I come back to." And there it is, the exact sentence, the one I've been begging the universe for since Tuesday, handed to me warm, and I feel myself flooding—eyes hot, throat tight, nine days of static going quiet all at once, loved, *loved*, I am so loved— *She's a good actor,* says the other voice, already, instantly, flipping the script. *Or she means it today. People mean things today.* I squeeze her hand back. "Okay," I say. "Same." And I mean it so much I could shake, and I don't believe a word either of us has said, and both of these are completely true. She smiles and starts a story about her coworker, and I nod, and laugh, and under the table my thumb finds the raw spot by my nail—because there was a pause, just now, before "the thing I come back to," half a breath, and I am already rebuilding the whole collapsing cathedral around it. I know. I know I'm doing it. The rehearsal starts anyway.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional psychological authenticity in portraying the internal experience of someone with borderline personality disorder. The writer masterfully captures the exhausting cycle of catastrophic thinking, preemptive defensiveness, and the inability to accept love even when it's genuinely offered. The narrative structure is sophisticated, weaving between imagined scenarios and reality while maintaining clear distinctions between the two. The prose is precise and evocative without being overwrought. Phrases like "I have survived whole relationships on the oxygen of being right about how they'd end" and "like warm water through a frozen pipe" are both beautiful and psychologically accurate. The voice remains consistently authentic throughout - anxious, self-aware yet trapped, intelligent but emotionally dysregulated. The characterization is nuanced and complex. The protagonist displays the hallmark BPD traits of abandonment fears, splitting, and emotional dysregulation, but they're rendered as a fully human character rather than a collection of symptoms. The friend/lover is also well-drawn through limited interactions, coming across as genuinely caring and patient. The piece excels at showing rather than telling. We understand the protagonist's condition through their thought patterns, physical behaviors (thumb shredding skin, shoulders hunched), and interpretations of events rather than through clinical exposition. The cognitive dissonance is brilliantly rendered - the simultaneous experience of feeling loved and immediately questioning that love. The dialogue, though limited, feels natural and serves the story well. The friend's words ring true as someone trying to reassure an anxious partner while the protagonist's sparse responses reflect their emotional guardedness. The ending is particularly strong - there's no false resolution or unearned transformation. The protagonist recognizes their pattern but remains trapped in it, which is psychologically realistic. The final lines capture the exhausting nature of this mental state perfectly. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements, delivering the requested internal monologue structure, the contrast between imagined and real interactions, and the specific psychological dynamics requested. At approximately 1000 words, it meets the length requirement while maintaining tight focus throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
